QB Fernando Mendoza.elevator_pitch:
Smart, aggressive, and an accurate downfield creator who can maneuver a pocket, Mendoza is one of the best 
quarterbacks I’ve scouted during the past 5-7 years. Mendoza and Caleb Williams have nearly identical grades although their playing styles, 
strengths, and weaknesses are different. 
Williams is an aspiring Aaron Rodgers in Rodgers’ prime. Mendoza is a mashup of Andrew Luck and Justin Herbert. 
For the casual football fan who follows a few college teams or reads college football previews during the summer, Mendoza seemingly came 
out of nowhere like his Indiana team to become one of the hottest commodities in the game. It gives Mendoza the vibe of a one-year 
wonder. 
Joe Burrow had this exact vibe from the public at LSU. Both players started with different teams the year before, but both teams lost 
enough games to avoid the limelight. Burrow lost three games at Ohio State, which is below expectations for one of the richest teams in 
talent and expectations in college football. 
Mendoza lost six games starting for the far less talented Cal Bears. Even so, all six losses were by a total margin of 17 points – five of those 
losses were by a margin of 9 points. I studied Mendoza at Cal and Indiana, and there’s no mistaking him for a one-year wonder. 
In addition to his ability to execute a passing game at a high level, Mendoza is an underrated runner who can move the chains with his 
acceleration, and he has enough speed to generate big plays. He’s not a breakaway runner, but he’s going to have runs where he’s gaining 
more than 5-15 yards. His burst and power through contact is underrated. 
Mendoza is a technically and conceptually sound quarterback who can play from center, Pistol, or shotgun. His drop game is advanced, and 
he has a full complement of play fakes. His range of accuracy and with good velocity is 55-60 yards. He can drive the deep post 56 yards 
from his release point – even with pressure bearing down. 
Mendoza is patient with unblocked edge pressure as he rolls toward it. He relies on good footwork to earn the separation and a passing 
lane. 
Mendoza is willing to take a hit to throw the ball downfield. He’s mobile enough to roll to the opposite side of a compressed pocket (but no 
pressure) after cycling through three covered reads – downfield, intermediate, and check-down – and finds a one-on-one to target – 
drawing penalties in the process. 
When unblocked edge pressure is heading where Mendoza is rolling, Mendoza decelerates well enough to create inches of separation that’s 
necessary for him to climb past and deliver a vertical-range accuracy that’s catchable at 28-30 yards to the boundary. 
He climbs quickly from bookend pressure and when there’s more pressure as he resets to fire, Mendoza is quick enough to flush and then 
break the pocket for a significant gain. He has climbed and rolled from pressure, reset, and delivered with deep accuracy. Climbing is 
Mendoza’s default reaction to pressure, which is an excellent trait even if he finds himself retreating after his first climb step due to 
secondary pressure in his path. 
Mendoza manipulates coverage with pump fakes, shoulder fakes, opening his body to one side of the field, and with his eyes. Mendoza will 
even take the extra step to manipulate the defense when considering a check-down. He’ll look at the shallow route, come of it to look 
deeper to hold the flat defender, and then target the shallow receiver. 
You’ll see Mendoza take unnecessary risks with situational game management or underestimate coverage like the rest of the prospects. 
What sets Mendoza apart from most is his ability to continue playing aggressive football while learning from the mistake. 
Some of this aggression is due to his offense forcing the issue. A lot of Mendoza’s bad decisions and sacks come on plays with long-
developing routes and no check-downs where he’s forced to buy time until the shortest route breaks open. You hope that with time and the 
right offense, Mendoza will develop the skill to adjust pre-snap and have built-in hots. 
I expect he will. As was the case with Williams, Mendoza is headed to a bad team. Don’t expect a rookie season like Justin Herbert’s. 
Mendoza will likely have a mistake-filled rookie year on a team with a bad offensive line and limited or still-developing skill talent. 
He’s good enough to survive the situation and the media onslaught that will question by Week 10 whether Mendoza even was good enough 
to be born onto this earth. As long as Tom Brady makes good enough decisions as part of the Raiders to build a good team around 
Mendoza, we should see him develop into a top-10 producer at the position with even better numbers if the front office improves the 
surrounding talent. 
Drops: Mendoza performs 1- , 2-, 3-, 5- and 7-step drops from shotgun and pistol. He’ll also set up without a drop, execute a half-roll and 
then reset and fire. He embeds a partial roll away from the pressure with his three-step drop. He does this when he spots blitzes or when he 
has a pass rusher facing mismatch and he wants to embed more time from the potential pressure. 
47
QB Fernando Mendoza.pre_draft_advice:
Mendoza is a top five selection in this draft, regardless of the format. He’s arguably the top selection in a 
Super Flex format. Like Caleb Williams, Mendoza can handle a bad organization for the first 1-2 years, muddle through it, and still grow 
from the experience.

QB Ty Simpson.elevator_pitch:
Simpson has as much potential to become a good NFL starter as any quarterback from the 2025 class. He would have 
edged Cam Ward for the No. 1 spot if Simpson had this grade last year and declared for the draft. Simpson would have been QB3 for the 
2024 RSP, just a sliver ahead of Bo Nix. 
Simpson has a good arm, he maneuvers pressure well, and he follows up with strong accuracy in the short, intermediate, and often in the 
vertical ranges of the field after avoiding defenders. Other than some leaning through his weight transfer during releases that might impact 
his post routes, Simpson has clean enough mechanics to start in the NFL and refine them as he goes. 
Simpson’s arm strength is good enough to make all the throws one would reasonably expect from a starting quarterback. It’s not a plus-
arm, but it’s better than average. He can drive the ball 50-55 yards on the deep post with pinpoint accuracy. Beyond that, the accuracy and 
velocity become erratic. 
Simpson is a capable scrambler who can break the pocket and earn chain-moving gains. He’s agile enough to extend some of these gains 
for big plays. He’s not a breakaway threat, so we’re talking about 15-25 yards. 
Then NFL will likely regard Simpson as a first-year starter and co-starring with Fernando Mendoza on the draft-day billing. This reminds me 
of the 2009 NFL Draft when Mark Sanchez was the co-star with Matthew Stafford. 
Just as I thought Stafford was the clear No. 1 option in that QB class, there’s a healthy gap separating Mendoza from Simpson for me. The 
differentiating factors are Simpson’s issues with reading/processing coverage and situational issues with his pocket clock and reactions to 
pressure. 
51
QB Ty Simpson.pre_draft_advice:
Simpson is a second-round value in one-QB drafts and a mid-to-late first-round option in Super Flex. If 
Simpson goes in the NFL Draft before Mendoza or in the same range, he’ll be overpriced.

QB Garrett Nussmeier.elevator_pitch:
Nussmeier is the volatile boom-bust talent in this quarterback class. He’s mobile and has the arm talent to deliver targets 
with velocity that belies his height and weight. 
Nussmeier has an aggressive, play-making mindset. This gets him into trouble, but he plays through the mistakes and doesn’t hesitate to 
attack at the next available opportunity. 
The son of former NFL, AFL, and CFL quarterback Doug Nussmeier, who is a veteran offensive coach in the NFL, Garrett has some 
inconsistencies with drop plans that impact his timing and accuracy. There are claims that Brian Kelly is to blame for this and other woes 
from 2025. 
Kelly has a reputation for pressuring players to play when they are injured and that he makes Jon Voight’s character from Friday Night 
Lights look like Pete Carroll. There’s talk that Kelly cajoled Nussmeier into play with injuries that impacted his play and scape-goated the 
quarterback. 
If true, this explains some of the erratic play from Nussmeier. Even so, there are constants that span multiple seasons and much of what I 
studied was from 2024. 
Nussmeier can underestimate the periphery defender on targets outside the numbers and get too aggressive. He must at least manipulate 
these defenders. This isn’t a bad habit everywhere. Nussmeier has moments that are very good in the middle of the field, accounting for 
that defender with placement that protects the target and the receiver. 
The place where Nussmeier has the most lapses is with downfield throws of 20 yards and greater, and the target is outside the numbers, or 
the defender is playing 10 yards under the target in the passing lane. 
Nussmeier capable of varying and combining stride lengths, pacing, and maneuvers to create space in traffic so he can throw an accurate 
target. The problem is that he creates more problems with his release and placement that he’s forced to overcome. This is due to the lack of 
uniformity of his drops and setups from play to play. 
Talented, but erratic, Nussmeier has good accuracy and pocket skills for a player whose feet aren’t giving him the reliable foundation for 
him to process and execute cleanly at the highest level. If he can clean up the leaks that spring from his game, he could become a starter. 
Drops: Nussmeier has a three-step drop, but he has a short stutter as the first two steps that isn’t fluid and could impact the consistency 
with his timing because the three-step pattern is different each rep and doesn’t need to be. One is as described above, the next is a short 
step preceding two large hitches. After that, he uses crossover steps for a three-step drop that appear more uniform. 
This could be an indication that Nussmeier is in an offense where his drop plan isn’t linked tight enough to the routes. At least this was the 
case in 2024. 
His one- and two-step drops on short passes are uniform. His set up out of a read fake can be wider than optimal. 
Nussmeier has a five-step drop that’s uniform and he adjusts the path to roll right at the top of the drop when interior pressure breaks 
containment. 
He delivers a well-paced and defined seven-step drop from center with a play-action fake mid-drop. 
Nussmeier displays all the drops but throughout 2024, his footwork wasn’t uniform from one drop to the next of the same type. The step 
width often had some irregularity that hindered a smooth and uniform process. This impacted the width and uniformity of his setups. 
His feet are often too wide when he sets up and this leads to throws that should be pinpoint, but are either catchable with an awkward 
adjustment or inaccurate. 
Nussmeier has good accuracy and pocket skills for a player whose feet aren’t giving him the reliable foundation for him to process and 
execute cleanly at the highest level. 
Ball Security: Nussmeier keeps two hands on the ball in the pocket and when he moves within controlled footwork in the pocket. When he 
breaks the pocket, he initially carries the ball loose and wide from his frame. When he passes the line of scrimmage, he realizes he should 
be protecting the ball, tucking it close to his chest but still loose from his elbow. He only uses his right arm. 
Nussmeier has lost the ball while maneuvering away from pressure in the pocket. The defender strikes the ball thanks to Nussmeier’s loose 
security. This has happened in multiple games and in the black zone multiple times. 
Nussmeier flips the ball unnecessarily while holding it in the pocket and surveying the field. 
57
QB Garrett Nussmeier.pre_draft_advice:
A mid-round value in rookie drafts whose value might increase if an NFL team falls in love with the pre-
injury version of Nussmeier from 2024 and takes the risk earlier. If you need a quarterback, Nussmeier is more likely a patience play, so 
don’t get too aggressive with him as your target.

QB Cade Klubnik.elevator_pitch:
Like Drew Allar, Klubnik was a nationally acclaimed high school prospect. Unlike Allar, Klubnik’s game took a promising 
statistical leap forward in 2024, and the NFL Draft media touted Klubnik last spring as a potential first-round pick, if not the top prospect at 
his position. Klubnik didn’t sustain that production in 2025 and led to a precipitous drop in the perceived NFL Draft stock. 
Allar is the Robo-QB who hasn’t developed the craft of the position necessary to warrant an early-round pick. Klubnik is the inverse of Allar. 
He lacks prototypical size, arm strength, and athletic ability. These traits are all good enough to make an NFL team, but these are some of 
the questions teams will raise about Klubnik’s potential as a starter. 
Another concern I’ve heard from those in the scouting game is that Clemson’s offense and coaching regime doesn’t advance the conceptual 
and technical skills of its passers while they are with the team. College football is big business, and development is a low priority with many 
big-time coaches. Winning is their primary job and there’s a lot of pressure to do so. 
While this observation shared with me sounds like logo scouting, and my source admitted as much, one can make the subtle distinction it is 
not. These players all worked in a similar offense in the Dabo Sweeney regime. Logo scouting would be saying Oklahoma State has a history 
of great running backs but grouping Barry Sanders and Thurman Thomas with Chuba Hubbard and Ollie Gordon. 
When examining Klubnik’s game, accuracy and velocity are the chief concerns. Klubnik has a habit of sailing passes in the intermediate 
zones, especially in the middle of the field. 
There are two potential reasons this is happening. Klubnik begins his release with a wide stance out of a three-step drop. His feet are wider 
than shoulder width. This contributes to targets that sail high in the short and intermediate ranges of the field. 
Another potential reason for his targets sailing is what happens when Klubnik rotates his front foot during the release. Klubnik pushes down 
on the toe of his front foot. This creates elevation upward as he follows through. This may contribute to the ball sailing on downfield routes 
in the middle of the field, and it was problematic throughout 2025 – most often early in the season. 
Klubnik must generate more velocity with far-sideline throws on intermediate outs and in the vertical game. Klubnik’s late-outs require 
precise footwork to generate velocity, and while he has shown he can generate the velocity, he must do it with greater frequency. 
Klubnik has lapses with velocity where the ball is catchable, but the lack of zip forces the receiver to slow his pace and turn back inside with 
his break. This opens the passing lane for the cornerback to play a play on the ball. 
The middle of the field and timing routes to the intermediate and vertical boundary are areas where NFL starting quarterbacks must thrive. 
Klubnik’s game raises questions whether it can. 
If Klubnik corrects these facets of his game, there’s a lot to like – enough to like him as a future starter. His drops are efficient and have 
some advanced skills that could help him work well from center. 
Klubnik’s lower body needs tweaking during his release, but his stroke is efficient and he can throw off platform. 
Klubnik exhausts three options and makes correct reads of leverage that leads to him holding the ball, resetting his position in the pocket, 
and finding an open receiver off script. When the sailing targets aren’t a problem, Klubnik has demonstrated good placement in the middle 
of the field because he accounts for underneath coverage that is potentially in position to cut off the target. 
Klubnik also displays good anticipation under pressure in the pocket because he sees the leverage of defenders against zone routes that will 
break open. He’ll split defenders with low placement so the receiver is the only option who can earn the ball in the shallow flat. 
Klubnik patient with the zone triangle – even after pivoting from a different quadrant of the field. He’ll allow his leak route to influence the 
triangle so he can make a tight window throw a viable target with good placement against the defender’s leverage. 
When breaking the pocket on the move, Klubnik is aware of backside receivers and can find the wide-open option across the field with no 
defender around. He’ll also throw the ball away when routes are covered. 
Klubnik doesn’t just play it safe, he makes layered throws over a dropping linebacker into the vertical ranges of the field. He even does this 
while side-stepping to his left from pressure. 
Klubnik hangs in compressed pocket for three reads, not panicking to escape as he pivots to the opposite side of the field to access that 
third read. 
These are promising field-reading skills paired with moments of advanced pocket management. The league covets players who can do this 
type of work. 
Klubnik has lapses and holes when reading the field. It’s rare, but Klubnik has had moments where he doesn’t account for the leverage of a 
defender on an intermediate out and tests the defender squatting on the route. 
62
QB Cade Klubnik.pre_draft_advice:
Klubnik is worth a late-round pick in Super Flex formats or one-QB leagues with large rosters and practice 
squads. He’s a luxury for most rosters as patience play, so most of you will monitor from afar.

QB Diego Pavia.elevator_pitch:
Diego Pavia is another Frankenstein QB experiment. The deluded doctor began with Baker Mayfield’s attitude from his 
college years. He then added the resourcefulness and smarts of Doug Flutie while failing to read the side effects of diminished arm strength. 
He tried to compensate for these Flutie side effects with doses of Ben Roethlisberger and Cam Newton’s toughness and aggression, but 
didn’t read the warning labels there, either. 
He slipped this concoction into an infant’s formula and decades later, we have Diego Pavia, easily one of the most entertaining quarterbacks 
to watch in college football this year. 
Pavia is one of my favorite prospects if you’re scouting for players Al Davis would have had on his squad in 1973. If not, you’ll want to drop 
Pavia to a tier where he’s a career backup who can start as needed. 
The arm talent is one of the few things that isn’t quite there with Pavia. The other is acceptable behavior for quarterback, the figurehead of 
the team. 
Pavia urinated on a rival team’s logo during his college career. He argued with an Alabama fan in public. He also made crass comments on 
social media about Heisman voters. When asked about his past behavior at the NFL Combine, Pavia downplayed it. 
From connected people I speak with, there’s more that will come to light about Pavia. As always, I caution people about character concerns 
because a lot of NFL personnel employees may be assigned to collect information about players off the field – and it’s a big part of their job 
these days. According to one of the most qualified individuals that I know who has been in a lot of these rooms at various levels, few NFL 
employees possess the expertise to accurately analyze the information. 
This individual notes that the best teams use qualified experts with education and training to analyze behavior and let them make 
recommendations to the team’s leadership. Unfortunately, many teams allow the information collectors and untrained executives to make 
judgments with the data and they’re often incorrect about their conclusions. 
My recommendation, sit with the uncomfortable limbo of not knowing for sure what type of person Pavia is: A bad guy, immature but is 
learning fast enough from his mistakes to be worth the risk, or like most people, a complex person who behaved poorly but is better than 
these moments indicate. We’ll likely learn more soon enough. 
On the field, Pavia’s game is appealing from the standpoint of toughness, intelligence, and creative resourcefulness. Pavia played in 2024 
with a torn hamstring. 
Based on how he takes on defenders, you wouldn’t have guessed it. For a small quarterback, Pavia finishes like a fullback. That’s not a good 
idea in the NFL, but you love the competitive attitude and selflessness for his team 
Small passers must be skilled at maneuvering the pocket and not just running with the football. Pavia has a good pocket clock. He’ll exhaust 
his reads and find the second available window of a zone route, demonstrating patience and awareness of which route to return to. Pavia 
will stand in the pocket and deliver with interior pressure bearing down if he has an open target available. 
Pavia senses backside pressure well and accelerates his process as needed. He knows when he must begin his moves based on the location 
of the blitzing defender. Pavia will wait until a defender is 3-4 steps away to begin his spin and roll. He’ll also wait until the defender is 1-2 
steps away from a climb or flush. This is good awareness in-time and processing skills that a quarterback can talk about in a whiteboard 
session but until you see them on the field, you have no idea whether they can execute. 
Many shorter quarterbacks display an unwillingness to climb the pocket in the NFL and it short-circuits their development. Pavia can 
combine a climb and a flush to work past two points of pressure. He spots a second-level pursuit mid-climb and can adjust back to the 
inside while factoring the leverage of a lineman on a blocker. This helps him buy time to throw downfield. 
Pavia sidesteps edge pressure when it gets high enough in the pocket, but before it breaks containment. This is a good way of preemptively 
buying time. 
Pavia’s good processor isn’t just in the pocket. He’s aware of coverage on the periphery of the target and places the ball away from the 
defenders threaten the ball. 
Pavia also displays rare judgment for a young quarterback. Every team runs schemed plays where there’s essentially one target and the 
offense is executing misdirection to get this one receiver open. If the receiver doesn’t get open, the play usually fails. 
Young quarterbacks often compound failure, because they force the ball into the receiver and ignore the defensive tells that should have 
prompted them to run or throw the ball away. Pavia has shown that he’ll pull the ball down, scramble, and/or throw the ball away. I rarely 
see young pros do this and Pavia is doing it. 
68
QB Diego Pavia.pre_draft_advice:
If you’re in a Super Flex format with large rosters, consider Pavia at the end of the draft as a stash with 
low risk and medium-high reward. In one-quarterback leagues, Pavia is a late-round pick if you have practice squad spots and a large 
roster. 
Otherwise, Pavia is at best, a player to stash as a free agent for expanded offseason rosters if his draft capital is better than expected, 
and/or he has a good fit on a weak depth chart.

QB Taylen Green.elevator_pitch:
If you’re not going to follow the RSP’s strategic approach to rookie quarterbacks as a fantasy GM or you’re in NFL personnel 
reading this material as a cross check of information, Green and Carson Beck – QB6 and QB7 on the RSP’s board – are realistically closer to 
QB4 and QB5 when accounting for factors other than on-field performance. 
The NFL evaluation community hasn’t been shy about leaking Diego Pavia’s exploits. Pavia is central casting’s pick to reprise Shane Falco 
with a bad-boy edge in “The Replacements.” If that doesn’t pan out, Pavia could be the QB of the dirty rival team in a Will Ferrell football 
sendup in a similar vein of “Talladega Nights.” 
Pavia has pissed away, literally, the NFL’s consideration of him as anything more than a late-round pick. It’s more than just the incident 
linked to here, but this incident is allegedly indicative of what they fear about Pavia. 
There are questions about Cade Klubnik’s velocity and Dabo Sweeney’s Clemson offenses being too basic. These factors will also limit 
Klubnik’s draft capital. 
It means Green and Beck will be included among the first or second tier of quarterbacks that the NFL will consider in April. Beck, as you’ll 
read, will likely earn value for what he already has to offer while hoping the immediate experience will season his game. 
Green’s value is a long-term bet on high-ceiling physical skills as he develops and/or refines the techniques and concepts of the position. 
Any quarterback that scores at least a 75 on the RSP’s Depth of Talent Criteria has the skills to develop into a starting quarterback. 
Green operates well from Pistol because the alignment supports a stronger ground game than shotgun while also leveraging a quarterback’s 
rushing ability from spread concepts. Green already possesses a sound play-action game with thorough and detailed fakes. 
These fakes can slow the defense and allow Green to assess whether he keeps the ball. When he does, he’s a dynamic runner. 
Green drops his pads into contact, bounces off glancing shots to his frame and keeps his feet moving to extend forward after the contact. 
Green beats unblocked defensive ends and middle linebackers around the far-side corner on a keeper. He has also shown that he can slide, 
but he should do it more often. 
Green can transition downhill with one step or use tight curvilinear bend around the short-side end. He hurdles defenders when he can earn 
that final meaningful bit of space to cross the marker or pylon. 
Green also manipulates pursuit with good footwork. His burst and speed help him turn the corner on perimeter runs, split defenders up the 
middle, and extend the runs into open space. He can break away from pursuit for field-flipping runs as well as full-field gallops of more than 
60 yards. 
We’ve seen this combination of play-action and running work as a basis for an offense when Jalen Hurts, Lamar Jackson, and Colin 
Kaepernick were the quarterbacks. Green must develop and refine the rest of his game to emerge as a starter. 
Green’s drops need refinement. He reaches a balanced stance, but there’s a lot of wasted movement that disrupts efficient flow from the 
snap to the setup as he’s trying to read the field. 
He’s off to a good start with his release mechanics, but he has lapses where he doesn’t point his front toe to the target and the ball travels 
closer to where his toe is facing. His arm motion is compact and he gets weight transfer, so velocity, balance, and stroke are all good 
enough as starting points for additional refinement as his QB coach will deem necessary. 
There is no question Green has arm talent. He also has moments of strong accuracy at NFL distances. There are strong examples of Green 
layering the ball over defenders with pinpoint accuracy in the intermediate and vertical ranges of the field. 
Green holds flat defenders and safeties in various areas of the field depending on the coverage. Green can cycle though a few reads against 
coverages like C3 and C2 and discern what’s covered before going off-script and finding an open option. He targets receivers in sync with 
the progression and route. 
If you’re an optimist, you can characterize Green as being on his way to developing more advanced quarterback skills that will serve him as 
a future NFL starter. Much of what’s left to fix are common ills with prospects: 
Predetermining check-downs when greater awareness and patience would have led to a better decision. 
Failing to account for coverage on the periphery of the targeted receiver. 
• 
• 
• Greater selectivity with targets based on his throwing stance, pressure, and field position. 
• 
• 
• 
• 
Poor judgment with the leverage safeties are playing over the receiver. 
Poor judgment with the leverage corners are playing while trailing the receiver. 
Taking unnecessary risks with low-odds efforts when in the black zone. 
Checking down more often. 
74
QB Taylen Green.pre_draft_advice:
If you’re in a Super Flex format or league with big rosters, Green is a second-tier QB after Mendoza who is 
worth stashing on a practice squad for the potential upside.

QB Carson Beck.elevator_pitch:
The NFL will covet Beck more than the initial press he earned in 2025 after transferring from Georgia to Miami. A top 
prospect heading into 2024, Beck had what the media has characterized as an up-and-down career at Georgia. 
Despite a respectable overall statistical profile in 2024, Beck didn’t follow up an even stronger 2023 campaign with similar highs in 
completion percentage and yardage. Beck also doubled his interception count from 6 to 12. 
Beck ended his career at Georgia after tearing a ligament in his throwing elbow before the college playoffs. Beck rebounded at Miami and 
either came close to matching or exceeding most box score figures media values and led the Hurricanes to the college football championship 
game. 
Still, the narrative makers cite that Beck was a fifth-year passer, and his strong statistical performance last year was a baseline requirement 
instead of an impressive layer to his evaluation as a pro prospect. The NFL will value these bullet points, especially team executives who 
subsist on them, especially when they’re infused with risk management data points that will cover their asses – I mean assets. 
Prototypical physical dimensions 
Top college programs that are playing winning football 
Arm strength 
• 
• 
• 
• Good box score statistics 
• Good NFL Combine and Pro Day performances with scripted football ballet vs air 
• 
Paid for interview training that comes with the correction dog collar and remote 
• 
Reasonable track record of health 
The more of these data points a GM, VP, and President can check off, the less risk they feel about taking the player in the earliest rounds of 
the NFL Draft. If things go wrong with the player’s development, they can at least point to the list of things about the player that were low 
risk. They can then point the finger elsewhere. 
Beck’s career checks most of these boxes on the list. There’s still a pervasive belief that because you can’t teach size, athletic ability, and 
arm talent at the levels they covet them, so you draft these Robo QBs. Then, you can teach them how to play the quarterback position at 
an advanced level. 
Except there’s a flaw to this mindset. There’s little to no teaching. Talk of coaches developing players is mostly lip service. 
The same scene plays out at most NFL organizations every year. A franchise considers drafting an early-round rookie quarterback: Owners 
and executives ask coaches if they can develop the quarterback. The coaches reflexively say, “yes,” because they know the question is the 
executives’ coded way of saying, “We want to draft him and you’re going to be the scapegoat if the player doesn’t develop.” 
The coach’s affirmative response of “Yes,” the coach’s version of only showing the tip of the iceberg. If you dive below the water’s surface, 
you’d see a longer response vocalized in the coach’s head. 
“Yes, I want to keep my job right now so I’m going to agree despite knowing we won’t have the time to do much with him. I’m just saying 
yes to end this painful meeting. 
I’ve known all along that I’m your scapegoat for just about everything that doesn’t work on the field. I’ll likely blame the player when he 
can’t figure out the game at this level. 
Not that it usually works, but I’ve seen that it can buy me a year while I make plans for my golden parachute elsewhere. I can live with 
your entrepreneurial-nepotistic-corporate cocktail of b.s. – like coming down to the sideline in the second half of games as if your presence 
is going to suddenly click all 316 team processes into place. Just please, stop sending me plays to run on cocktail napkins at halftime like 
you’re a hybrid of Ceasar and Madden.” 
Odds are likely this is the environment Beck will be headed to if he’s a first-round selection. Whether Beck earns a first-round pick depends 
on how much franchises are being real with themselves, or they’re leaning too hard the historical economics of position scarcity instead of 
working harder on figuring out what makes a good QB and what they can do to be a part of that process. 
As with most of these prospects, Beck needs time to refine his game. He has enough skills for decision-makers with personnel convince 
themselves he’s better than he is right now. 
Beck plays played in system with pro concepts and he’s well-versed in the drop-back and play-action game. Whether you want to use Beck 
under center, shotgun, or Pistol, he has done it and shown competent work with all the bells and whistles that come with misdirection out of 
these alignments. 
79
QB Carson Beck.pre_draft_advice:
Beck’s fantasy value could increase if the NFL gives him first-round draft capital. Based on what we’ve 
seen play out with quarterbacks for decades, it’s better to target Beck in a few years via trade or as a free agent than spend capital on him. 
Target receivers.

QB Cole Payton.elevator_pitch:
Payton is an enticing athlete who possesses good speed and strong acceleration. He hurdles defenders in the open field, 
and he has the size to work through contact. 
He’ll draw attention from the Robo-QB niche of NFL executives who inadvertently give double credit to athletic ability. There’s a lot of work 
ahead. 
Payton’s drops and releases lack precision and efficiency. His ball security exposes the team to turnovers. 
Payton identifies pressure pre-snap and finds quick solutions, but he has lapses where he overreacts to pressure and climbs pockets when 
unnecessary. He places himself in harm’s way when this happens. Otherwise, he has a good pocket clock. 
Payton retreats from interior pressure and then doubts his decision mid-escape. He has plays where he stops a rollout as if he might reverse 
field or square and throw, but he then continues a full roll to the far-side boundary. Hopefully, Payton doesn’t try this against NFL defenders 
with better quickness and angles of pursuit. 
Payton must become more selective with the maneuvers he chooses for the pressure that arrives. He chooses maneuvers that lead him into 
pressure he could have otherwise avoided. 
Payton cycles through 2-3 reads and breaks the pocket if he doesn’t target a receiver. He understand how to use his legs to reshape an 
area of zone that’s dense with coverage, stretching the window, and making the against-the-grain throw to the open receiver. He’ll also 
check the ball after exhausting downfield options. 
Payton is willing to get aggressive when targeting a receiver between two defenders, placing the ball away from the defenders. He also 
places the ball away from dropping linebackers, throwing the receiver open with pinpoint placement to adjust away from the adjacent 
coverage. 
Payton has more difficulty IDing peripheral coverage above the target and peeling off a receiver to attack downhill on the intended option. 
Still, he does this well against safeties on short slants. He’s also good at delivering a vertical route to a receiver breaking under a safety 
who is showing his back to the QB. 
Payton displays good anticipation in the quick game versus pressure. He can set up with one step and fire into a tight passing lane due to 
pressure. 
The upper limit of his accuracy is 47 yards (catchable, not pinpoint) to the middle of the field. He lacks a strong arm, and it may prove too 
questionable for him to become a starter if he cleans up his technical skills and refines the rest of his game. 
There’s talk Payton could be the third quarterback off the board. This is probably false. 
It would be an impulsive decision based on Payton’s one year as a starter at a lower level of football and the clear deficiencies he must 
address with his game. This would be an egregious upside pick based on athletic ability, and it would be better if Payton were a third-day 
pick and afforded time to work behind the scenes as a developmental project. 
Drops: He has a three-step drop with slow but long steps. He finishes with his feet at shoulder length to begin his setup. 
Payton has an effective seven-step roll to the left, getting the ball out on that seventh step to the flat. 
Ball Security: When scrambling, Payton carries the ball low and wide from his frame. When hitching or climbing to break the pocket, 
Payton holds the ball with both arms, but wide from his frame. There’s also exaggerated movement with is arms as he hitches and climbs. 
When he breaks the pocket and commits to running, he carries the ball high to his chest with his boundary-side arm and his elbow is tight 
to his side. He can take a hit to the ball or his ball-carrying arm. 
Play Fakes: Payton extends the ball fully as he works through his play fake toward the I-back from center. He turns his back to the defense 
as he retracts the ball, hiding it as he completes his turn on a sprint pass the opposite side as the fake to the back. 
From Pistol, Payton will slow his movement toward the RB to simulate an exchange before he retracts the ball and begins his roll or drop. 
Pump Fakes: Payton has full range of motion with his pump fake, but the violence is minimal. 
Release: Payton holds the ball closer to his stomach than his chest. When he begins his draw, the ball dips even lower – toward his hip -- 
and winds behind his back shoulder. The ball reaches the apex of his draw about a ball’s length behind his back shoulder. 
Payton takes a step with his front foot just before he begins his draw. As he brings the ball forward, Payton transfers his weight from his 
back foot to his front foot. The midline of his back foot and the toes of his front foot are aligned with the target as he begins to transfer his 
weight and bring the ball forward.

QB Jack Strand.elevator_pitch:
Is Jack Strand the best quarterback in this draft class like the buzz from a story on the Internet proclaimed? The ranking 
corresponding with Strand is a clear answer when it comes to my thoughts, but I don’t blame Strand’s agent and/or coaches for 
orchestrating buzz to get attention for the small-school passer. 
After all, the last time a quarterback of note from Minnesota State Moorhead earned an NFL job, it was Phil Simms. I’m also appreciative 
that Strand’s team quarterback coach provided access to his All-22. 
While Strand is not a quarterback I would want in an NFL starting lineup this year, he has the talent to make a roster. There’s growth 
potential for Strand to advance his game and his career. 
Strand has good drops and setups as a shotgun passer. His release motion needs tightening and there will be coaches wanting to address 
his footwork during the release because it’s likely limiting him from attaining optimal velocity. The lack of accuracy is showing up in his 
game. 
Although the release has an elongated motion, Strand gets the ball out of his hands quick. Strand will lead a receiver downfield after the 
receiver settles into an open zone. The placement is just enough downhill to influence the receiver to obey the ball without a forced and 
uncomfortable adjustment. 
If Strand has an inaccuracy tendency with vertical targets, he underthrows the target when he places air under the ball. His vertical targets 
that look like fades force the receiver to turn back for the ball but not in the area where a back-shoulder would be. He’s good at putting air 
under the ball on vertical throws and short targets to the flat over coverage. He must be careful about placing too much air under the ball in 
the shallow range of the field. 
The back-shoulder targets the WR anticipates are placed behind the WR but not to the boundary. This gives the defender a shot to attack 
the ball. This is one of the consistent issues with his accuracy. 
Strand is adept at holding second- and third-level defenders with his eyes to set up targets inside the hash. He’s a decisive thrower when he 
spots his first target breaking open early or a coverage break. When he reads leverage correctly, Strand places the ball to the correct 
shoulder of the receiver facing the tight coverage. 
Strand understands where to split Cover 2, especially a wide Cover2, and he attacks downfield immediately. If the split in C2 isn’t there, he 
has the accuracy and wherewithal to attack the honey hole at the boundary between the safety and corner. 
Strand has misread leverage reads of linebackers inside and above a receiver’s hitch and targeted the route that’s clearly advantageous to 
the defender. He also misreads leverage outside and above the targeted receiver at the top of the stem. 
Strand underestimates trail coverage with opposite-field throws that he must deliver with velocity and a low trajectory. 
He has misread Palms – a C2 variation where the corner pattern matches vs two-receiver sides and will play aggressively in the flat if the 
situation is right. 
Strand identifies open receivers off-script and across the width of the field. He has been successful targeting receivers off script but with 
catchable targets, at best and not pinpoint accuracy. This could become problematic if he makes some of these choices in the NFL. 
Strand throws the ball away when under heavy pressure that he can entirely evade. He will exhaust options while working off-script, finding 
the open man. 
Strand will check the ball, but he has first reactions to buy time and make low probability throws elsewhere. When forced to run, he can 
move the chains, decelerate quickly to make a defender miss, and throw the stiff arm to ward off reaches. 
The jump from Moorhead to the NFL will be big enough that Strand will need time to acclimate, but there’s enough skill for him to develop 
into a third option and eventually graduate to a backup role. 
Drops: Strand drops into a balanced stance with 1- and 2-step drops. He has efficient short drops/setups on screens/RPOs where he 
initially opens to one side of the field before finishing his drop to the target’s side with a balanced stance and efficient release. 
He has three- and five-step drops from shotgun that finishes with him in a balanced setup. 
Ball Security: Strand drops with both hands on the ball. When pressure arrives from the edge and/or up the middle, Strand turns his 
throwing shoulder away from the pressure to protect the ball. 
When he breaks the pocket as a runner, Strand doesn’t tuck the ball until he encounters a defender. Until then, Strand carries the ball loose 
and wide from his frame. He exclusively uses his right arm to carry the ball.

RB Jeremiyah Love.elevator_pitch:
2006 was the first year I published the RSP. Reggie Bush was the next big thing in football. Former 
pros who became analysts compared Bush to Gale Sayers. 
If you’re subscriber under 40 and came to football on your own, it’s likely you don’t know who Sayers was. One of my 
friends is 35, and I had to explain who Sayers was to the history of the game and the running back position. 
It clicked for him when I said Sayers was revered in the same way his parents (who are my age) revere Barry Sanders. 
Broach the names Sayers or Sanders to fans who saw one or both, and they begin speaking with hushed tones. 
Bush earned those comparisons on-air in sports programming before clickbait was a legitimate word. It was my first season 
evaluating players when Bush was the most popular skill player in that draft class. 
While I’m positive my current level of experience would have made the evaluation different, I’ve watched some of those old 
highlights. Bush would have had an excellent score. 
When Bush arrived in New Orleans, he pressed too hard to be the savior of the football team and the city, trying to bounce 
and cutback runs for big plays that he had approached with more discipline at USC. 
Bush needed six seasons to generate three years of production that was, at best, in the general vicinity of his rookie 
expectations. Bush missed 21 games during those first 6 years in the league, and his knees were becoming a problem. 
Bush had a good career, but it was nowhere near the expectations pre-draft. For fans and media who remember this as well 
as I, Jeremiyah Love’s style not only bears similarities to Bush, but there’s a vibe that public who knew Bush’s USC game 
are hoping Love has the leading role in “Reggie Bush II: This Time It’s for Real.” 
Considered the top skill-player in this draft class, Love is a versatile big-play threat with tremendous athletic ability for his 
size. If you could mix a lot of the stylistic elements of Bush and Alvin Kamara and blend their frames, you’d get a player like 
Love. 
Love is a slippery runner with quick feet, sudden deceleration and acceleration, hip mobility, sharp lateral cuts, and the hops 
to clear safeties aiming for his waist. He excels at making the first defender miss, even when it’s in the backfield. 
Love has NFL starter-caliber skills as a gap runner. His work with Duo and other interior tight-crease blocking could use 
more patience to set up second-level blocks. 
Although there are lapses with patience, Love has shown more than enough time’s that he will press within 1-2 steps of his 
blockers to set up creases. Once Love finds an open lane, he has good-to-great acceleration and elite speed to pull away 
from pursuit. 
He’s not just a speedster. Love wins against contact, often multiple points of contact during the same touch. 
Love bounces off hits at all three levels of the defense because he attacks collisions. A lot of backs don’t do this well 
anymore. 
61
RB Jeremiyah Love.pre_draft_advice:
For many, Love is the top prospect among the skill players in this class. Love is the most 
likely to have a high-ceiling impact as a rookie and to sustain starter production for consecutive years early in his career. 
If you’re looking at having the rights to a player for 8-10 years and generating starter production for most of them, 
Fernando Mendoza could have more value depending on the league. If you’re seeking 6-8 years with the most difference-

Love and Tate are 2 of the first 3-5 picks in this class. If your team is a contender and needs one impactful piece right now,

Last year, I detailed how Fannin’s “bad NFL Combine” was an overstated and overrated analysis point. Fannin had a strong

The gist of the analysis was about interpreting the tests, knowing how much weight to give them compared to other sources 
of information, and who else compared stylistically to Fannin in the NFL with similar production. 
Johnson is this year’s candidate for “The NFL Combine Threw a Red Herring into the Collective’s Draft Analysis.” 
Let’s begin with Johnson’s 4.56-second, 40-Yard Dash. It’s the same time as Aaron Jones and they had similar dimensions 
at that point in their careers. The number is fast enough. 
So is the 4.29-second, 20 Yard-Shuttle. A 4.30-second mark is the RSP’s minimum standard guideline for the Starter Tier. 
Both marks are in the Starter Tier, for whatever that’s worth when considering that the timing technology is either second- 
or third-tier technology with a high variation that makes it much less reliable than they want you to believe. 
These were underwhelming marks for Johnson because the media and draft culture have been brainwashed to overvalue 
Relative Athletic Score percentiles. RAS is an entertaining and informative resource. 
There’s value to knowing the percentile that an NFL Combine participant’s performance is relative to his peers. It 
unintentionally creates noise for the signal. 
That signal is knowing what the minimum expected times should be for each position based on physical dimensions, style of 
movement, and expected role (elite starter, starter, rotational contributor, situational contributor, reserve, and free agent. 
These factors provide valuable context well beyond RAS percentiles. When Johnson ran a 7.32-second, Three Cone Drill, the 
RAS Percentile Apostles of the Pseudo-Science Church of Oblong Money Ball that Bounces Too Funny for Predictability in

Let’s dig deeper than the good book, shall we?

RB Demond Claiborne.elevator_pitch:
Claiborne has some LeSean “Shady” McCoy to his game. Definitely not the weight, we’ll get to that 
soon enough. It’s Claiborne’s pace changes, peripheral vision, ability to flip his hips to create sudden and sharp escape 
angles. 
Shady often looked like he was walking a tightrope, a matador facing off a bull, or Oberon performing acrobatics against the 
Mountain. Claiborne possesses that similar sense of control and bravado at the razor’s edge. 
Claiborne changes of direction are notably quick because he’s skilled at playing with pacing. He presses slow and deep 
before entering the crease with breathtaking suddenness. 
There’s a legitimate feel for the flow of the defense. Whether that comes from studying box counts pre-snap, his peripheral 
vision, or advanced processing that comes from extensive experience, Claiborne has it. 
As daring as Claiborne’s runs can look, he’s also a wise decision-maker who takes what the defense gives him and doesn’t 
take risks in the red zone and other short-yardage situations. This may not show up early in Claiborne’s game in the NFL, 
because he may have to recalibrate his gauge when it comes to the speed and quickness of box defenders. 
Claiborne pairs head and shoulder fakes with footwork better than most backs I’ve watched in recent years. Watching 
Claiborne move with the football is like listening to a musician who can tell a lyrical story with impressive technique versus 
musician who shreds with technical patterns but hasn’t learned to make it as musical. 
While Claiborne may need to recalibrate his gauge for defensive pursuit as a mover, he has excellent speed and 
acceleration, so don’t expect a massive struggle. Clairborne turns the corner all three levels of the defense, including 
cornerbacks. He’ll hold off most defenders in the open field with a clean lane – or clean enough to weave his open-field 
magic. 
Claiborne’s power is good for his size, but that power is ordinary by RB standards. He’ll run through reaches, bounce off 
glancing shots and win some direction collision as the aggressor into contact. He can drag or push defenders when wrapped 
high. 
At his current weight, Claiborne doesn’t pull through wraps, and his contact balance is limited. If there’s an area that could 
limit Claiborne’s ceiling as a professional, it’s the 188-pound frame. 
There will be legitimate questions about how much of a workload Claiborne can handle each week. Will the league prove too 
punishing? 
Jahvid Best was a great young running back, but concussions forced him to retire after three years in the league. Best, a 
dynamic speedster, was 5’9”, 190 pounds. 
There are questions about Bucky Irving’s workload moving forward. The 5’10”, 195-pound runner couldn’t get healthy last 
year after suffering an injury that usually doesn’t take so long to heal. 
71

RB Myles Montgomery.elevator_pitch:
Montgomery is the RSP’s pick as the most underrated skill talent in the 2026 class. A Hula Bowl 
participant, Montgomery didn’t earn an invitation to the NFL Combine. 
I searched for news about Montgomery online after I finalized my grade. Based on the results, I’d be surprised if he’s 
considered a top 20 talent on most RB boards. 
As a point of comparison, I have Montgomery nearly 10 points higher than my grade for his former teammate, RJ Harvey. 
He’s not as explosive as Harvey, but he’s a wiser runner, a more adept processor of information, and a better all-round 
player on passing downs. 
Processing information is one of Montgomery’s true strengths. He sees what’s happening early, accurately, and it helps him 
be a step ahead of opponents. 
He’s a creative runner with efficient moves who can mitigate the impact of defensive penetration that foils plays early as 
well as produce as a short-yardage runner. 
Montgomery knows when to attack tight creases, when to create, and when to stick with the play design. He has some 
lapses where he overthinks his decision about playing it safe vs. taking a risk and errs on playing it safe. 
Montgomery is one of the most underrated movers for his position in this class. The thing that sets him apart is his 
processing of information and refined skill for combining movement and pacing to generate the desired result. 
Montgomery has better acceleration than top speed. His speed is on the cusp of the Starter Tier. Montgomery’s acceleration 
is in the upper range of the Starter Tier. Combined with his precise footwork, quickness, and fast processor, Montgomery 
will create space in the NFL. 
Smaller backs who thrive in the NFL understand how to dictate contact with defenders. Jamaal Charles was always one of 
the best examples. Montgomery isn’t in Charles’ tier as an athlete, but his athleticism is good enough to execute 
successfully at the point of attack. 
Montgomery understands if he attacks larger defenders first, beating them to the punch, that he can dictate terms. This 
means he’s often making the second move away from the defender as the defender is still reacting to the first move. 
Montgomery has shown a knack for doing this against defensive ends and linebackers. 
Other than one clap attack and a focus drop, those are the only two drops over a 27-target sample during the past two 
years of Montgomery’s tape. 
Montgomery is primarily a check-down receiver, but he has some good stem and break work with routes when detached 
from the formation. There’s more potential to mine here. 
Based on the hierarchy of college all-star games, Montgomery is likely a UDFA looking for training camp invite. I grade 
talent, I don’t do rankings based on potential NFL Draft capital. I don’t ask scouts and personnel directors what they think. 
Most rankings from Big Draft do this. That’s not necessarily a bad thing. It’s that content provider’s desire to be accurate 
about where players are getting drafted and how the NFL is assessing talent. 
76
RB Myles Montgomery.pre_draft_advice:

the draft while you have expanded offseason rosters. He’s more likely a late round pick in leagues with the largest rosters

RB Nicholas Singleton.elevator_pitch:
Singleton burst on the college football scene a few years ago as one of the top recruited backs in the 
country. By year’s end, Singleton and the less-heralded Quinshon Judkins were the marquee freshman performers. 
My former podcast co-host, Felix Sharpe, asked me to watch the two later in the year. I thought Judkins was a more 
powerful runner with twitchier movement. Still, I liked Singleton’s game and believed he deserved his early acclaim. 
Fast-forward to the present and Singleton played in a committee, his coach got fired, and he was paired with a quarterback 
who didn’t perform to his physical potential. This may contribute to production declines and lead draft analysts to be down 
on Singleton’s draft stock. 
Singleton isn’t flash, but he’s a smart, patient and scheme versatile runner. His footwork is well connected with what his 
eyes see and his brain processes. 
Singleton has strong acceleration and good speed. He’ll beat defensive backs – including some cornerbacks – into the 
secondary and hold them off to flip the field, if not breakaway for a long score. 
He’s a well-built runner who can pull through reaches from all three levels of defenders. When he encounters defenders in 
his path that he can’t avoid, he accelerates into the collision with good pad level. 
Whatever the angle of the collision, Singleton can bounce off the hits of linebackers. He forces opponents to gang tackle 
him. 
Singleton shines as an outlet receiver as well as a first read on screens and RPOs. There are lapses with clap attack when 
reaching wide for a throw as well as focus drops, but these are first-world problems for otherwise high-volume receivers. 
Singleton’s blocking is competent and there’s room to clean up details that will make him reliable for the NFL. 
Overall, Singleton has a versatile game. He’s a candidate to build on his college career and become a capable NFL starter. 
Vision: Singleton is patient with gap blocking, reads the leverage of defenders in the box, and finds the open hole—this 
includes cutbacks. When running a gap play to the perimeter, Singleton gives a brief press inside to set up the outside 
crease. 
Singleton is patient with zone blocking, reading the leverage of defenders on the play side and finding a cutback to set up 
with a press of a block that gets within 1-2 steps of the blocker and defender. He sets up double teams on the backside of 
his approach with a deep press, demonstrating an understanding of the play design. 
There are off tackle plays where the backside crease is coming open where he can continue to press outside 1-2 steps 
longer. 
Singleton identifies penetration as he approaches the exchange, and he avoids some of the contact so he can continue 
forward. He also identifies linebacker run blitzes between the tackles pre-snap. 
80
RB Nicholas Singleton.pre_draft_advice:

RB Jadarian Price.elevator_pitch:
The talented sidekick to a star player in a backfield often gets a pre-draft bump from media 
evaluators. Often, it has proven unwarranted. I don’t know if I have a good feel for the dynamics driving this bias, but Felix 
Jones, Roschon Johnson, and Sony Michel come to mind. 
Jadarian Price could join this list, although I’m inclined to believe Price will transcend it. I don’t take many liberties with the 
RSP’s grading system. There’s only one point separating RB2-RB6 on the board, and they are all in the same tier. 
There are projected improvements baked into the scoring system already, but I tend to underestimate the value of those 
likely improvements, because it’s my grading philosophy to make a player prove what he can do instead of presuming he 
will. 
In Price’s case, a player who is slated to have a higher NFL Draft capital than most of the names above him on the RSP’s 
board, I have no problem endorsing Price as one of your top three RBs. He’ll get an early opportunity due to the capital and 
that will facilitate experience and potentially quicker growth. 
Price is a scheme versatile runner who doesn’t take creative liberties that could hurt the offensive game script, but he is 
adept at finding cutback lanes, and he’s creative in space. 
Price has mobile hips, he adjusts stride length and pace to set up rushing lanes, he keeps his knees and feet high as he runs 
past low shots, and he drops his weight deep enough for strong deceleration. Pace also possesses curvilinear speed so he 
doesn’t have to make hard cuts with every run. 
Price must keep his feet tighter when setting up moves while tight to the line otherwise, he doesn’t operate from a place of 
balance, and it limits his skills. 
Price has the speed, acceleration, power, and ability to bounce off glancing shots like a starting NFL runner. 
Other than his occasional lapses with his wide stance, Price has all the tools necessary for him to develop into a high-end 
contributor. This includes blocking, routes, and catching the ball. All three have minor issues that need refining, but they 
aren’t fatal flaw on their own. 
If Price can add 10-15 pounds to his frame and either maintain or increase his speed, he’ll eventually start in the NFL. If 
Price doesn’t add the muscle, he could still deliver as a productive RB2 in an offense. 
Vision: Price reads the leverage of a double team and the unblocked end and sticks tight to the interior block to maximize 
his space away from the unblocked end. He identifies interior penetration as he’s taking the exchange and finds the bounce 
or cutback lane. 
Price sets up zone and gap blocking effectively. He finds cutback lanes between the tackles and hits the decisively. 
Price doesn’t try reversals of field, taking what the design and the defense gives him if there are no nearby cut back or 
bounce lanes. He’s creative in the open field and finds cutback lanes in the second and third level for big plays. 
Still, Price doesn’t take unnecessary risks and manages the game well. He identifies good leverage in dense traffic and hits 
the crease decisively. 
84
RB Jadarian Price.pre_draft_advice:
Although Price’s ranking is slightly lower than RBs 2-5, his ceiling is as high and his 
opportunity due to NFL Draft capital will be better. If you want to consider Price as a top three RB pick, I have no argument

The biggest question is whether Haynes can make a living between the tackles. The outlook would seem more promising if 
Hayes can gain 10-15 pounds and have a frame like Aaron Jones. It’s likely that as a college slot receiver for most of his 
career that Haynes will add quick-twitch muscle to his frame. 
Kyren Williams is much less explosive and only weighed a few pounds more than Haynes when entering the league. Bucky 
Irving arrived in the league at 5’9”, 192 pounds. While there will likely be a public referendum on Irving’s size being an 
obstacle to full-time volume and his ability to handle it, there’s more to the story. 
Still, anytime a running back weighs less than 200 pounds, there will be questions. For the sake of Haynes’ elevator pitch, 
let’s project that Hayens follows Williams, Jones, Jahmyr Gibbs, and a few other backs who shared his height and gained 
weight to get over 200 pounds after entering the league. 
If Haynes gets between 205-210 pounds and maintains or adds more explosion in the process, the skills to start in the NFL 
are there to mine. Whether it’s setting up pullers or double teams in Gap, Duo, or Inside Zone, Haynes has the leverage-
reading skills to do the job. 
Despite his dynamic speed, acceleration, and quickness, Hayens has shown wise decision-making based on game script and 
down and distance. When defenses foil the blocking scheme early, Haynes demonstrates a feel for the pursuit and finds 
solutions. 
Haynes has the footwork of a good running back. He alters stride and pace, uses curvilinear bend, and gets his feet over low 
shots in traffic. As we’ve seen with many elusive backs of all shapes and sizes, Haynes uses footwork that wide receivers 
use to earn separation at the line to make defenders miss in traffic and the open field. 
Haynes has power for his size and that will improve with added muscle. At Georgia Tech, Haynes has won head-on collisions 
at the end of the crease against safeties and off-ball linebackers. He finishes with low pad level and uses his legs and leans 
well enough to push these defenders downhill, if not run through them. 
Haynes isn’t going to break the will of a defense with his physical play, but he can be physical with defensive backs and 
withstand physical play in the box – just like the backs mentioned earlier. 
As I’ll mention later with Adam Randall, players converted from receiver to running back are usually switching because they 
aren’t good wide receivers. There’s some aspect of their game that’s missing. 
This is likely the truth for Haynes as well. His stem work lacks refined moves, but he plays with pacing and he has 
technically sound breaks. He’s also effective at finding and settling in open zones. 
92
RB Jamal Haynes.pre_draft_advice:
Haynes is worth a late-round pick before the NFL Draft. After the NFL Draft, he could earn 
draft capital and/or a team fit that bumps him to a mid-round pick. Either way, Haynes is a worthwhile component for a 
low-risk approach with a potential high-reward in 1-2 years. 
94

RB TJ Harden.elevator_pitch:
Harden could appeal to teams moving to heavy base sets. He’s a well-built power runner with better 
acceleration than long speed. 
Harden is difficult to bring down. He runs through multiple reaches, wraps, and hits during a carry. His contact balance is 
good and in the case of handling indirect shots, it might be better than what I could track when charting his game. 
Harden is an agile bulldozer who protects the ball. He decelerates efficiently and has dynamic jump cuts and spins in tight 
quarters to mitigate pursuit angles. Harden pairs his moves well and often turn losses into gains 
Harden only has 2 fumbles in nearly 600 career carries. He’s not one of those power backs with poor technique that gets 
exploited whenever in traffic or fighting for extra yards. 
One of the bonus features of Harden’s game is his hands. He has excellent hand-eye coordination, a primary receiver’s 
catch radius, and good body control near the boundary. 
Harden hasn’t demonstrated an extensive route tree, but he runs leak routes well. He’s a capable pass protector against 
safeties and linebackers right now. He has shown moments where he can handle some assignments against edge defenders. 
There’s more upside to mine with Harden’s blocking that should make him an asset. 
The big question marks will be speed and acceleration. He’s a committee-level player in these two areas, but his 
acceleration is on the cusp of the starter tier. If Harden proves he’s explosive enough, he could emerge in 1-2 years as a 
high-end contributor. 
If not, he’s likely on the back end of a roster and earns situational playing time. 
Vision: When Harden identifies favorable leverage early, he hits the crease decisively on inside zone. When running power 
or other gap blocking, Harden adjusts his stride and pace with footwork patterns to give his puller time to make the lead 
block and then accelerates into the crease. 
Harden begins his zone runs heading downhill to press his blockers. When he identifies unblocked penetration, he adjusts to 
the opening that has the best leverage for him to access in the situation. 
Elusiveness: Harden can decelerate on an edge path within two steps with good weight drop and sidestep a force defender 
shooting for his legs. He can turn a loss into a gain with this effort. His two-step deceleration also helps him elude middle 
linebackers coming downhill into the gap. 
Harden has an effective double up to adjust his stride length and pace when setting up blocks with inside runs. 
In short-yardage situations, Harden presses interior creases, but takes what’s available rather than attempting the cutback. 
Harden has an effective spin in tight quarters to work away from pursuit angles. He can make a small jump stop to avoid a 
penetrating DB shooting for his feet at the line of scrimmage. 
Harden has a good pairing of a jump cut and pressure cut to work around penetration and then into the open crease the 
penetration left behind. 
96
RB TJ Harden.pre_draft_advice:
Likely a UDFA, Harden is worth a cheap investment at the end of your drafts. In fantasy 
drafts after the NFL Draft, you might have to bump him up a round if the NFL selects Harden. If not, you can wait for your 
post-draft waiver run for your expanded offseason rosters.

RB Davon Booth.elevator_pitch:
Booth is another one of the sleepers in this running back class who could become product part of a 
committee. My first impression of Booth was that he was a more explosive Kyle Monangai without Monangai’s relentless 
power in the open field. 
Booth has a low center of gravity and the pads and footwork to bounce off hits from linebackers and safeties. He works 
through reaches, wards off contact with his stiff arm, and he can pull through some wraps after he has dragged the 
defender along. I still stand by that characterization, but there’s more. 
Booth’s creative skill in the open field while operating at a good rate of speed as well as his curvilinear movement reminded 
me of Dalvin Cook and Khalil Herbert – a pair of accomplished runners between the tackles who can break big-play runs 
that flip the field, if not break away from the pack. 
Then, I watched Booth catch the ball. He was more reliable than Herbert and more dynamic than Cook. Booth can take 
contact while adjusting to targets that lack pinpoint accuracy. Combine the best of Monangai, Herbert, and Cook, and you 
get a back the caliber of J.K. Dobbins before the Achilles tear and the knee surgeries. 
Booth isn’t on Dobbins’ level as a creator or after-contact runner, but he has the athletic ability and skills to do a mean 
rendition of Herbert circa Chicago Bears but with legitimate passing-down potential. 
Depending on the offense, Booth might have to prove he’s more than a check-down/screen option who can execute a fuller 
route tree with nuance. Still many teams aren’t seeking anything more than leak routes and Booth can do this. 
Booth has lapses with his punch and position at the point of attack as a blocker. He has shown that he can handle 
linebackers and safeties. If he cleans up his lapses, he should become a reliable pass protector. 
Consider Booth a Khalil Herbet clone with more three-down potential – a smart runner with good explosion who runs 
tougher between the tackles than his size might lead you to believe. 
Vision: Booth has a can press within a step of the line of scrimmage to set up cutbacks and bounces. Booth is decisive 
through tight creases in the box and the second level. 
Booth identifies edge penetration and finds bounce lanes. He attacks gap plays decisively. He finds cutbacks on gap plays. 
He’s adept at both gap and zone. 
In the second level, Booth identifies pursuit from the periphery of his vision and veers away from it. He creates solutions 
when pursuit blocks the way in the second level of the defense. He finds cutbacks quickly as he clears the initial hole. 
Elusiveness: Booth has effective pressure cuts across multiple gaps. He has curvilinear speed to bend away from pursuit at 
a good rate. 
Booth can transition downhill from a perimeter approach with two steps. He can decelerate in two steps and reverse field. 
Booth adjusts the length of his stride to set up defenders in the open field. He’ll spin off contact in creases to the open 
space. 
99
RB Davon Booth.pre_draft_advice:
If Booth earns early Day Three NFL Draft capital, consider him in the middle rounds in 
drafts with rosters of 35 players or greater. Consider him at the end of drafts for teams with smaller rosters. 
100

Mari Taylor.elevator_pitch:
Taylor is likely a career backup, but the caliber of backup that could have 1-2 strong seasons as a 
starter if called upon to substitute for a franchise-caliber back. Taylor runs with good power, breaking tackles, bouncing off 
collisions with linebackers and safeties, and drags and pushes runners who wrap him high. 
His decision-making and agility are good enough to get Taylor into creases and/or avoid unblocked defenders. Taylor takes 
what the defense and the scheme give him. He doesn’t take outsized risks based on down and distance and field position. 
Taylor has the footwork and cutting ability to create space with efficiency and dynamism. It makes Taylor dangerous 
between the tackles and in the open field. 
Taylor has the acceleration to reach the secondary, the power to run through defenders at this level, and the speed to at 
least maintain the separation he initially earned with his burst so he can prolong the run and force defensive backs to give 
chase. 
Taylor is a check-down/screen receiver who must prove he can do more if his new NFL offense demands a bigger route tree. 
Otherwise, he has capable hands if you’re optimistic Taylor’s focus drops will subside or remain at a low frequency of 
occurrence. 
Despite Taylor’s rugged work, he has a strong track record with ball security. His pass protection will improve as Taylor 
curtails his habit of overextending at the point of attack. 
Taylor should help a team and contribute on the field. He could develop into a starter if he adds more quick-twitch muscle 
that aids his power, speed, and acceleration. 
Vision: Taylor sets up his puller inside-out to work around penetration at the line of scrimmage and get into a crease. He 
has a feel for soft spots in traffic to keep plays alive after wrapped. 
Taylor sets up his double team on inside zone with a press to the inside shoulder of the inside defender within a step of the 
block before bouncing to the outside gaps. Once in the second-level, Taylor is decisive about splitting blocks in the 
secondary and using his low center of gravity to run through defenders. 
Taylor identifies penetration off the edge and mitigates it with his burst and movement. He also identifies interior 
penetration and can make a quick jump stop to avoid the lineman and reach the hole. 
Taylor presses within a step of his blockers before executing cutbacks and bounces. 
When bouncing a run outside, Taylor shows some skill to press the tackle to incrementally work to the edge. He also 
presses lead blocks in the open field within a step of the blocker. 
Taylor takes what the defense and the scheme give him. He doesn’t take outsized risks based on down and distance and 
field position. 
Elusiveness: Taylor has an effective jump cut to work across the back of a blocker. He also can slide across the face of an 
unblocked lineman penetrating into the backfield. 
102
Mari Taylor.pre_draft_advice:
Taylor is a mid-round consideration in a draft with 7-10 picks. He’s an end of round 
consideration in shorter drafts of 4-6 picks.

Veon Moss.elevator_pitch:
Moss has a running style that makes you want to love his game because he plays with an edge. You’d 
guess he was 220 pounds based on his running style. The same could be said about 205-pounder, Walter Payton. 
Moss is no Payton, but he also has enough size to earn productive workloads in the NFL. Moss attacks creases with urgency. 
He’s decisive into tight creases and sets up blocks on perimeter runs. 
Although good at taking what the defense gives him in the green zone, which is vital, Moss has a bad habit of cutting back 
on gap plays because he’s running to open space instead of sticking with the play design. He must also show more patience 
with Counter. 
Few backs accelerate into collisions anymore. Moss is one who does. It makes him a capable tackle-breaker who wins 
collisions with linebackers and safeties. 
What Moss lacks in speed, he can make up for with acceleration and cuts. Moss’s acceleration is good enough for him to 
reach the secondary and hold off pursuit long enough to flip the field. It’s also good enough for Moss to turn the short 
corner on perimeter runs against box defenders. 
Moss had good deceleration, hip mobility, and footwork that can be precise, subtle, and dynamic. If he can stay healthy, 
Moss has enough well-rounded skills to develop into a productive “B” back in a committee backfield. 
Vision: Moss runs with urgency on gap plays, attacking the creases downhill and getting past his pullers on blockers as 
quickly as possible. At the same time, he’ll press close to his pullers to set up the block. 
He identifies interior or edge penetration and quickly transitions downhill under the penetrator. 
Moss has a bad habit of attacking backside creases when he sees an open space, but it can come at the expense of the 
play’s success. Moss has run into the back of blockers because he attacks the wrong gap when he could have continued to 
set up Counter with greater patience to the front-side gap. 
He’s good at setting up lead blockers in open space and perimeter runs, pressing within one step of the blocker. Between 
the tackles, Moss is a decisive tight-crease runner. 
When in the green zone, Moss takes what the blocking gives him and uses his size and physicality to move forward. In the 
open field, Moss veers away from pursuit closing on him and sustains runs for much longer as a result. 
Elusiveness: He hurdles linebackers shooting for his feet in open space. Moss can drop his weight at the top of the stem. 
Moss can stick his foot in the ground and open his hips in the opposite direction. 
Moss can transition downhill from a perimeter approach with one step – a jump stop. He uses two steps to transition 
downhill from a perimeter approach when he has a longer runway. 
Moss adjusts his stride length and pace to set up creases and defenders in creases. He uses a double up effective as one of 
those stride adjustments. 
105
Veon Moss.pre_draft_advice:
Moss is a mid-round selection in fantasy drafts. He could develop into a committee back 
similar with what we saw in Detroit. Moss’s ceiling is high enough to become a lead back with a more explosive partner on 
passing downs.

RB Roman Hemby.elevator_pitch:
Hemby is one of those players who has a high floor of potential, but a lower ceiling than some of the 
players ranked below him whose floor/ceilings are volatile by comparison. Hemby is a capable runner in zone and gap 
schemes. 
Regardless of the scheme, Hemby has a feel for setting up good angles for himself and taking away good angles from 
defenders. 
Hemby attacks lanes decisively on gap runs. He sets up double teams on Duo with good stride length and pacing 
adjustments. When he’s forced to bounce, he opens his hips efficiently. When he reaches the edge of the defense, he 
decelerates efficiently to transition downhill. 
Hemby finishes runs with starter-level power and contact balance. His ball security track record is excellent. Hemby can 
earn volume and deliver. 
Hemby’s speed and acceleration are between committee and starter grades. He’ll get some corners on perimeter plays and 
he can often reach the secondary and force a long chase. It depends on the play type and the personnel he’s facing. 
Overall, Hemby is explosive enough to contribute on a statistically relevant level. 
Hemby’s pass-catching is good, but his blocking and routes can be better. Hemby can deliver as a check-down option right 
now. His blocking is not ready for a significant role on passing downs. 
Hemby can become a capable RB3 with an RB2 ceiling. 
Vision: Hemby reads the double team on inside zone and looks for advantageous leverage. When it’s not there, he cuts 
back or bounces to the next gap with a soft spot or opening and takes what is available. 
Hemby presses within 1-3 steps of the line of scrimmage depending on the blocking and leverage of the key defenders in 
the scheme. 
Elusiveness: Hemby adjusts stride length and pacing to set up combo blocks to the linebackers on Duo. When this doesn’t 
happen in timely fashion, Hemby opens his hips with the point of his boundary-side foot to the sideline and bounces the run 
outside efficiently. 
Hemby just needs two steps to decelerate and drop his weight so he can transition downhill from a perimeter approach on a 
toss play. 
He uses a stick to freeze and accelerate past a CB at the second level. 
Hemby’s ability to alter his stride and pace works well between the tackles on inside zone. 
Hemby can spin off contact. 
Hemby only needs two steps to transition from a perimeter approach outside the numbers to the short-side boundary and 
get downhill on a zone run. 
108
RB Roman Hemby.pre_draft_advice:
Hemby is a player to monitor from afar despite the high floor. This may change if Hemby 
earns a draft pick.

RB Leshon Williams.elevator_pitch:
Before I tell you about Leshon Williams, let me tell you about Camille Thurman. I come from a musical 
background. A lot of what I’ve learned from that environment translates to what I’ve been doing for the past 22 years. 
Football is a mix of theory, structure, and improvisation. So is Jazz. Players and performers have a lot in common, 
especially when it comes to their respective stages and how emotion is involved, especially during a performer’s 
developmental years. 
Camille Thurman is a world-class saxophonist and singer. She has several players on her Comparison Spectrum. Joe
