Henderson is one I hear prominently. The simplest way I can describe Henderson’s place in the history of the instrument 
and the music is this analogy: 
If I asked the average 35-year-old American who doesn’t listen to jazz if they know who John Coltrane was, they’d know. If 
I asked them who Johnny Hodges was, they’d have no idea, but Hodges was one of the main saxophonists Coltrane studied. 
In the same vein, if I asked the average 35-year-old American who didn’t the NFL who Patrick Mahomes was, they’d know. 
If I asked them who Fran Tarketon was – the first off-script scrambler with flights that rivaled Mahomes and opened the 
game to be more accepting of this skill archetype, paving the way for Mahomes decades later – they’d have no idea. 
Thurman sounds like the Fran Tarkenton of the saxophone and Sarah Vaughn – the Barry Sanders of singing. If you gave 
Sanders Bo Jackson’s power, Walter Payton’s stamina, and Marshall Faulk’s versatility – even then – I’m not sure I’ve 
mashed up enough greatness to do Sarah Vaughn justice. 
Thurman sounds like these two geniuses. She’s probably not more than 35 years old. When learning about her history, she 
nearly quit music because a high school instructor that replaced her original teacher was marginalizing the female musicians 
in her school. 
This discouraged Thurman enough that she decided not to pursue music in college. It took the encouragement of several 
people to convince the young Thurman that experience wasn’t going to be the experience with music, and she was too good 
not to perform if that’s what she wanted to pursue. 
I thought of Thurman’s story when I read about Leshon Wiliams, who has a starter ceiling in the NFL. Williams lost his love 
for the game in 2024 and according to KU Strength and Conditioning Coach Matt Gildersleeve, Williams wasn’t in good 
shape and lacked the approach he needed to thrive at football. 
According Gildersleeve, who had this conversation with 247 Sports, “When [Williams] came here, you could tell he hadn’t 
done much. I don’t want to say struggle, but it took him a long time to get there, to see where he’s at now.” 
KU Head Coach Lance Leipold said Williams grew “by leaps and bounds” between December of 2024 and fall camp of 2025. 
“He’s a totally different athlete now,” said Gildersleeve. “But to me, it’s always the mental side of things that when you have 
an athlete that they’re enjoying waking up and being where they are every day and doing what they love to do, that part is 
tremendous. You can see it in how he goes about every day.” 
116
RB Leshon Williams.pre_draft_advice:
Consider Williams a viable late-round pick who will likely be a UDFA or selection in the 6th-
7th round for the NFL. Stash him for the summer and determine in August if Williams earns a long-term stash.

RB Kaytron Allen.elevator_pitch:
Allen should be a stalwart RB3/RB4 on several rosters for as long as he stays healthy and maintains 
his athletic prowess. He could have some years as a committee back or even a low-end starter for teams in a desperate 
situation because he’s a powerful runner with good decision-making, agility, and reliable ball security. 
Allen is physical. He breaks tackles, bounces off hits, and pushes piles. His pad level can be late at the point of attack, and 
it costs him yardage he’d otherwise earn. 
Whether it’s gap or zone, Allen earns what’s blocked for him and finds bounce lanes and cutbacks when defenders fill the 
gaps. Allen can overreact with his bounces in short yardage when a more incremental move to the next available gap is 
wise. Otherwise, he’s a smart runner who rarely places his offense in a bad situation with an unforced error. 
Allen has mobile hips to make 90-degree changes of direction efficiently. He adjusts his stride and pace to set up blockers 
and pursuit. When in the open field, Allen can deliver combinations of moves to set up unblocked defenders. If there’s a 
move, Allen has it and he delivers it with competence and occasional dynamism. 
Speed and acceleration are the weaknesses of Allen’s ball carrying. They are good enough for him to make an active roster 
in the NFL and even contribute as an RB2 – at least on paper. 
The current reality of the NFL is that there’s a glut of RB talent. Most backs on depth charts in the league have starter-level 
acceleration and speed – not what most NFL Combine followers think starter-level explosion is, but the RSP’s minimum 
standards for a starter and a committee-level contributor. 
Allen’s speed and acceleration are closer to the minimum for a contributor, and not a starter. Most reserves are closer to the 
starter tier. 
Think Trey Sermon, who has a lot of starter-level skills, but his lack of explosiveness hindered his play and his opportunities 
in the NFL. Sermon’s top speed in 2020 at Ohio State was 21.7 mph. This should be closer to his average and not his 
outlier. His top five plays were between 20.2 mph and 21.7 mph. 
Kaytron Allen’s top speed was 20.6 mph and low range of his top five plays was 19.9 mph. Based on what I’ve seen on film 
Allen isn’t fast enough to become a starter in most systems. 
The rest of his game is good enough to make a roster, but he may fall out of favor at the end of a season once coaches 
trust the younger player with more explosion in his athletic profile. Then, Allen will be on the next town as the RB3/RB4 
insurance policy who can handle volume, hold onto the ball, and execute the scheme, but isn’t fast enough for their long-
term needs. 
Vision: Allen reads leverage well and presses creases within a step of his blockers to access cutback lanes between the 
tackles. 
Allen finds bounces after attacking interior rushing lanes that get clogged. He doesn’t arbitrarily bounce to the farthest lane, 
but he rides the wave to the nearest outside lane and works his way there incrementally. There are some short yardage 
120
RB Kaytron Allen.pre_draft_advice:
Allen is a late-round selection in most leagues in case Allen plays faster than he did at 
Penn State. He’s a good all-around back, but doesn’t project to become a good fantasy contributor.

RB Kaelon Black.elevator_pitch:
Black is a smart runner with cutback ability who also attacks downhill with ferocity for his size. He 
finds cutbacks with deep presses into the line, but when it’s time to hit a tight crease as designed, Black gets skinny. 
Black has good footwork, understands how to manipulate defenders with pace and stride changes, and he has dynamic 
jump stops and spins to avoid late shots. If Black can get his feet higher in traffic, he could become even tougher to bring to 
the turf. 
Black is quick enough to reach the secondary and fast enough to make defensive backs chase him down after flipping the 
field. At the end of runs, Black delivers contact with excellent pad level. He forces defenders to wrap him, and he’ll push and 
drag defenders, bouncing off multiple reaches and/or hits to his frame. 
Black has enough skills as a pass-protector and pass-catcher that these facets of his game shouldn’t hold him back from 
competing for playing time this year if the depth chart is thin enough or Black impresses fast enough. 
Black reminds me of Jordan James or a slower version of J.K. Dobbins without the same refinement, but enough versatility 
to continue growing into committee-level asset. 
Vision: Black displays enough patience with heavy run boxes to adjust his approach to buy time for creases to open but 
when they don’t, he takes the nearest soft spot at the line available and makes the most of the play. 
Black also presses stalk blocks at the second level, pressing within a step of one side of the blocker to set up the bounce or 
cutback to the open crease. 
Black identifies interior penetration as he approaches the line and bounces the run away from the defender. 
In short-yardage situations, Black has attacked the designed crease hard, hitting it decisively with his pads low. There are 
short-yardage runs where he should have done this but opted to press deep and bounce it when there was a crease to hit 
decisively up the middle. He got the yardage, but the decision wasn’t optimal. 
Black presses the back-side hip of the puller to set up gap runs working to the outside. He presses inside gaps to set up 
perimeter designs. He’s a decisive gap runner whether it’s an interior run or a perimeter design. 
Black gets skinny through creases after attacking the press point at a good pace and cutting back. 
Elusiveness: Black adjusts his gait and pace as he approaches the line of scrimmage on interior run designs. He points 
outside foot to the boundary to open his hips for 90-degree turns. He can do this to make transitions at the edge of the box 
with two steps and make the force defender miss. 
Black has a capable jump stop at the edge of a crease to adjust his gait and pace so he can set up a block at the second 
level. 
Black only needs two quick steps to transition from a perimeter approach to a downhill path. He spins off glancing shots to 
get downhill for an extra 1-3 yards when he doesn’t keep his feet. 
123
RB Kaelon Black.pre_draft_advice:
Black is a patience play, but he could pop if there are training camp injuries on a depth 
chart. He’ll likely endear himself to coaches and teammates with his competitive demeanor, smarts, and physical playing 
style. 
Black would be an unlikely full-time starter, but he has the profile of a player who might have 1-2 massive years due to a 
season-ending injury as the reliable replacement behind a good offensive line. He’s a late-round value.

RB Desmond Reid.elevator_pitch:
Is Reid the next Bucky Irving? By the end of 2026, we might be asking if Bucky Irving is what his 
believers thought after he couldn’t stay healthy after a little more than one season of a heavy workload in the NFL. The 
Buccaneers tagging Sean Tucker and adding Kenneth Gainwell could be reason for pessimism. 
Irving isn’t too small. He’s 20 pounds heavier than Reid and within an acceptable range of size, but there will be a public 
referendum on Irving with the perception of size as a question mark. 
Considering the public referendum on RB size could be forthcoming, maybe the better question is whether Reid is the next 
dynamic utility back with big-play value in a situational role? Maybe if we allow a running back to surprise us with his ability 
to remain durable despite his size limitations, we’ll have a grounded approach with the valuation of these archetypes. 
Final thought on this topic: Darren Sproles was an anomaly. 
Reid is a smart runner who has vision, agility, strength, and contact balance to work between the tackles. The likelihood 
that Reid’s body can hold up with a rotational starter’s workload during a 17-week season is another story. This would 
require his body to rebound fast enough to sustain these athletic abilities and concentrated forces on smaller bodies can 
create more havoc. 
As comfortable as Reid is with running gap and zone plays, pressing blocks tight to the line, running through reaches from 
all three levels of defenders, and stalemating linebackers in head-on collisions, it’s likely we’ll see Reid do these things 4-6 
times a week between the tackles and the rest of the time in open space. 
Reid is an effective zone receiver, and he’s developing footwork releases to defeat man coverage and off-man coverage off 
the line or with setups halfway through his stems. Reid has a good catch radius. Other than two different lapses with 
specific types of targets, Reid has reliable hands. 
If a coach is asking Reid to match up one-on-one with box defenders, they’re doing something wrong. Reid is a runner, 
receiver, and return specialist. He has the explosive play-making ability to generate productive weeks as a part of a game 
plan, often on 3-5 plays. 
Reid might have 1-3 seasons where he earns enough touches per week to deliver 1,400-1,500 yards from scrimmage and 
2,000 all-purpose yards, if he’s a perfect match for a scheme and plays to his potential. That’s the unlikely ceiling. Think of 
Reid as a valuable piece to an NFL’s offensive puzzle who is capable of 500-700 yards from scrimmage and 4-6 touchdowns 
in a good year. 
Issue worth noting: 
Reid’s pressure cuts can be a problem for Reid at top speed. The foot working off the plant step must get further in 
front of him and steady on the ground. He has fallen because of this issue as he cut across the face of a safety in 
open space. There was another play in the game where the step was far enough in front, but he was putting his 
weight outside that plant step and slid to the ground trying to bounce outside. Still, he can make hard pressure 
cuts and maintain his footing. 
126
RB Desmond Reid.pre_draft_advice:
If your league awards points for returns, Reid is a mid-round pick. Otherwise, Reid’s best 
shot at weekly fantasy value will come in a “Sproles Role” where he maximizes 8-10 touches per game with big plays and 
50-60 percent of the touches come in the passing game. 
Pre-NFL Draft, this makes Reid a late-round pick for leagues with large rosters and practice squads. Post-draft, Reid’s fit will 
help us determine if he’s a player to monitor from afar or if there’s a legitimate intent to use him in the offense.

RB Rahsul Faison.elevator_pitch:
Faison is a versatile and patient runner who works tight creases and also possesses a wide range of 
footwork to make blocked and unblocked defenders miss and manipulate creases. Faison is quick enough to bounce runs to 
the edge and turn the corner against most defenders in the box. Once he’s in the secondary he forces a prolonged chase 
from defensive backs. 
Faison has effective power and contact balance. He bounces off glancing shots from box defenders and wins direct collisions 
with some second-level defenders and frequently against defensive backs. Defenders must wrap Faison to bring him to the 
ground. 
Faison is a reliable route runner against zone coverage who will work with his quarterback off script. He must prove that he 
can offer more as a route runner or develop this phase of his game. 
Faison has good hands, but he has lost focus with defenders coming downhill and it has led to unforced errors. Faison’s 
blocking techniques have enough craft for him to earn situational playing time, but he needs more precision for him to be 
reliable on every down. His diagnosis of blitzes and twists needs more work. 
If Faison can prove that his acceleration is better than I observed, and his passing-down work improves, he could emerge 
as a productive RB2 in a committee system. 
Vision: Faison identifies interior penetration and finds solutions to avoid the defender. There are runs where he could see it 
a step or two earlier and have a better plan to avoid the defender. 
He’s more effective against edge penetration. 
Faison is decisive as a gap runner, and he’ll press creases when running behind zone blocking. He reads leverage effectively 
as he’s running downhill on Duo and bends away from penetration into the crease. 
Faison is effective at setting up linebackers to the wrong gap and then splitting a double team to earn positive yardage. 
He’s patient with zone runs and will ride the wave in tight quarters to work across his blocks until he presses linebackers 
into one crease to cut back to another. 
Faison is an effective tight crease runner who can get skinny and work through the contact into the second level. 
Elusiveness: Faison has a jump cut that can get him to the next gap or two over from his starting point. Faison can stick 
inside and slide outside the penetration of a defensive end just after making the exchange. 
He uses jump stops to alter his stride length and set up creases in tight traffic at the entrance of a hole. Combines his stick 
with pressure cuts. 
Faison uses a spin to work away from contact in the crease. In short-yardage situations, Faison takes what he can get when 
the creases aren’t well blocked and he’s facing unblocked defenders. 
While I still want to see if Faison can get a 90-degree turn to open his hips, he points his toe to the outside to open his hips 
efficiently enough to bounce plays outside when he has pressed the line within 2-3 steps of his blocks. 
130
RB Rahsul Faison.pre_draft_advice:
Faison is a late-round pick who could earn mid-round value if the NFL gives him better 
draft capital. If he improves fast, he could become a surprise as a situational contributor this year. A more likely expectation 
is waiting 1-2 years for Faison to address his weaknesses, hone his strengths, and fight off the rest of the depth chart. 
131

RB Jamarion Miller.elevator_pitch:
A speedster with questionable acceleration, Miller was slow this year due to a lingering injury, but he 
healed enough for the NFL Combine. Acceleration is one of the two most important physical traits for running backs. 
Acceleration without speed in a running back is like eating soup without a container. You’ll get burned trying to use it, or 
you’ll have to wait too long for it to have the desired effect. 
Miller’s acceleration is passable, but not starter quality. It could make his usage predictable in the NFL if an offensive 
coordinator doesn’t like that he’s being charged with using a less explosive option ahead of one of his favorites. 
Considering that speed is cat nip for coaches, they’ll find ways to give Miller a long enough runway to reach that top speed. 
Even if they fail, they’ll be dogged about trying for at least 1-2 seasons. 
If Miller’s acceleration doesn’t limit his usage, his intelligence as a decision maker and his agility will show up, especially as 
a zone runner where he’ll have more freedom to manipulate blockers and defenders as well as use his power and balance to 
work through traffic. 
Miller’s blocking must become a priority if he’s to earn an active roster spot. His receiving as a check-down option is 
competent but could improve. If the team runs a bigger route tree with its backs, Miller must prove he can do more than 
what Alabama asked of him. 
If Miller can emerge as a reliable special teams option, he could maintain a role on active NFL rosters for 5-7 years as the 
third or fourth back on the depth chart. If he improves where he’s weakest, Mills could earn situational opportunities in the 
future. 
Vision: Miller identifies penetration in the backfield and sets up lanes with small pressure cuts and alterations of footwork. 
He can make two defenders miss off each edge with one efficient cut in the backfield. 
Miller identifies second-level blocks on downhill runs that will take him outside. He also identifies safeties and linebackers 
shooting gaps on downhill runs and presses within two steps of his blocks before taking the hole away from the defenders 
shooting those other gaps. 
As an outside runner, Miller presses the interior gaps within 2-3 steps of his unblocked pursuit to set up the bounce to the 
outermost blocks. 
As a zone runner, Miller reads leverage and makes the smart management decision to take when he can get in short-
yardage situations so he’s not trying to cut back and risk losses. When a crease is tight, Miller attacks and will knock over 
box defenders with glancing shots in the hole and find a soft spot to extend through. 
Miller is an effective Duo runner, finding creases and second-level bounce and cutback lanes. 
Elusiveness: Miller adjusts stride length and pace to set up creases when behind the line of scrimmage. He only needs 1-2 
steps to transition from a perimeter runway to a downhill runway. He has good pressure cuts at full speed to dip inside 
pursuit over the top in the secondary and into the open field. 
133
RB Jamarion Miller.pre_draft_advice:
Miller is a late-round dart-throw in larger formats because he could make a roster as an 
RB3, earn playing time due to injury, and deliver short-term value. If he finds a good fit, he could continue refining his 
game and have a better pro career than his college days. This is unlikely, but possible because of his decision-making, 
quickness, and speed.

RB Coleman Bennett.elevator_pitch:
Bennett is a decisive, tight-crease runner if he spots the anticipated opening and understands the 
defense and play design. When he doesn’t, or the crease is clogged, his decision-making can go to extremes and this will 
lead to worse outcomes in the NFL than it occasionally does in the college game. 
The saving grace for Bennett is that he accounts for game scripts and field position as a decision-maker, which makes him a 
promising game manager that NFL teams want from running backs in the black and red zones. Bennett has efficient and 
dynamic footwork. He also possesses good curvilinear bend and the ability to spot and avoid low shots with high knees and 
feet. 
A former running back, receiver, and return specialist at Bucknell, Bennett has sound hands and good technique with 
attacking the ball. He’s a competent route runner against zone coverage. 
Bennett can be slow to react to pressure. His footwork can get too wide, and he overextends at the point of attack. Still, 
Bennett has enough good reps with sound technique that he isn’t far away from developing precise execution as a blocker 
that can be relied upon against linebackers and safeties when facing them one-on-one. 
Bennett is quick and fast enough to contribute as a pro. He’s also reliable with holding onto the ball. If he can develop more 
precision with pass protection and make more incremental decisions when the initial crease is clogged, Bennett could 
emerge as a team’s RB2 or often used RB3 in a rotation who begins his career on special teams. 
Technical: Played running back and receiver at Bucknell. Also returned kicks at Bucknell. 
Vision: Bennett sets up his puller with a press toward the defender’s leverage and then cuts to the lineman’s leverage on 
gap plays. He identifies penetration and presses toward it when necessary, before cutting back. 
Bennett’s reactions to clogged creases can be too extreme. He bounces or cuts back to the widest possible gap immediately 
rather than riding the wave incrementally to the next crease over. Bennett will go to the next crease, but when that’s 
clogged, he abandons the next incremental possibility to his detriment. 
This tendency has forced Bennett into a second dramatic move to avoid a pursuit angle he could have avoided running into 
in the first place. 
Bennett is a willing tight-crease runner when he sees a push downhill from his linemen through a small opening. He hits the 
crease decisively and with good burst. 
Bennett accounts for the down/distance/field position/game script when running the ball. He’ll try to manage the clock 
ahead of gaining a few extra yards in a situation that calls for it. 
Overall, Bennett is an effective cutback runner and second-level creator who can press the line within a step and find the 
open lane as well as make multiple unblocked defenders miss in succession. 
Elusiveness: Bennett uses pressure cuts and jump cuts in succession to press blocks and navigate creases. He can point 
the outside foot to the boundary to open his hips and earn a 90-degree bounce from the line of scrimmage. 
136
RB Coleman Bennett.pre_draft_advice:
Bennett could be worth a late-round pick in deep leagues with at least 30 roster spots and 
practice squads. For leagues drafting before the NFL Draft, he’s worth adding after the draft to an expanded roster. 
After the NFL Draft, Bennett might have late-round capital that earns him commensurate value for fantasy GMs in these 
large formats.

RB Kejon Owens.elevator_pitch:
Owens won’t have NFL Draft capital, but if comparing him to Adam Randall, who is next on the list, 
Owens offers more usable skills immediately and a safer long-term outlook. 
What Owens lacks is Randall’s size and big program logo. What he has is a confident, tight-crease mentality as a decision-
maker and a quick processor of information while in traffic. 
Owens lacks top speed, but he has acceleration, which is more important. He’ll may not hit the occasional breakaway run, 
but while that runner is getting stuffed on multiple runs and limiting the playbook, Owens is getting positive yards and 
moving the chains. 
Owens is good at adjusting his gait. He can get as good at lifting his feet and knees high in traffic as he is against unblocked 
defenders coming downhill to shoot for him. 
When Owens finishes, he takes on linebackers, bouncing off hits. Owens breaks through reaches and pulls wrapping 
defenders downhill. 
Owens has excellent ball security. His third-down game needs work, but it’s not insurmountable if he can address it soon. 
Owens likely has no draft capital and there’s nothing special about his game to warrant a priority UDFA contract. He is a 
sound player with the ball in his hands and capable of improving on passing downs. If he does, he could become a third or 
fourth RB with a situational role. 
Vision: Owens is decisive into creases a double team opens, especially small creases where penetration takes away options 
for a cutback or bounce. He works tight to the back of his double team to maximize space between himself and the pursuit 
from the outside. He’s a confident small-crease runner when there’s a puller or lead blocker to the primary rushing lane. 
He’ll hit it even if it appears closed. 
Owens identifies penetration into the backfield from run-blitzing safeties. He has the footwork to avoid getting tackled in the 
backfield. 
Owens also reads leverage quick enough to hit a backside lane when defenders compromise the front side or play-side 
lanes. 
Owens presses within a step of his blocks and finds the bounce opportunity. He sets up pullers effectively to work between 
the tackles. He also rides the wave effectively to work incrementally to the backside edge, pressing multiple creases to draw 
defenders downfield while still making his way to the edge. 
Owens knows he lacks top speed so he looks for cutback opportunities in the secondary. There are runs where he does this 
exceedingly well. He’s in the middle of the field on these runs and has a two-way-go. There are also runs where he would be 
better off making the pursuit catch him rather than cutting back into traffic with multiple defenders in pursuit with angles. 
Elusiveness: Owens has a small two-quick adjustment with his footwork to work inside-out to avoid a blitzing defender into 
the backfield. He adjusts gait length to avoid interior penetration as he’s taking the exchange. 
Owens also adjusts his pacing to set up creases. 
139
RB Kejon Owens.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Owens from afar until there’s reason to add him to an expanded summer roster in 
1-2 years, or he earns unexpected NFL Draft capital.

RB Adam Randall.elevator_pitch:
Big and fast for his size, Randall earns buzz for his potential to become an excellent professional. 
Former pros in the NFL Draft media have praised Randall’s studious nature and approach to the game. 
A mature and professional approach to football can get a player far. Randall has some road to travel if he’s going to 
graduate from an RB3/RB4 or practice squad talent to a starter in 2-3 years. 
While Randall has speed, he needs a long runway to build that speed. Some will cite Randall’s size will help him break 
tackles as he makes room on that runway, but without excellent acceleration, he’ll rarely reach top speed with the ball in his 
hands. 
Every great back from Jim Brown to the present has mentioned acceleration as the chief physical characteristic that matters 
for running backs. It’s a form of speed and power combined, and Randall lacks it. 
A former wide receiver, Randall would have been moved to tight end if he had strong receiving skills. He doesn’t. He’s late 
with getting his eyes to the quarterback out of his breaks, and his 3-step breaks on timing routes need 1-2 extra steps. 
Randall has good hands when the ball is on his frame. When forced to extend high or away from his frame, his hands are 
too wide for him to attack the ball with reliability. 
Randall’s size should be an asset against contact. Sometimes it is. His pad level needs work. On some runs, his pads are too 
high, and he gets caught with hits into his frame that he could have run through. On other runs, Randall brings his pads 
over his waist and loses balance before he even reaches the point of attack. 
Randall has adapted well to the decision-making required of a between-the-tackles runner. He’s patient, finds cutback 
opportunities, and reads leverage well. 
Randall could become an effective gap runner soon. With time, he could also do some damage with inside zone. Randall is 
also a capable blocker, but there are a lot of small flaws with his game that needs precise details that are lacking. 
If Randall fixes the blocking improves his routes, and gauges how to use his pads better, he could become a situational 
producer. If his acceleration is worse than I’ve graded, and it’s possible, he might never emerge as a viable option in an NFL 
backfield. 
Presuming that Randall will play quicker as he learns the position better, we could see Randall emerge as a “B” back in a 
committee in 2-3 years. 
Vision: Randall presses within a step of a puller to the puller’s outside shoulder to set up a cutback to the inside. He 
presses with a two-step move downhill after bouncing outside to set up a safety and then bounce outside him. 
Randall identifies cutback opportunities on gap plays, pressing to the play side of the puller, reading the leverage of the 
defender, and accessing the cutback lane. 
Randall can press within a step of the line on an inside run and bounce the play one gap over for a significant gain. 
142
RB Adam Randall.pre_draft_advice:
Randall is worth a stash in deep leagues with practice squads – think at least 30 roster 
spots. If the NFL Draft capital is somehow strong (and absurd), Randall could be worth a pick in the final rounds if he lasts 
that long, just because he’ll get opportunities and it could translate to his learning curve being faster than expected. 
I’m monitoring from afar, but I’m trying to be open to the good possibilities.

RB Dean Connors.elevator_pitch:
Connors is a skilled pass-catcher and space player who has success as a gap runner. He could begin 
his career as a return specialist and work his way into a contributing role on passing downs because of his excellent hands, 
acrobatic skill at the catch point, and athletic ability in the open field. 
As good punt return specialists are, Connors understands when to use efficient and/or dynamic moves to make defenders 
miss while still attacking downhill with aggression. This is also what makes Connors a capable runner behind gap blocking. 
Connors finishes runs like a pro, using his hands, pads, and his legs to work through reaches, wraps, and hits. His ball 
security needs work if he’s going to translate these skills to the NFL. 
Connors must also iron out the flaws as a pass protector. He has a lot of little flaws that are correctable but could hurt him 
if he’s doing something different that gives up pressure on multiple reps during a series or a game. 
Unless Connors proves a quick study from his college tape, or he has been working on the flaws of his game behind the 
scenes, it may take him 1-2 seasons before he can emerge as a situational contributor. If Connors shores up his blocking 
and his ball security, he could earn a situational role soon. 
If Connors gets a little faster and improves his decision making between the tackles, he could emerge as a rotational starter 
with statistical value. 
Conceptual: Although he needs work with sticking with play designs—Connors has the running style, quickness, footwork, 
and aptitude to deliver as a punt return specialist if he can catch punts. 
Vision: Connors reads edge penetration slanting inside to the backfield as he takes the exchange. 
In the open field, Connors will make a defender with combinations of movements, but he also knows when to drop the pads 
and take what’s in front of him. He’ll freeze one defender than then split that defender and the inside pursuit with good pad 
level and determined downhill running. He has a good feel for pursuit and veers away from the flow that pursuit in the open 
field. 
As an inside runner, Connors tends to run to the immediate open space rather than manipulate defenders into blocks as the 
play design dictates. It leads to wasted movement and unnecessary bounces/cutbacks that limit the potential of the design. 
He’s at his best as a gap runner who sets up lead blockers. Lead draw is one of his best plays. 
Elusiveness: Connors has effective curvilinear speed to bend inside a cornerback’s run blitz within 2-3 steps. He has a 
double up to juke edge containment and combines it with a head fake. Connors can follow up these jukes with a spin inside. 
Connors sets up creases and unblocked defenders with change of stride length. He has the quickness to give and take away 
a leg in the crease and make a linebacker miss in traffic. 
Connors has routine success layering moves in succession to defeat defenders in space. His use of the head fake is a central 
component in the combinations that he uses. 
Connors only needs two quick steps to transition from a perimeter approach to a downhill approach to the far-side edge of a 
play. He only needs one step when transitioning at the near-side edge. 
145
RB Dean Connors.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Connors from afar until we see if he earns better draft capital than expected. If he 
has low or no draft capital, as expected, consider Conners if he competes for a return role in leagues that score for returns. 
Otherwise, watch, wait, and add to expanded rosters if there are signs he’s in the mix for a complementary role in 1-2 
years.

RB Chip Trayanum.elevator_pitch:
I profiled Trayanum in 2025, and he did not have a grade worth an NFL roster spot. The former Ohio 
State linebacker-turned-running back spent another year in school. He transferred yet again, this time to Toledo. 
The film at Toledo shows that Trayanum has made progress with position switch. He’s a big back with speed, but that speed 
and acceleration haven’t translated well to the field in the past, because he was still learning to process information fast 
enough to use it. 
While that processing speed isn’t where he needs to be in the NFL, Trayanum has made enough gains between 2024 and 
2025 that there’s reason to project more gains could be possible. Trayanum has become decisive as a gap runner and he’s 
beginning to press creases as a zone runner – even if the depth of these presses isn’t deep enough for the professional 
game. He is now pressing interior gaps on perimeter runs that he didn’t do in previous years. 
You can see the processing bogging down when Trayanum is hopping during his gait while assessing potential creases 
behind the line of scrimmage. Le’Veon Bell would hop behind the line of scrimmage, but this was a ploy to set up a lane or a 
defender. Trayanum is hopping well before he’s even setting up a path. That hop often precedes more tentative behavior. 
Trayanum has the athletic ability to become a contributor in the NFL. The speed, acceleration, hip mobility, and strength are 
all there for him to use as a running back if he can connect these skills with the mental side of the game. 
He has good downhill contact balance, and he’ll drag defenders for extra yardage. Trayanum also gets his feet over low 
shots. The issue is experience with processing information and seeing enough pursuit angles for his mind and body to 
connect faster with footwork and micro-movements that is the invisible thread to fluid and timely reactions. 
This also impacts his pad level. When Trayanum uses his pads, he’s productive against contact. He’s naturally an upright 
runner who doesn’t run with a great bend. This can get him caught with hits while he’s upright, or Trayanum drops his pads 
too low at the point of attack. 
Trayanum is improving as a blocker, but instead of catching contact and getting driven back as he did in previous years, 
he’s lunging into collisions. 
If Trayanum can use some of those linebacker skills for coverage and continue working on his craft, he could emerge as a 
viable role player or committee back with a bigger ceiling than originally thought. 
Conceptual: Arrived at Arizona State as an RB. Played LB initially at OSU and then returned to RB. 
Vision: Trayanum sets up Counter with a press within 1-2 steps of his puller’s back side hips/shoulders. He reads interior 
penetration and works away from it in the backfield. 
Trayanum reads leverage and sets up cutbacks. He does this with zone and gap plays. He also hits creases decisively, 
attacking at full speed downhill. 
When running zone plays, he presses 3-4 steps from blocks/blockers and should press deeper to allow the play to develop. 
There’s some gathering of steps before a decision to change direction at the entrance of a hole, or in the backfield. He’s still 
processing information slowly by the standards of a top prospect at his position. One of the tells that Trayanum is still 
148
RB Chip Trayanum.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Trayanum from afar, but he might be worth adding to a roster in 1-2 years if he 
makes as much progress in the NFL as he did in college.

RB Jaden Nixon.elevator_pitch:
Ashton Jeanty backed up Jaden Nixon in high school. This is fascinating in the same way that Saquon 
Barkley was 188 pounds as a freshman at Penn State. Things can change fast when there’s still a lot of room for growth. 
Jeanty may have surpassed Nixon as a promising runner, but it doesn’t diminish Nixon’s value as a potential NFL 
contributor. Nixon is an explosive runner with elite speed and acceleration. He sees the field well in space, traffic, and 
between the tackles. He sets up runs, mitigates penetration, and understands how to manage his risk based on game script 
and field position. 
Nixon is at his best as in Gap and outside zone schemes, because he can hit a crease fast, build up speed, and outrace 
pursuit. Because he reaches his top speed fast, he’s a straight-line mover in open space because decelerating at his pace is 
tough. He’s a curvilinear mover in space and on the perimeter. Before Nixon begins his acceleration in the hole, he has 
efficient footwork to set up blocks. 
Nixon has a low enough center of gravity to work through reaches and keep his feet moving when wrapped high. The NFL 
will cast Nixon as a scat back who can become part of a rotation. If he proves good enough, Nixon could earn 1-2 situational 
roles as a contributor off the bench. 
Nixon is a better blocker than you’d expect for his size, he’s a sure-handed receiver who adjusts to the ball, and if he earns 
more than check-downs or screens, he might demonstrate more as a route runner. 
Expect Nixon to have a role in the return game and fight for a reserve role that eventually leads to playing time. USF had 
one of the most underrated running back tandems in the country last year. Nixon could prove that he’s a sticky reserve who 
occasional provides on-field value when called upon. 
Vision: Nixon identifies penetration and/or blitzes as he’s approaching the crease and adjusts his gait so he can set up a 
cutback to the open area behind the blitz once he’s through the initial hole. 
In the open field, Nixon is a patient runner who presses blocked and unblocked defenders within 1-2 steps of the pursuit 
before making a cut to avoid them altogether. 
Nixon identifies penetration up the middle as he takes the exchange, and he takes what the play will give him. He doesn’t 
attempt risky cutbacks or reversals of field. 
Nixon works behind his puller on Counter, and he hits the crease at full speed when it’s a sizable hole. 
He identifies favorable leverage on perimeter runs and runs between the tackles. He’s not a creative runner in the open field 
and part of it is how fast he’s moving. It’s difficult to change direction efficiently when running at his top speed. He focuses 
on the speed more than pacing. 
Elusiveness: Nixon has enough curvilinear speed to bend a run downhill from a perimeter approach to the far-side 
numbers. He can also use two steps to transition inside a cornerback after the curvilinear bend and make the corner miss. 
Nixon only needs two steps to transition downhill at the short corner past the box. 
151
RB Jaden Nixon.pre_draft_advice:
In most formats that don’t award points for the return game, Nixon is a player to monitor 
this summer and next if your rosters are between 15-25 in number. Rosters with at least 35 spots could take a shot on 
Nixon when there are expanded offseason rosters, especially if points for returns are given. Otherwise, monitor closely.

RB Kentrel Bullock.elevator_pitch:
Bullock has a low center of gravity and strong contact balance to win against box defenders near the 
line of scrimmage. He sets up gap plays effectively. His decision-making with zone blocking is competent because he gets 
within two steps of traffic when pressing blocks. 
Bullock’s footwork and ability to bend away from pursuit is a strength of his game. He weaves between defenders at a good 
pace, but his speed is not as strong as his initial acceleration and quickness. 
Bullock’s hand position and footwork are weaknesses of his pass protection. If he can address them, he could become 
capable against safeties and linebackers. He’s a competent check-down receiver. 
Bullock’s true value could be special teams. He was on kickoff and punt coverage between his freshman and junior years at 
Ole Miss. 
Vision: Bullock sets up pullers effectively on gap plays, working one shoulder to set up the crease at the opposite shoulder. 
One zone runs, Bullock can press within two-steps of his blockers and hit the cutback lane. 
He reads the leverage of defenders on blocks when running inside zone. He’s decisive about hitting tight creases on inside 
zone. 
Bullock identifies interior penetration and work around it. He takes what’s available when there are no viable cutbacks or 
bounces or field position and/or down and distance dictates to avoid risk. 
He’ll find cutbacks on gap plays. 
Elusiveness: Bullock has enough curvilinear speed and bend to weave around box defenders into the secondary on gap 
plays up the middle. He only needs two steps to transition downhill from an approach to the edge of the box outside the 
tackle. He can use a jump stop, which is just one step. 
Bullock gets his feet over defenders and teammates on the ground while working through a crease. 
Bullock spins through reaches to his frame. He sidesteps shooting defenders while in creases. 
Bullock adjusts his stride and pacing to set up pullers or bounces on zone plays. He opens his hips efficiently so he can 
bounce outside. 
Acceleration: Bullock has enough acceleration to weave between box defenders and reach the secondary on runs off guard 
on gap plays up the middle. He can beat box defenders to the short-side edge on a designed perimeter run. 
Speed: Bullock has enough speed to maintain separation against defensive backs if he reaches his top speed in an open 
area. In these scenarios he can flip the field. 
Power: Bullock initiates contact to finish runs against safeties. He keeps his feet moving through lower-body wraps. Bullock 
has good pad level in tight creases on short-yardage plays. 
Bullock can pull through reaches to his upper body from linebackers in the crease and the flat. He uses a stiff arm to finish 
the interaction, warding off the defender’s second effort. 
154

RB Jonah Coleman.elevator_pitch:
Coleman is a nimble back with a low center of gravity. He’s a patient enough zone runner, reads 
leverage well, and finds lanes to bounce or cutback. 
Coleman also manages the game well as a ballcarrier, taking what the defense gives him and limiting his riskier decisions to 
down-and-distance and game-script scenarios that are open to creative license. He’s not as patient of a runner with Duo 
blocking as he is with zone. 
Once Coleman is past the line of scrimmage, he must leverage his size and get downhill. He’s prone to bouncing plays 
against outside pursuit slanting inside, thinking he’ll find openings against the grain of the defense’s flow. Coleman is 
neither that explosive nor agile enough for this ploy. He’s big and he should be sticking his foot in the ground and forcing 
the pursuit to hit him from angles that won’t bring Coleman to the ground as easily. 
Coleman has enough acceleration and speed to reach the open field, but linebackers routinely catch Coleman. What would 
be a field-flipping gain for many backs is a chain-moving gain for Coleman. What would be a breakaway run of 70-80 yards 
for his peers is a field-flipper for 35-45 yards for Coleman. 
If Coleman can prove more explosive from what I have observed, he could be worth the pre-draft buzz I’ve seen from him 
since last summer. Based on what I’ve seen, Coleman’s burst and speed are befitting an NFL’s RB3 or RB4. 
Combine this with pass protection that lacks sound technique at the point of attack and power and contact balance that’s 
contributor quality, and Coleman’s acclaim doesn’t match his film at this point. He’s a reserve runner who will get what’s 
earned and protect the ball who might develop his passing-down skills to become useful on the field. 
Vision: Coleman is a patient runner who works within 1-2 steps of his blocks. He presses advantageous leverage for the 
defender to set up the block to the favorable side for the blocker. He’s willing to press within a step of a defender in his face 
to set up a block. 
Coleman must do this more often with Duo. He abandons the press too soon and it doesn’t allow his other combo block to 
set before bounces or cuts back toward it. He leaves yards on the field in this situation. Overall, Coleman is a decisive Duo 
runner who reads interior penetration and finds the bounce or cutback lane. 
Coleman rides the wave well on zone runs, incrementally working his way inside-out to keep pursuit honest with their 
angles as he goes along his path. 
When Coleman reaches the flat, he tries to bounce outside flat defenders slanting inside, rather than sticking his foot 
downhill and having the leverage advantage to run through contact or even accelerate past it. In this respect, he can 
improve his decision-making with bounce-outs. 
Coleman reads leverage and penetration between the tackles and finds the bounce lane. He’s effective at setting up double 
teams on inside zone and hits the crease with confidence. He’s effective working with his pullers on sweeps—reading the 
heads of the defenders positioned against the blockers. 
159
RB Jonah Coleman.pre_draft_advice: Monitor Coleman from afar despite your peers considering Coleman a draftable player.

RB Freddie Brock IV.elevator_pitch:
Brock is a shifty runner with decision making that’s a good blend of decisive and patient. He can 
operate in zone or gap schemes. If he reaches the secondary, he can hold off defensive backs, but that best way for this to 
happen is with inside runs. 
Brock lacks the explosion of most prospects. His pass protection has potential if he can earn better position at the point of 
attack because he oversets and overextends. 
If Brock can sharpen his third-down game and show decent short-range explosion, he could earn a reserve role, especially if 
he can play special teams. 
Vision: Brock identifies penetration from interior defenders working across his intended rushing lane and adjusts his stride 
and pace to let past him before entering the crease. He can handle unanticipated obstacles and find solutions. 
He’s decisive into creases, but patient enough to press within a step of blockers or defenders to set up bounces or cutbacks. 
Brock has a good feel for pursuit in the open field and veers away from that pursuit effectively. 
Brock can run gap or zone plays. He reads leverage well enough on gap plays to press just long enough for the pullers to 
engage and then find the cutback lane if he can’t stick to the play side. 
Elusiveness: Brock adjusts his stride length and pacing to set up creases. Brock has effective pressure cuts to change 
direction at the line of scrimmage and get into the cutback lane. 
Brock only needs two steps to transition downhill from an approach to the far side flat. He has a sudden stick-and-spin 
combo that can beat a pursuing linebacker in the hole and lead to a cutback or bounce to the perimeter. Brock has a good 
stutter step to set up safeties over the top and beat them in the second level. 
Acceleration: Brock can accelerate past most defensive linemen, but cannot reach the far-side flat beyond linebacker 
depth without an inside linebacker running him down in the second level. He can beat a middle linebacker to the short-side 
flat and boundary. 
Speed: When Brock reaches the open field, he can outrun the pursuit of a middle linebacker into the secondary. He can 
beat a cornerback over the top of him to the near sideline and hold the defender off for 15-20 yards if he has a runway to 
reach his top speed. He will veer away from pursuit to open space and hold off cornerbacks for 30-40 yards. 
Power: Brock pulls through reaches to his upper body and waist, but has more difficulty pulling through reaches to his 
lower body that should result in a lot more big gains on his film. When he lifts his knees and feet high enough, he breaks 
these tackle attempts, but he must do it more often. 
Brock can push a pile and win collisions, but he’d win more of them if he dropped his pads into collisions with linebackers 
and defensive tackles. He can get caught with his pads too high, and while he wins the collisions with a push, he could be 
running over some of these defenders with better pad level. His stiff arm is effective enough to work through reaches to his 
frame as he’s running to the edge of the box. 
162

Jay Henderson.elevator_pitch:
Henderson has starter size and enough explosion to threaten a secondary when he earns a long 
enough runway. Put him in a gap scheme with some inside zone sprinkled in, and Henderson can find cutbacks that earn 
him that runway on occasion. 
He has good hip mobility, curvilinear bend, and confidence to hit tight creases and take what the defense gives. This 
combination gets him into creases. From there, Henderson finishes well enough to generate yards after contact. 
Henderson’s third-down game needs the most work. His hands also have messy technique with specific target trajectories. 
Blocking and routes are the areas that could hold him back from emerging as a candidate for an RB3/RB4 spot on a depth 
chart. 
Vision: Henderson reads leverage on gap plays and presses the line to set up his puller. When there’s penetration to the 
play side, he can find the cutback lane on a gap play. 
Henderson presses the double team within a step on inside zone, reads the leverage and finds the bounce or cutback. He 
identifies interior penetration into the backfield. 
Henderson feels the soft spots of the box when in heavy traffic and he’ll slide to that area and generate a strong push for 3-
4 yards more. He’s also a confident tight-crease runner who will take what’s there for him and not attempt to create where 
the gaps are filled. 
Elusiveness: Henderson can flip his hips for efficient bounces away from interior penetration. He has curvilinear 
movement to bend downhill from an E-W path. Henderson also has effective pressure cuts. 
Henderson alters his stride length and pacing to set up blocks. He gets his feet over low shots in the crease. He can layer 
moves in succession. 
Acceleration: He can threaten safeties with his acceleration. 
Speed: Henderson can beat some cornerbacks if he earns the runway. 
Power: Henderson initiates contact and uses his pads effectively. He keeps his feet moving when wrapped high. 
Henderson’s stiff arm can ward off a linebacker in the flat as he works toward the boundary. 
Henderson can finish as a pile pusher in the box – dragging or pushing linemen wrapping him or pushing opposite. 
Direct Contact Balance: Henderson can stalemate linebackers meeting him head-on in the crease. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Henderson can initiate hard contact with a defensive end in the crease and bounce off a 
glancing shot to his pads. Henderson has a capable balance-touch to work through cuts to his legs and at least earn another 
yard falling forward. 
Routes: He’s not going to run away from linebackers when they play Henderson man-to-man. 
164

RB Seth McGowan.elevator_pitch:
I’m betting you’ll see McGowan with a higher ranking elsewhere simply based on his size and timed 
speed. Fans of McGowan will be rooting for a redemption arc with McGowan. He arrived at Oklahoma as a 4-star recruit, and 
eventually served time for beating up an individual after a pot deal went awry. 
McGowan is a well-built back with straight-line speed who needs a big runway to reach that speed. If he can prove that his 
acceleration is better than it appears, McGowan could contribute to an offense that runs gap plays. 
McGowan has lapses with seeking cutbacks in situations where leverage his size with a downhill track is the better choice. 
McGowan’s cut-back ability is efficient, but the issue is his choice to do so. 
McGowan’s agility is good enough to contribute in a gap scheme, but he’ll struggle in a high-end contributing role if asked to 
run a zone scheme, because his footwork lacks the precision and balance for the baseline creativity needed there. 
McGowan’s power and contact balance is not as good as his size might lead people to believe. He can win against some 
linebackers, but most of his success against collisions comes against defensive backs. McGowan will drag box defenders and 
generate a push, but he lacks top-end power. 
McGowan could become a versatile pass protector and check-down receiver if he can clean up a myriad of fine details with 
positions, angles, and hand usage. 
McGowan could be a nice fit as a backup to a power running game who could eventually deliver some situational production 
or earn a rotational role as a first-call substitute for the starter. 
Vision: McGowan follows his pullers to the edge of the box and makes a timely transition downhill as his lead blockers 
engage. He’ll stick tight to his down blocks as he works downhill. 
McGowan adjusts his stride length, identifies penetration up the middle, and presses creases within 1-2 steps of the 
penetration to set up a cutback or bounce. 
McGowan is effective at making the first defender miss in the backfield – whether it’s edge or interior penetration. He’s 
creative enough with his feet to find solutions to unplanned obstacles. 
There are interior runs where he reads the leverage correctly in theory, but would get more yardage sticking with the design 
against the suboptimal leverage than attempting the cutback. Matching up one-on-one with a DB that’s partially blocked is a 
better choice than cutting back into an unblocked defensive tackle. 
Elusiveness: McGown can transition downhill to the short flat on a perimeter run with two-quick steps. When working to 
the edge of the box, he only needs one transition step to get downhill. When working to the far-side flat on a perimeter run, 
he needs three steps. 
McGowan has an effective head fake when heading downhill and setting up a safety. He delivers a sharp stick to set up 
pressure cuts. He uses a double up on approach to the line of scrimmage to set up a defensive tackle’s shot into the 
backfield before bouncing outside. 
He delivers a two-quick juke with a head fake against the high safety to set up a lane outside the defender. 
166
RB Seth McGowan.pre_draft_advice:
A late-round consideration in leagues with over 35 rosters spots and a practice squad, or 
for expanded offseason rosters until GMs can learn McGowan’s roster status by late summer-early fall.

RB Anthony Hankerson.elevator_pitch:
Hankerson fits the Kyren Williams-Devonta Freeman mold of runner. He has a low center of gravity, 
he makes intelligent decisions between the tackles, and he’s just creative enough to get more than what’s blocked for him. 
Hankerson’s speed needs a longer runway to reach its top gear than what might prove viable in the NFL. Unlike Williams 
and Freeman, Williams lacks the play strength and contact balance of these starters. 
If there’s a chance for Hankerson to prove otherwise, winning a special teams role will be the first step. 
Vision: Hankerson sets up cutbacks on Duo with presses within 2-3 steps of his double teams. He reads the leverage of 
interior defenders during the exchange and can work away from interior penetration. 
Elusiveness: When he can keep his feet, Hankerson can transition downhill from a perimeter approach with two steps. 
Hankerson adjusts his stride and pacing to set up creases. He uses his spins to work through reaches to his upper body. 
Hankerson can point his toe and open his hips with economy when in traffic or approaching the line of scrimmage out of the 
exchange. Hankerson gets his feet over low shots in open space. 
Acceleration: Hankerson can beat some LBs and most DLs on designed perimeter runs. 
Speed: When Hankerson reaches a wide-open secondary untouched, he can hold off DBs from the other side of the field for 
60-70 yards. 
Power: Handerson pulls through reaches of smaller off-ball linebackers. 
Direct Contact Balance: He can win direct collisions with safeties and linebackers in the crease. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Hankerson bounces off indirect shots to his frame from linebackers in the crease and safeties 
with runways in the second level. 
When he can initiate with his pads, he can drop a defensive end coming from the inside. 
Routes: Hankerson will reduce the inside shoulder on the linebacker covering him on a wheel route. He could follow up with 
a rip to earn better separation. 
Hankerson identifies the second-level defender, works to depth, and settles into the open area. He presents a friendly target 
to the quarterback. 
Hankerson has an effective swat-swim against man coverage playing over the top to get inside. He sets up the hands with a 
two quick that could have more exaggerated movement. 
Receiving: Hankerson tracks the ball over his shoulder. He has lapses of focus where he turns his head downfield before 
securing the ball. This leads to him fighting the ball. 
Hankerson can win the ball after a focus lapse and while taking contact to his back and chest from tight coverage. 
169

RB Jay Ducker.elevator_pitch:
Ducker is a cutback runner who attacks tight creases with discipline. He sets up defenders and blocks 
with effective footwork, and he has efficient moves to work round or over contact. 
Get Ducker into the open field, and he has skill to layer moves in succession to make multiple defenders miss. Ducker will 
reach the secondary when he has a downhill runway and he can hold off pursuit for 30-40 yards because he has strong play 
stamina. 
Ducker has some kinks to work out as a blocker, but they aren’t insurmountable. He’s a competent pass-catcher for leaks 
and screens. When the ball is in his hand, Ducker isn’t giving it up – his ball security track record is impressive. 
If Ducker has enough skill to work on special teams, he might hang onto an RB3/RB4 role. 
Vision: Ducker can set up cutbacks on inside zone with presses within a step of his play-side blocks. He identifies edge 
penetration at the edge of the crease and sidesteps the pursuit. 
Ducker has shown that he can be an aggressive tight-crease runner. 
Elusiveness: Ducker adjusts his stride length to wait for his blocks to set up. Ducker can bend runs outside with a flip of 
his hips. He can also point his toe to the boundary. 
Ducker can spin away from contact in the open field. He gets his feet over low shots. He’s good at layering moves in 
succession in the open field. 
Ducker only needs two steps to transition from a perimeter track to a downhill track. 
Acceleration: Ducker has the burst to beat a safety in the box to the short sideline and turn the corner on him. He can do 
the same to the far-side boundary. 
Speed: Ducker can flip the field and hold off a safety to the near sideline for 35-45 yards. He can hold off corners and 
safeties for 30-40 yards. 
Power: Ducker can drag box defenders wrapping him high. He uses his free arm to ward off reaches with a shed or a wipe. 
Ducker also uses a stiff arm to ward off reaches and get his feet and knees high over reaches as he does it. 
Direct Contact Balance: Ducker will drop his pads into a safety at the end of a crease, initiating the contact, but he turns 
his frame so he’s using one shoulder and slows his pace rather than accelerating through the contact. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Ducker bounces off glancing shots from off-ball linebackers and undersized defensive ends. 
Routes: Ducker uses a wipe to counter the hands of an off-coverage defender. On vertical routes he’ll work to the open 
area and wait to show his eyes only after passing the buzz defender. He will tempo his breaks on vertical routes. 
Receiving: Ducker can turn behind his break path and catch the ball at helmet height with overhand position as he turns 
into an oncoming defender and take a hit and wrap. He attacks with underhand position wide from his frame and tracks 
over his shoulder. 
171

RB Dominic Richardson.elevator_pitch:
A physical runner who fits well in the run schemes that more NFL teams are adopting, Richardson is a 
physical runner with just enough shiftiness to thrive on a steady diet of off-tackle zone, inside zone, and gap blocking. 
Despite limited speed and acceleration, Richardson can extend downhill runs between the tackles because of his contact 
balance, cutback ability and shiftiness in open space. 
Richardson has soft hands and good body control. He must prove he can run an expanded route tree from the backfield to 
become something more than a screen-leak-flats option. If he can, and he develops more initial explosion with his game, 
Richardson could become a situational contributor off the bench who could also wear down a defense if given enough carries 
as a substitute starter. 
We’re likely getting ahead of ourselves on that upside, but Bam Knight is still in the league and has shown all of what I 
described about Richardson. 
Vision: Richardson identifies interior penetration as he begins his press toward the line of scrimmage and executes a quick 
cutback. When there is no penetration, Richardson presses within a step of his blocks at the line to set up his cutbacks. 
Richardson is 1-2 steps late to make meaningful reads of leverage and penetration off the edge on the play side of off-
tackle or perimeter runs. If he could make the assessment during the exchange and decelerate within the first two steps 
after the exchange, Richardson could mitigate losses or earn bigger gains with an immediate cutback downhill. 
In the open field, Richardson is shifty enough to use his footwork to alter his path just as pursuit that has been giving chase 
closes on him. This can extend a run another 15-20 yards. 
Richardson is patient setting up pullers on gap plays. 
Elusiveness: Richardson can transition downhill at the opposite guard on a short perimeter approach within two steps, but 
he must learn to identify this opportunity 1-2 steps earlier. 
Richardson only needs two quick steps to transition from a perimeter off-tackle to downhill and this undercuts the pursuit of 
a linebacker working east-west. He also can work to the play-side numbers and transition downhill with only two steps. 
When cutting downhill on an off-tackle play, Richardson gets his knees and feet high through the initial crease. 
After making a spinning high-point catch, Richardson can return to earth and execute an immediate jump cut to try to avoid 
the flat linebacker. The range of motion with the jump cut isn’t wide enough but the movement mitigates the amount of 
contact from the defender. 
Richardson alters stride length and pacing to set up creases. He has an effective spin in the crease to work away from traffic 
to get downhill. 
Richardson opens his hips behind the line of scrimmage after pressing tight by pointing his foot to the boundary. He has 
good curvilinear speed and bend on perimeter runs around far-side and near-side edges. 
Acceleration: A defensive tackle on the play side of a perimeter run can run down Richardson before he turns the corner at 
the boundary. Richardson can outrun box defenders on open creases to the near-side numbers. 
173

RB Ky Thomas.elevator_pitch:
Thomas earned MVP of Guaranteed Rate Bowl in 2021 at Minnesota while working in a committee with 
Bucky Irving, Thomas is a decisive inside runner with has facility with inside zone and gap plays. 
Thomas moves well in traffic and in the open field. He’s both efficient and dynamic with his footwork, and he’s a powerful 
runner who can work through multiple points of contact. 
Thomas’s speed is competent, but not top end. He has positive moments as a pass protector, but he overextends too often 
and this can hurt him. 
If Thomas can get more explosive and improve his blocking, he could make an active roster as a reserve who could 
contribute. 
Vision: Thomas is decisive into the open crease on inside zone runs. When he faces penetration between the tackles, 
Thomas can slide outside laterally from the downhill penetrator. 
He reads leverage effectively on perimeter runs, knowing when to cut downhill. With Duo, Thomas will take what’s in front 
of him and push through contact in tight creases to maximize what’s available. 
He works ahead of down blocks and sets up pullers on Power. If there’s an obvious cutback, Thomas will take it. With zone 
plays, Thomas will work within 1-2 steps of the line or unblocked defender before making a bounce or cutback. 
Elusiveness: Thomas spins off hard glancing shots. He has sharp enough pressure cuts to work away from penetration off 
the edge and up the middle. 
Between the tackles, Thomas can transition east-west to north-south with two quick steps to cut inside the penetrating 
linebacker shooting a gap. He only needs two quick steps to transition at the edge of the tackle box. 
He’ll also use an efficient jump cut or jump stop to avoid pursuit in traffic. He adjusts his stride length and pace. He also 
layers moves in succession to work past multiple points of pursuit – jump cuts, spins, and sticks. 
Thomas can open his hips with the point of his toe to the boundary and bounce from penetration. 
Acceleration: Thomas has enough downhill acceleration to beat box defenders and box safeties through a crease into the 
second level. He beats box defenders to the far side and near side edges on perimeter runs and bounce-outs. 
Speed: Thomas lacks top-end speed in the open field, but he can hold off safeties and cornerbacks for 25-40 yards. 
Power: Thomas has the lower-body strength to keep his feet when multiple box defenders wrap him high. He runs through 
lower-body wraps from defensive backs at the second level. He kicks his feet up and gets his knees high to work through 
reaches to his legs from box defenders. 
Thomas pulls through multiple reaches from box defenders and safeties in the crease and at the second level. When 
wrapped high, he keeps his feet moving. 
Thomas uses a stiff-arm to chop away the reach of a defender. He has an active stiff-arm as a runner. In the open field, 
Thomas attacks defenders as a finisher. 
176

RB Jaydn Ott.elevator_pitch:
Ott is a good runner in space who catches the ball well enough on check-downs and screens to utilize 
his speed. He must improve his hands and routes to become a dynamic receiver in the NFL. 
This will be important, because Ott does his best work in space. He has sharp, efficient cuts and changes direction well 
enough to create separation from unblocked defenders. 
Between the tackles, Ott has some clear strengths and weaknesses. When running against light boxes, he’s patient, reads 
leverage well, and takes what the defense gives him. When running against heavier boxes, Ott makes ill-advised cutbacks 
and bounces. 
Ott is a competent blocker in chip-release, or scenarios where he’s double-teaming, handling a DB or off-ball LB one-on-one 
before leaking to the flat. He could compete for a third-down role in training camp, especially if he pushes hard for a special 
teams role as coverage or as a return specialist. 
Vision: Ott reads leverage on inside zone and sets up his full back and double team blocks. He identifies penetration into 
the backfield and finds efficient solutions. He’s patient with this concept and will take the tight crease and generate a push. 
Ott presses within 1-2 steps of the line of scrimmage to set up creases. He uses the double-up effectively to set up 
penetration before sliding inside the push into the backfield and into the open crease. 
Ott takes what he can get on gap plays that get foiled early. He doesn’t attempt unnecessary cutbacks or bounces away 
from the design when the boxes are light (6 defenders or less). When boxes are heavier (7 or more), he look for daylight 
with cutbacks that are ill-advised rather than following the play design that would have earned more yards with less 
physical exertion. He also tries these cutbacks deep in his own territory in the black zone. 
He’s better at reading leverage against lighter boxes when running Power or Trap. 
Elusiveness: Ott adjusts his stride length to set up lead blocks as well as make multiple penetrators into the backfield 
miss. He’s light on his feet and possesses sharp pressure cuts. 
Ott uses spins after contact to extend forward through wraps after the initial collision. He opens his hips with the point of 
the toe and can bounce or cutback with economy. 
Ott only needs two small and quick steps to transition downhill from a perimeter approach. Ott can spin off that two-step 
transition 
Ott can jump stop and jump cut in succession to avoid penetration and slide into an adjacent rushing lane. 
Acceleration: Ott has enough acceleration to beat a linebacker up the short-side boundary and accelerate past a safety’s 
angle of pursuit. This is also the case with runs to the far-side perimeter. He possesses enough stop-start quickness to 
avoid penetration. 
Speed: Ott can threaten a secondary because he can get angles on safeties and hold off some cornerbacks. Get Ott in 
space, and he can flip the field and even author a breakaway run. 
Power: Ott has effective pad level that helps him get skinny through tight creases that Duo plays often produce. This helps 
him earn a forward lean through wraps from box defenders. 
178

RB Eli Sanders.elevator_pitch:
The RSP has become known for finding skill-talent that, to the general football public, seemingly came 
out of nowhere. There are a handful of backs profiled earlier that could eventually fit this characterization if the conditions 
are right. 
Let’s flip this idea on its head. If there is a player who could seemingly come out of nowhere from the perspective of the 
RSP – a player ranked near the bottom of the publication’s board – Sanders is a fun candidate. 
An explosive gap runner who has competent zone-running skills, Sanders has breakaway speed, strong acceleration, and 
the footwork to create spaces for liftoff. 
Sanders lacks significant power, but he runs with good pad level and breaks tackles when he generates momentum. 
Because he gets his feet high at the point of attack from low shots, he avoids wrap-ups that drop less agile backs. 
Sanders has room for additional development on third downs. He’s a basic leak receiver with capable hands with screens, 
wide routes, and check-downs, but hasn’t shown much more as a route runner. 
Sanders IDs blitzes but can be slow to reach the spot to deliver a well-positioned block. When he does, he has shown 
promise. 
Sanders is rehabbing a knee injury and could be a year away from competing for a roster spot. Earning a roster spot in this 
situation is a tough initial hurdle for a late-round/UDFA talent to clear, but the NFL covets speed and Sanders has that 
working in his favor. 
Vision: In the open field, Sanders presses creases within 2-3 steps of his blockers before executing a cutback. He’ll also use 
a juke of 2-3 steps to press inside-out on unblocked defenders in the open field. 
Sanders identifies penetration into the backfield and can work past the contact – edge and interior penetration. 
Sanders displays enough patience with pullers to adjust his pacing to set up the block before accelerating past the blocker. 
Sanders hits creases that are tight or non-existent when there’s no wise alternative. 
Elusiveness: Sanders needs two steps to transition downhill from perimeter approach on a screen to the flat and when 
running off-tackle zone to the edge of the box at the far-side flat. He needs three steps to the far-side flat at full speed. 
Sanders varies his stride length as jukes to set up open field pursuit and makes them miss. He gets his feet high enough to 
work through contact from low shots. He has an effective double up to vary his stride length and can follow up with a jump 
cut to work across the back of a lead blocker. 
Sanders also alters his pacing to set up blocks behind the line of scrimmage. He spins off contact or through wraps. Sanders 
opens his hips at the line of scrimmage to set up bounces by pointing his toe to the boundary. 
Acceleration: Sanders has enough acceleration to reach the short-side boundary on a design run to the perimeter and turn 
the corner. Sanders has an effective jump cut in the open field. 
181
RB Eli Sanders.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar. If Sanders earns a nice UDFA contract and the phrase “red-shirt year,” 
is invoked, it will be the first layer of optimistic information fantasy GMs need to at least keep Sanders in their memory 
banks for another year or two.

RB OJ Arnold.elevator_pitch:
Arnold is a capable tight-crease runner on gap plays, who can find the cutback lanes. He has efficient 
footwork and knows how to set up creases with his movement, and when he needs a dynamic solution, he has explosive 
lateral cuts. 
Arnold has adequate third-down skills for the NFL that can improve if he learns to use his hands at the point of attack as a 
blocker and more efficiently as a receiver. What might prove too difficult of a fix is his athletic ability. 
If Arnold has reached the apex of his burst and speed, he’ll be deemed too slow to play in the NFL. There’s a glut of backs 
on the bench who possess more explosion as athletes and are more skilled and experienced with the techniques Arnold is 
still developing. 
Vision: Arnold presses within a step of the line on gap plays to set up his bounce to the edge on Power. He identifies 
penetration into the backfield, and he’s poised about working past it when he has an angle. 
Arnold reads the leverage of defenders on blockers and can slide across the formation to the open gap. He’s a capable tight-
crease runner against gap plays who also finds cutback lanes when necessary. 
Elusiveness: Arnold can transition downhill from a perimeter approach with two steps. He can use a two-step juke to set 
up a linebacker into a block and slide to the open crease. 
Arnold uses a variety of stride variations to set up blocks on gap runs. He’ll use a jump stop to set up pulling linemen. He 
can deliver spins off jump stops. He also executes jump cuts off jump stops. 
Acceleration: Arnold accelerates past box defenders on runs to the short-side edge and gets around the corner. He forces 
safeties to chase him down. He can reverse field and reach the short-side edge. 
Speed: Arnold can get through the box into the secondary, but the situation must be ideal for him to flip the field and/or 
earn a breakaway run. 
Power: Arnold finishes with low pad level against unblocked defenders meeting him downhill. Arnold runs through reaches 
high to his frame from defensive linemen. He pulls through reaches from defensive backs. He finishes with low pads to get 
under defenders blocking his rushing lane. 
Direct Contact Balance: Arnold can drop his pads and knock a defensive end backward when he gets to the other side of 
the crease. If there are more reps like this, upgrade him. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Arnold runs through glancing shots to his shoulder from safeties and linebackers at the end of 
a crease whether he initiates the contact or the defender does. 
Routes: Arnold identifies second-level defenders, works to depth, and settles under the defender into open space, 
presenting a friendly target to the quarterback. Arnold will also tempo his breaks across zones. 
Receiving: Arnold can be prone to clap-attacking targets arriving behind his break path at his back hip. His hands are wide 
when using overhand position. 
Arnold tracks targets over his shoulder with underhand position without leaving his feet unnecessarily. 
183

RB C.J. Donaldson.elevator_pitch:
A former tight end converted to running back, Donaldson is a one-speed, power runner who lacks 
notable acceleration. Give him one crease to hit, and he’ll create. Feed him the ball in the underneath zones, and he’ll make 
the catch. Ask Donaldson to create when what’s supposed to be blocked for him isn’t there, and he’ll find solutions, but 
don’t expect big gains. 
Donaldson needs a season or two to improve his pass protection. If he can do that, he could fit that Malcolm Brown-Darrel 
Williams role of a big, short-yardage pounder who can also serve as a passing-down, two-minute receiver from the 
backfield. 
The key for Donaldson will be special teams. If he can contribute in this capacity, he could make a roster and springboard 
his career as a reserve-contributor with a situational role from there. 
Vision: Donaldson presses within a step of his down blockers to set up pullers on gap plays. He also presses within a step 
of a double team on Duo or Inside Zone to set up a cutback lane. Donaldson reads the leverage of first- and second-level 
defenders to set up his presses and cutbacks. 
When working off tackle or the perimeter, Donaldson presses inside gaps to set up the perimeter blocks. He does this within 
a step of his blockers. 
He attacks creases with a blend of patience and decisiveness. Donaldson reads penetration and has the footwork control 
and vision to weave around interior pushes and return to the original design of the scheme. 
Donaldson has the peripheral vision to account for slanting linemen arriving outside from the second level while he’s 
negotiating traffic in a crease. 
In short-yardage situations, Donaldson attacks tight creases and pushes for what he can earn from the blocks. 
Elusiveness: Donaldson can point the toe to open his hips efficiently. He’ll spin off contact or to avoid contact slanting 
toward him from the outside. 
Donaldson adjusts his stride length to set up creases. When working to the flats with a perimeter track, he only needs two 
steps to transition from that perimeter runway to a downhill runway. 
Donaldson has an effective stick to set up a downhill run. Jump cuts aren’t a big part of his repertoire but when there’s a 
need, he can make a small jump cut to transition away from the designed crease to the one next to it. 
Acceleration: I haven’t seen the acceleration of an NFL starter or high-end contributor – especially on designed runs to the 
perimeter. 
Speed: Donaldson has enough to speed to reach the secondary with a good runway.
