Power: Donaldson keeps his feet moving through contact and can drag box defenders forward or push them backward. He 
can stand up a pair of defensive linemen in the crease who are unblocked and stalemate them. 
Donaldson can drag linebackers 3-5 yards downfield when they wrap him high. He lowers his pads into contact and attacks 
with them. 
185

RB Shomari Lawrence.elevator_pitch:
If Lawrence can improve his ball security to a commensurate level with an NFL starter, he has enough 
skills to earn a reserve role. At this point, Lawrence coughs up the ball every 3-4 games, depending on the workload. That’s 
an unacceptable NFL rate. 
Otherwise, Lawrence is an intelligent decision-maker who reads line of scrimmage effectively, has efficient and dynamic 
movement, and he’s quick and fast enough to beat box defenders and sometimes all three levels of the defense depending 
on the play design. 
Lawrence has competent power and good contact balance, especially when he attacks first. He doesn’t offer much as a 
third-down back. Blocking is his current weak area because his positioning at the collision point has flaws. 
Lawrence can catch, but he has some work to do if he’s going to become reliable enough to handle less accurate but 
catchable targets. His route running is basic and even so, he’s not creating friendly targets on routes setting under zone 
defenders. 
Lawrence is a pure runner. If his skills with the complementary roles of the job improve, he might make a roster and work 
his way up a depth chart and into a reserve role as a situational contributor. 
Vision: Lawrence identifies interior and edge penetration into the backfield and veers away from it after the exchange. He 
reads the leverage of defenders on Man and Gap plays and works off his lead blocks. He’ll also find the rare cutback on a 
gap play. When there’s penetration into the backfield, Lawrence doesn’t attempt risky bounces or cutbacks based on the 
position of the defenders and/or the field position of the play. 
Elusiveness: Lawrence only needs two quick steps on a Toss play to avoid penetration off the edge and transition from a 
sideline path to a downhill path into the hole. 
Lawerence adjusts his stride length to set up lead blocks. He can hurdle low shots in the open field. He has effective pace 
variation in the backfield to identify defender leverage and work away from it with sharp pressure cuts. 
He has an effective spin at the line of scrimmage to work across 1-2 gaps and to an open crease. 
Lawrence gets his feet over low shots at the edge of creases or through a crease. 
Acceleration: Lawrence’s acceleration is good enough to beat box defenders into the secondary when he has a crease 
downhill or around the edge of the box to the near-side boundary. He can beat some box cornerbacks around the near-side 
edge on a perimeter run. 
Lawrence has the acceleration to beat all three levels of defenders around the far-side edge and turn the corner on 
defensive backs. 
Speed: Lawrence can turn the corner on defensive backs when working to the far-side edge and he’ll hold them off in the 
secondary. 
Power: Lawrence has effective placement with his stiff arm in the open field, landing it in the chest of the safety and 
warding off contact to his frame. 
190

WR Eli Heidenreich.elevator_pitch:
Heidenreich has special teams promise as a return specialist and/or coverage option who can 
contribute to an offense as a fourth or fifth receiver but more likely as a utility player who earns as many touches as a 
running back as he does a WR3-WR5. 
Heidenreich has the ball-carrying skills on the perimeter and the open field to deliver as an extension of the ground game 
from a variety of spots. He tracks, adjusts, and catches the ball effectively in the capacity of a zone receiver, an outlet from 
the backfield, or on schemed plays up the seam or boundary for big plays. 
For Heidenreich to become a wide receiver, he must develop a complete route game, and he’s not as far from that 
possibility as his role with Navy might lead you to believe. He has developed techniques for releases, setups, and breaks, 
but he must expand the precision of their execution beyond the shallow range of the field. 
Developing these skills may prove easier than trying to develop a ground game between the tackles and add 10-15 pounds 
of muscle to try to become an every-down back. Expect Heidenreich to begin his career as a return specialist or coverage 
player while competing for situational opportunities in an offense in a utility role. 
Vision: In the open field, Heidenreich is patient with luring the safety downhill before delivering a stick and spin 
combination at the boundary to the inside. 
He’s primarily an option runner taking tosses or jet sweeps. 
Elusiveness: When earning a run to the near-side edge, Heidenreich can transition downhill from a perimeter approach 
with one step. To the far-side edge, he needs two steps. 
In the open field, Heidenreich is patient with luring the safety downhill before delivering a stick and spin combination at the 
boundary to the inside. He gets his feet over low shots. 
Heidenreich has good curvilinear bend when working around end and still inside the hash. He has a nice two-quick cut inside 
downhill pursuit from a safety to get by the defender. 
He’s quick enough and has enough moves with spins, pressure, cuts and two-quick footwork to make multiple defenders 
miss during a run. 
Acceleration: Heidenreich can turn the short corner on linebackers, but the backside linebacker will run him down after 
getting 5-10 yards downhill. The same is true with runs to the long corner. He’ll turn the corner, but the backside linebacker 
will run him down 5-10 yards later. 
Speed: Heidenreich has the speed to flip the field and hold off defensive backs for long gains, if not breakaway runs 
depending on the angle. 
Power: Heidenreich can run through reaches from defensive backs. He uses a stiff arm to swath away from reaches. He 
keeps his feet moving when wrapped high. 
Direct Contact Balance: He can stalemate a safety. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Heidenreich will drop his pads and finish, colliding with a cornerback and running through the 
hit to extend past the defender. 
Releases: Heidenreich has a staggered stance with 80/20 weight distribution favoring his front foot. His hands rest at 
either side of his front knee. When he begins his release, he sinks into his stance and rolls off his front foot without waisted 
movement. 
Heidenreich uses sticks at the top of stems to set up a break. He has a read step that he pairs with a sudden two-quick 
inside to break outside on a speed turn. He counters the defender’s hands with a wipe or a swim. 
Heidenreich must create a greater contrast between patience and suddenness with his releases. He uses footwork to be 
patient and then accelerate into his stem, but he must develop patience and suddenness with the actual footwork move. 
Heidenreich uses a slow double up with a wipe and then stacks the safety up the seam. He steals a release with a read step 
outside to then break inside and shield the CB with his back on the backside of run.

WR Eli Heidenreich.pre_draft_advice:
While there’s a chance he could develop in full-time offensive player with fantasy value, 
Heidenreich is likely a utility option with potential special teams value who you can monitor from afar in most formats. 
Heidenreich has a nominally higher score than some of the players in his tier who I like more as investments because of his 
versatility.

RB Ayo Adeyi.elevator_pitch:
Adeyi is a cutback runner with a low center of gravity, curvilinear speed, and starter-caliber footwork 
and agility. Adeyi’s speed and acceleration are enough to make an NFL team in theory but in practice, the glut of RB talent 
in the sport has upped current baseline for these athletic skills. 
The RSP hasn’t raised the minimums for what’s acceptable speed due to the glut, because there are exceptional runners like 
Devin Singletary and Dalvin Cook whose physical skills had limitations in specific areas – limitations most reserves didn’t 
possess – but they had compensatory factors that helped their overall game. Raising the minimums because there’s a glut 
of talent would be a bad reason for changing standards. 
It just means that Adeyi will have a tougher road to earn a roster spot because speed and acceleration capture the attention 
of coaches in the preseason. Adeyi’s passing-down work is limited to check-downs and screens, and his blocking is a 
liability. 
Add this up, and Adeyi’s best shot is special teams or futures contract. He might be seeking an opportunity in another 
league, if he wants to continue playing the game professionally. 
Vision: Adeyi identifies tight creases and hits them with confidence. He reads leverage effectively to set up creases, and he 
identifies linebackers and defensive linemen penetrating into the backfield from the edge or the inside. 
He finds cutbacks on gap plays. Adeyi presses within a step of his offensive line to set up bounces. 
Elusiveness: Adeyi has a hard stick to set up bounces. He can open his hips with one step – pointing his toes to the 
boundary to do so. He has effective curvilinear speed in the open field to bend runs downhill from a perimeter approach at a 
good rate of speed. 
Adeyi has a two-step transition of 90 degrees downhill hill. He only needs two steps to decelerate to a complete stop. 
Adeyi gets his feet high enough to work through reaches and wraps to his lower body. Adeyi spins through reaches to his 
upper body. 
Adeyi can change stride and pacing to set up blockers and defenders. 
Acceleration: Adeyi’s acceleration is good enough to get the short corner and beat box defenders and some safeties. He 
adjusts stride length to set up blocks. He can be box defenders to the far-side edge. 
Speed: Adeyi can reach the secondary on a lot of runs, but most defensive backs can limit how far he gets in most cases. 
Power: Adeyi has a low center of gravity and drags defensive linemen forward when wrapped high. He has an effective stiff 
arm in the open field to ward off reaches and wraps to his upper body. 
Direct Contact Balance: Adeyi stalemates defensive backs. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Adeyi wins glancing shots with safeties – initiating with his pads. 
Routes: Adeyi will use a wipe at the top of a stem as he breaks across the face of a defender playing over the top of him. 
Adeyi uses a flat turn on speed breaks against underneath coverage. 
197

RB Sieh Bangura.elevator_pitch:
Bangura is a well-built back with competent athletic ability as a runner to make an NFL roster as a 
reserve or contributor off the bench. The problem with these designations is that there’s a glut of running back talent in the 
league and most backups on RB depth charts have talent in line with the RSP’s Rotational Starter Tier. 
If there’s a subset of skills where most backs on depth charts have a higher baseline during this era where there’s a glut, 
it’s speed, acceleration, and agility. This is where Bangura is passable, but not at this current baseline. 
Bangura’s best traits and skills are vision/decision-making, power, contact balance into direct hits, and ball security. Even 
so, only the contact balance is starter-quality. The rest is in line with a rotational/committee player off the bench. 
If Bangura can deliver on special teams, he might make an NFL roster. This could give him time to hone his skills and 
athletic ability just enough to earn a career as a backup. 
Where has the player improved? Pass protection. Although Banura still drops his head with a lot of blocks, he’s 
becoming a good striker in an all-or-nothing fashion, but his angles are good enough to make him successful at a higher 
rate. He’s winning more often against linebackers and delivering substantial hits. 
Vision: Bangura presses within 1-2 steps of perimeter blocks to set up bounces outside. He identifies leverage and 
penetration early in a run – interior and edge penetration. 
As a gap runner, Bangura sets up his pullers. 
Elusiveness: Bangura only needs two steps to transition downhill from a perimeter path at the edge of the box on a run to 
the short-side edge. Bangura opens his hips with the economy of one step where he points the toes of his outside step to 
the boundary. He’ll adjust stride length to set up creases. 
Bangura has effective curvilinear bend at a solid speed to bounce from the line of scrimmage to the edge. He can use pace 
changes at this phase of his runs to fool safeties pursuing downhill. 
Bangura’s spin is quick enough to avoid a safety shooting for his legs in the crease. Over the years, Bangura has improved 
in traffic, learning to get his feet and knees high through traffic as he works to soft spots in the defense. 
Acceleration: Bangura has enough acceleration around the short-side end to work past box defenders. He can force 
safeties to give chase. He can work around a cornerback to the boundary on a perimeter run to the far sideline. 
Speed: Bangura can hold off a cornerback up the boundary for 20 yards that he accelerated around on a perimeter run to 
the far sideline. 
Power: Bangura uses a stiff arm to ward off reaches from defenders. He’ll keep his feet moving when defenders reach or 
wrap high and drag defenders forward. He can keep his feet so teammates rally and generate a push. 
Direct Contact Balance: Bangura has won direct collisions with defensive tackles in the crease, but he was the aggressor 
and the defender was coming off a block and leaning into the contact rather than attacking. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Bangura bounces off glancing shots from defensive backs in the crease. 
199

RB Lincoln Pare.elevator_pitch:
N/A 
Vision: Pare works tight to his blockers, identifies penetration, and bounces the pay outside. He presses lead blockers and 
finds cutbacks or bounces. 
Pare is quick to hit creases on gap plays as soon as his lead blocker engages a defender. He accesses cutback lanes when 
the defenders stuff the intended crease. 
Pare is decisive when he hits a crease. He’ll attack tight creases when nothing else is available or a viable path. 
Elusiveness: Pare opens his hips efficiently with the point of his toes to the boundary. Pare has effective pressure cuts in 
the crease. He slides well laterally when opening his hips, so he can slide across creases to look for a crease for the bounce 
or cutback. 
Pare has a violent stick he uses to bait defenders crashing into the box from the edge. Pare uses a double up at the edge of 
the box to set up safeties coming downhill and freeze them to the outside. 
He can jump cut to avoid a cornerback playing the force role and cut back inside. Pare uses a jump stop to change direction 
after a tight press to the line. 
Pare’s jump cuts are also effective against penetrating linemen in the backfield. 
Pare adjusts stride length and pacing to set up blocks. He’ll hurdle low cut attempts to his legs in the open field. He has 
curvilinear speed to bend around the short-side edge and beat a linebacker. 
Pare will spin off contact in tight creases as well as duck under linemen in these spaces. 
Acceleration: Pare can turn the corner on box defenders and safeties in the box when working to the short-side edge on a 
bounce. He can beat box defenders around the far-side edge. 
Speed: Pare can hold off a safety 15-25 yards up the short-side boundary. He can hold off a corner and a safety for 50 
yards if he gets into the secondary untouched on a run up the middle. He can beat a safety’s angle from over the top and 
outrun him for 45-50 yards. 
Power: Pare meets defenders with low pad level and good body alignment to extend forward through contact. He has 
lapses when working between the tackles where he doesn’t drop his pads and gets hit in the chest. 
He can pull through reaches to his lower legs at the line of scrimmage. He can generate a goal-line push against linemen, 
moving his feet after the wrap. 
Pare has an effective stiff arm to ward off reaches in the backfield from penetrating linemen. He can also work around 
defensive backs with the stiff arm to push through their wraps to his frame, depositing them to the ground. 
He’ll drop his pads and initiate contact when finishing runs – especially against defensive backs in the open field. 
201

RB Dylan Edwards.elevator_pitch:
N/A 
Vision: Edwards drops his pads into tight creases and takes what he can get from the design of the play. He identifies 
leverage and outside penetration as part of his decision-making process. 
Edwards will press inside creases on outside runs, working within a step of his blockers and 1-2 steps of unblocked linemen 
in the interior crease to freeze them inside. 
Edwards has the vision to weave through blockers and avoid unblocked defenders as a result. He veers away from pursuit 
on the periphery of his direct vision. 
Elusiveness: Edwards sets up pullers with stride length and pacing variations so he can be patient with oncoming 
defenders working into the blocker. 
Edwards only needs 1-2 steps to transition downhill from a perimeter approach. Edwards opens his hips with economy and 
can do so as he finishing his two-step transition downhill. 
Edwards also gets his knees and feet high enough to avoid reaches to his lower body. He flips his hips effectively and dips 
his shoulders away from reaches to his frame. He executes sharp pressure cuts. 
He has quick jump cuts to avoid penetrating defenders working up the middle. In the second-level, Edwards has a sudden 
two-step juke to avoid backside pursuit working across the field. He can undercut the defender with it and put the defender 
down with a stiff arm. The juke is actually a two-step stop with a drop of his hips. 
Acceleration: Edwards has a notable second gear that can help him accelerate past safeties in the flat and boundary who 
have an angle on him. 
Speed: Edwards can outrun all three levels of the defense when he earns a runway in the open field. 
Power: Edwards uses his stiff arm effectively to avoid reaches to his frame. When building speed, Edwards can pull through 
reaches high to his frame from all three levels of defenders and from multiple defenders during a run. 
More often than not, Edwards’ legs go dead when a defender wraps him high—even a safety. What he does best is pull 
through reaches with a duck and/or stiff arm. 
Direct Contact Balance: Edwards can stalemate direct contact from a linebacker when he has momentum into the hitter. 
He’s unlikely to pull through the wrap up or get forward without assistance from teammates. 
Indirect Contact Balance: He can bounce off glancing shots to his frame from safeties when he as downhill momentum. 
Routes: Edwards identifies the second-level defender in the underneath zone, works to depth, and settles into the open 
area. He presents a friendly target for the quarterback. 
He’ll steal a release as an outside receiver with a double up. 
Receiving: Edwards used high-low attack of a wide route he tracked over his shoulder, clapping onto the ball. His default 
catching method is a high-low or side-by-side clap attack.

RB Dylan Edwards.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar unless Edwards earns a return specialist’s role and your league awards 
points for that job.

Sherrod is a decisive runner on Duo, and he finds bounce opportunities at the second level, or the optimum crease when

Sherrod will press within a step of his linemen to set up a cutback on inside zone. He takes what he can get when his

Sherrod can jump-stop into opening his hips where he points his outside foot to the boundary. He only needs a jump-stop to

Sherrod layers moves in succession: jump stops, hip-openers, spins, and lateral cuts. He also gets his feet over low shots

Sherrod will jump cut out of a deep press toward his linemen on inside zone. 
Acceleration: N/A 
Speed: N/A

N/A

defenders on blockers on gap and zone plays and uses stride and pacing to set up blocks or cutbacks. When there are no 
viable cutbacks or bounces, Johnson takes what’s available. 
Elusiveness: Johnson has quick and sharp pressure cuts at the line of scrimmage. Johnson can transition downhill from a 
perimeter approach with two steps. On some runs tight around the edge of the box, Johnson only needs one step or a jump 
cut to bend around the corner. 
He can also bounce outside with the point of the toe to the boundary that opens his hips efficiently within a step of edge 
defenders and interior penetration. 
Johnson has an effective stiff arm-and-spin combination. Johnson gets his feet high enough through the crease to work over 
and/or pull through the reach of low shots in the hole. 
Johnson has an effective jump cut to avoid interior penetration. 
Acceleration: Johnson has the burst to accelerate past box defenders into the secondary when cutting back to the near 
side flat. Johnson has the acceleration to beat safeties and corners around the far-side edge when they are within 5-7 yards 
of the line of scrimmage. 
Speed: N/A 
Power: Johnson’s stiff arm is accurate enough to ward off reaches to his frame. He keeps his feet moving through high 
wraps in the secondary. He pulls through reaches to his lower legs from defensive backs. 
Johnson drops his pads into oncoming defenders in the secondary. He’ll split safeties downhill. 
Direct Contact Balance: Johnson can stalemate the off-ball linebacker at the end of the crease and force a second 
defender to help. 
Indirect Contact Balance: When Johnson has built up speed, he can bounce off glancing shots to his pads when working

Diggs can leap over a pile in short yardage and do it with good height. 
Diggs can bounce outside with a stick and then open his hips with one step to the outside. 
Acceleration: Diggs’ acceleration rarely gets him past linebackers untouched on wide-open lanes in the box downhill. 
Speed: When there’s a packed box, Diggs can break it up the middle and force a safety to give chase for 25 yards before

Indirect Contact Balance: Diggs bounces off glancing shots from defensive backs hitting his thighs and hips. He’ll bounce

RB Kevon King.elevator_pitch:
King would be a much better runner if he connected the defensive scheme with his blocking scheme 
on any given play. He runs to open space first and doesn’t do enough to set up better opportunities based on where the 
best crease could be. Instead, he takes whatever space is available within his line of sight, and it costs him yardage – often 
significant gains. 
King is better with pure gap runs where he knows the hole he’s supposed to hit based on the design and it’s his job to find 
the best way to hit it with less leeway than other run designs. 
Vision: King identifies interior penetration during his exchange with the quarterback, but he must develop more efficient 
footwork so he’s not stepping 1-2 steps toward the line of scrimmage before making a move. 
A more efficient solution, such as opening his hips at the exchange point would give him space and time to identify the line’s 
overall leverage and access the cutback lane. Instead, King saw one gap, worked toward it, and tried to react to it – and the 
move was inefficient. 
King hits gaps decisively behind pullers. He identifies penetration of slanting defenders on gap plays and accesses the 
cutback lane. 
King reads the linebackers on zone runs and when the backside linebacker slides early to the inside, King begins his 
cutback. King can press within 1-2 or 2-3 steps of the line of scrimmage based on the whether he’s reading the linebackers 
or his double team. 
When running inside zone, King doesn’t press early enough toward the double team and away from the DT’s one-on-one 
with the near-side guard. If he presses toward the double team he avoids the pursuit of the tackle and has room up to the 
linebackers with a center working off the combo block to the backer. 
When running a perimeter play or bouncing outside, King will incrementally work to the outside with a press or two to 
interior gaps, drawing pursuit downhill that was heading to the boundary. 
King will adjust his stride at the boundary to earn position on pursuit over the top so he can deliver a stiff arm with a square 
position and push the defender downhill. This can be the difference between getting run out of bounds and scoring. 
King presses stalk blocks downfield when he reaches the secondary. He presses within 2-3 steps of the block before 
bouncing or cutting back. 
Elusiveness: King has a spin, but he doesn’t read the situation behind the line of scrimmage well enough to know when or 
when not to try it. 
He opens his hips effectively to bounce a play away from tight pursuit, but he must learn when to use it. He has left 
yardage on the field, because he didn’t use it when there was an opportunity. 
King has an effective stick and double up combo where he sticks to one side, doubles up to the other, and finishes with a 
jump cut away from the defender. This is an example of good work layering moves in succession to defeat penetration. 
King uses the double up well to set up a double team to the outside and cut inside and downhill off it. 
215

RB Julian Gray.elevator_pitch:
N/A 
Vision: On perimeter runs, Gray will press the inside gaps to set up his lead blocker and then bounce to the perimeter. 
Elusiveness: As an outside runner, Gray has a sudden stick inside to set up the bounce outside. Gray can decelerate to a 
quick stop in the open field and jump cut away from pursuit at his face. 
Gray has the curvilinear movement to transition downhill without any transition steps when running from a perimeter 
approach working from the opposite hash. 
Acceleration: Gray can accelerate past an outside linebacker who is in the far side flat on a run to the far boundary. 
Safeties can work from the middle of the field to reach him. 
Speed: When he reaches top speed with a long runway, he can work past safeties and corners coming from the middle or 
opposite side of the field when he’s at the far side boundary. 
Power: Gray can keep his feet moving through direct contact. His stiff arm wards off contact to his frame, but he must 
improve the placement and timing so he can drop the player to the ground and sustain the contact long enough to do so. 
He slaps the stiff arm at the defender, pawing at a part of the defender’s frame that allows the defender to eventually work 
into Gray’s frame. 
Gray will drop his pads into a defender at the boundary at the end of a run. He loads up the forearm shiver and strikes. 
Direct Contact Balance: Gray wins direct collisions with cornerbacks hitting him in the hips. 
Indirect Contact Balance: He can take a light glancing shot in the open field from a linebacker trailing him and hitting him 
in the back. When he drops his pads, he can bounce off a glancing shot to his pads from an off-ball linebacker. 
Routes: Gray has a sudden feet-switch and two-quick combo with a wipe counter when he’s split outside. He also has a 
double up with varying step lengths with each step. He also uses a read step to set up man coverage at the line. 
His wipe counter is well timed against linebackers on wheel and bullet routes. 
Gray uses a long break step at the top of stems. His breaks to the quarterback can have more snap with the turn. 
Gray can drop his weight out of his break step when running shallow routes. 
Gray must sell stems with greater pacing and various techniques available to bait defenders such as widening/narrowing the 
stem, insets, etc. 
Gray displays boundary awareness, tight-roping the sideline as he catches a target over his shoulder. 
Receiving: Gray uses underhand attack for targets at his chest, trapping the ball to his frame rather than attacking away 
from his frame at an earlier point with underhand position. 
218
RB Julian Gray.pre_draft_advice:
Gray began his career as a receiver. He finished as a running back. What he’s best known 
for is kickoff returns. This is his likeliest path to contributing, if he makes the NFL. If you’re league scores for returns, Gray 
is worth monitoring from afar.

WR Carnell Tate.elevator_pitch:
Carnell Tate might be a fake name. I have half the mind to call for an investigation of the Ohio State 
Athletic Department, because it wouldn't surprise me if Justin Jefferson went back to school and decided to play football on 
Saturdays under the stage name of "Carnell Tate." 
Carnell Tate and Justin Jefferson weigh 195 pounds. They run excellent routes, take a physical approach as run blockers, 
and make some of the most impressive catches you'll see. 
48
WR Carnell Tate.pre_draft_advice:
There are contingents who believe Tyson is the No. 1 receiver at this point of the year. 
Others believe it's Makai Lemon. Carnell Tate is my bet as the player most likely to see his stock rise during the offseason to 
challenge for that pole position. 
If you're drafting in a startup league, Tate would be my current choice for No. 1 overall. Fernando Mendoza is a compelling 
choice in Super-Flex formats. Jeremiyah Love makes sense if you’re one back away from playoff prominence that could lead 
to dominance.

WR Makai Lemon.elevator_pitch:
NFL Draft season -- and arguably the NFL Draft itself -- is a football beauty pageant that becomes 
hyper-focused on factors that aren't as important as the public, analysts, and teams make them. Some of you will argue 
that draft capital plays out and is an indicator of talent. 
Draft capital is a rigged game. Although considered a top, if not the top, wide receiver prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, 
Makai Lemon does not fit the classic archetype of the NFL Draft season's beauty pageant contestant. 
53
WR Makai Lemon.pre_draft_advice:
I expect Makai Lemon will remain an early-round prospect through "draft season," but it 
won't shock me if his workout metrics don't thrill in the swimsuit competition of the Underwear Olympics. Harold Fannin Jr. 
was a beloved prospect until the Combine Accountants got a hold of his numbers and didn't understand the operational 
context of his skills. 
The Fannin love disappeared overnight, except for those who understood how to balance film with the metrics. Fast forward 
to today, and Fannin might be the best part of the Browns' lowly passing offense. 
If Makai Lemon's draft capital takes a hit, buy into the fear. You'll be happy you did.

WR Malachi Fields.elevator_pitch:
Relative Athletic Scores (RAS) are a fun and informative layer of information because we can see how 
players compare to each other athletically over a history timeline. It’s a great conversation starter and an entertaining 
reference point. 
This is catnip for beat reporters and feature writers. Give them information and let them weave them into something 
magical. 
Here’s the thing about RAS. They are based on the NFL Combine. 
59
WR Malachi Fields.pre_draft_advice: Fields is a top-10 rookie pick in most formats.

WR KC Concepcion.elevator_pitch:
Concepcion is a savvy receiver who can excel at flanker or the slot, and he also delivers in the return 
game. He has some of the best releases from the line of scrimmage in this class because of the range of moves he offers 
and the expertise in his delivery. 
Concepcion understands how to incorporate pacing, violence, and subtlety with paired movements to set up breaks during 
his stems. His breaks are technically sound in both the short and intermediate ranges with timing routes. 
65
WR KC Concepcion.pre_draft_advice:
Concepcion is a first-round value in rookie drafts. While I’d rather see him slide to the late 
turn of the first and second rounds because of his lapses as a pass-catcher, I’d live with him in the middle of the first round.

WR Skyler Bell.elevator_pitch:
The prospect who is the exception to the rule is a dangerous game in the world of prospect evaluation. One 
of the common “exceptions to the rule” invoked with wide receiver prospects is the productive college player who exhibits 
lackluster technique as a pass-catcher. 
Good scouting involves projection. Determining when one is going too far with the projection or not far enough is the 
challenge. Skyler Bell is an excellent example of this challenge in 2026. 
70
WR Skyler Bell.pre_draft_advice:
If you want low-risk selections, Bell’s clap-attacking rules him out. If you agree with my 
logic surrounding his likelihood to become an exception to the rule, Bell has a WR2 floor and a WR1 ceiling that makes him 
a first-round rookie pick.

WR Jordyn Tyson.elevator_pitch:
Considered one of the best, if not the best, wide receiver prospects in this draft class among much of 
the fanbase and media, Tyson offers big-play vertical prowess, promising route skills, and flashy athletic ability at the catch 
point. 
There's a lot to like. Tyson is acrobatic, fluid, and a dynamic mover with the ball in his hands. 
76
WR Jordyn Tyson.pre_draft_advice:
Tyson is a first-round pick in rookie drafts. There’s a boom-bust volatility embedded into 
his grade relative to the receivers ranked ahead of him due to his injuries and more importantly his inconsistent execution 
of skills required of pro receivers. 
It’s most likely that any issues from Tyson’s bouts with lackluster technique won’t hold him back from becoming an 
impactful NFL receiver. However, it’s this lackluster work that could be an additional layer that potentially bogs down 
Tyson’s development path that the other players lack. 
Tyson will be WR1 on some boards. He’ll likely have a fair share of WR1 picks embedded in his ADP. I’d rather risk him at 
WR3 as my earliest pick. That’s my line of demarcation. Good luck determining yours.

WR Chris Brazzell II.elevator_pitch:
And the winner of the “Who Looks the Most Like George Pickens Contest,” is… 
There are three receivers in this draft class whose physical dimensions and style of play are like the Cowboys’ new arrival in 
2025. Brazzell has the highest score of the three. 
While the comparison may be a superficial layer of information working in his favor, his stint at Tennessee raises doubts. 
Logo scouting is frowned upon, but it also becomes a thing when information meets the desire to simplify. 
82
WR Chris Brazzell II.pre_draft_advice:
Brazzell is a first-round fantasy selection in rookie drafts unless an unanticipated event 
changes his value.

WR Chris Bell.elevator_pitch:
If there’s a player in this draft where I have equal fear about passing or drafting, Chris Bell is a strong 
candidate for that designation. He may be the only candidate I feel this way about this year. 
Bell’s game sears a hole into the film one moment and needs adult diapers the next. Sometimes these alternating thrills and 
horror are part of a learning process. After all, progress may appear more linear at times, but it never is. 
Add Bell’s surgically reconstructed ACL to the mix – a ligament he tore late in the 2025 season – and it’s just another layer 
to the internal drama surrounding Bell’s game, his potential, and his timeline to make an impact in the NFL. 
92
WR Chris Bell.pre_draft_advice:
If Bell falls to the second round in fantasy drafts, he’s worth the risk. You’ll have to wait a 
year to see the athlete he was before his injury, so maintain realistic expectations – including a risk that Bell never returns 
to pre-injury form. This is rare with first-time ACL injuries. They are usually just a time-consuming rehab process.

WR Elijah Sarratt.elevator_pitch:
Sarratt is a well-built possession receiver who can deliver plus-yardage plays with the help of play-
action, double moves, or constraint plays that fool the tendencies of a coverage thanks to good scouting and coaching. 
Sarratt’s style of play has some aspirational comparisons to Cardinals’ receiver Michael Wilson. 
Sarratt is a more reliable pass-catcher than Wilson was at this stage of his career, but it was because Wilson’s technique 
was still a work in progress. This led to higher highs and lower lows for Wison as he was still in the early stages of 
addressing the issue with a program he created and shared with the media as a senior. 
97
WR Elijah Sarratt.pre_draft_advice:
Sarratt may be the safest option by the RSP’s scoring criteria among WRs 10-16 on the 
board – and you could probably expand the range to WR23 – but there are several options ranked below Sarratt with higher 
ceilings. 
As you read through the rankings, you’ll see who I’ve noted in this way so you can make an educated decision whether to 
roll with the safest picks or take a slightly larger gamble on a player with a potential WR1 profile.

WR Antonio Williams.elevator_pitch:
Jayden Reed has been an RSP fave since he was playing at Michigan State. Williams compares well to 
Reed at the same points of their careers. 
Primarily a slot receiver, Williams has the speed, leaping ability, and skill after the catch to become a productive NFL flanker 
– perhaps a leading producer in his passing game if he continues refining his skills. 
Williams’ grade isn’t far from a designation of an immediate starter. He’s a reliable pass-catcher who wins the underneath 
zones and makes plays working across the field that leverages his skill as a ball carrier. 
101
WR Antonio Williams.pre_draft_advice:
Williams is worth consideration in the second round and a potential bargain in the third 
round. Depending on his NFL landing spot and draft capital, you could bump those valuations up by a round. 
104

WR Josh Cameron.elevator_pitch:
Cameron shared in an interview that Larry Fitzgerald is the player he tries to model his game after. 
It’s a great choice, even if after watching Cameron I saw different players based on physical dimensions and athletic ability. 
Cameron and Fitzgerald are roughly the same weight, but Fitgerald has two inches on Cameron. While I don’t remember 
how many releases Fitzgerald had at Pitt, Cameron has the most thorough display of release types in his portfolio that I 
have seen from a prospect in many years. 
106
WR Josh Cameron.pre_draft_advice:
We should acknowledge that Cameron’s floor as a potential WR3 in an offense isn’t 
exciting, it’s better than most of the receivers whose ceilings are WR3 value. Cameron’s most realistic ceiling is team’s WR2 
who could deliver top 15 fantasy production in an offense with an elite route runner opposite him and a top quarterback 
distributing the ball.

WR Germie Bernard.elevator_pitch:
I’m hesitant to place a receiver like Stevie Johnson or Robert Woods into a comparison spectrum for 
Germie Bernard without reminding readers that while a part of these comparisons is about talent, the point of the exercise 
is about style of play, and the players listed are checkpoints with how the player’s development path could evolve, plateau, 
or regress. 
I heard from a friend that NFL.com’s Lance Zierlein used Robert Woods as his comparison for Bernard. I know and respect 
Lance, and I understand how he would consider Woods because there are common elements between how Woods was used 
and the potential fit Bernard could have in an NFL offense. 
111
WR Germie Bernard.pre_draft_advice:
Bernard has a medium floor with a high ceiling – not an elite (top 3-5 at his position) 
ceiling. He’s a solid second- or third-round pick in rookie drafts this year.

WR Denzel Boston.elevator_pitch:
Boston is a skilled boundary receiver with excellent acceleration for his size that gets his open early in 
a route and then he uses his size, physicality, and knowledge of positioning at (and after) the catch point to impose his will 
on defenders earn the sideline. Depending on how well Boston develops his route game, he could aspire to become a 
featured option like Nico Collins or he develops into a skilled secondary sidekick like Tee Higgins. The variable is how much 
Boston adds and improves to his promising game. 
115
WR Denzel Boston.pre_draft_advice:
A quality pick in the late half of the first or early second rounds in rookie drafts. Josh 
Cameron, Germie Bernard, Antonio Wiliams, and Chis Bell have higher ceilings, but Boston’s floor is safer. There’s a good 
argument that the only safer prospects ranked ahead of Boston are Carnell Tate and Makai Lemon. 
So, if you find that the RSP’s ranking is lower on Boston than the consensus, remember that Boston is in the same tier as 
WR6-WR13 and 3.5 points separate him from the WR4 on the board – which is the value of an upside bonus in the RSP’s 
Receiving category. If you want safety over upside and still get a high floor and high ceiling, I wouldn’t begrudge you 
considering Boston as your WR3-WR5.

WR J. Michael Sturdivant.elevator_pitch:
Sturdivant is a deceptive evaluation. Watch him play at Florida, and he’s used in a role that doesn’t 
maximize his tools. He looks like a tall and fast player with limited route skills. Completing a pass for a big play, that was

The same felt true at UCLA. A big program out West recruited a big receiver to transfer and used him to get one big play a 
game. 
120
WR J. Michael Sturdivant.pre_draft_advice:

but the volatility of their games make the timeline difficult to predict when it will happen – if it happens at all. Consider

WR Zachariah Branch.elevator_pitch:
Branch has a boom-bust profile embedded into his grade that requires an explanation. He’s explosive 
enough to eventually play inside or outside in the NFL. To do both, there’s some development Branch will need with his 
game. In fact, there’s additional development Branch will need to play either role at a full-time level. If he doesn’t handle 
his business here, his career will take the shape of Mecole Hardman’s as a special teams option who can deliver as an

The issue is Branch’s routes. They are good enough to get on the field, but not good enough remain on the field every 
down. 
124
WR Zachariah Branch.pre_draft_advice:
Branch will have a volatile ADP range pre-draft. Post-draft, his draft capital could dictate

The candidates are Ja’Kobi Lane, Eric McAlister, and Bryce Lance. After the NFL Draft, Kevin Coleman might join this list.

WR Eric McAlister.elevator_pitch:
McAlister is a big play threat with excellent length, good speed, and an acrobatic knack for winning the 
football. He’s one of multiple prospects in this draft who fits along the George Pickens stylistic comparison spectrum. 
McAlister’s speed is deceptive because he’s a long strider, but he has smooth acceleration and he’ll force defensive backs to 
give chase for long swaths of the field if he’s allowed a runway for takeoff. For this to happen more often, McAlister must 
develop more nuance with his releases from the line of scrimmage. 
132
WR Eric McAlister.pre_draft_advice:
McAlister will likely have lower NFL Draft capital than his current skills and ceiling might 
suggest. His route refinement is close enough that I could have made an argument to score him among the top 10 receivers 
on the board. Ultimately, I prefer to give prospects credit for things they’ve earned, not what they appear on their way 
toward earning. 
Still, this is worthwhile information for your consideration when taking his pre-draft ranking into context. McAlister is worth 
a selection in the middle to late rounds of your averaged-size league of 20-25 roster spots.

Kobi Lane.elevator_pitch:
Lane can become primary receiver in the NFL – possibly a star receiver. Details of his game grew as 
the season progressed, including his releases from the line of scrimmage, savvy methods for setting up breaks, and flatter 
speed turns. 
Lane isn’t far from developing into an NFL starter. At the same time, there are details lacking with his game that could hold 
him back depending on his landing spot. 
137
Kobi Lane.pre_draft_advice:
Lane is a medium-floor, medium-ceiling option because there’s consistently a role for tall-
rangy ball-getters in offenses who can win at the boundary or in a window of zone coverage, but his man-to-man game may 
never emerge to a level where he becomes a clear No. 2 option in the team’s pecking order. 
Lane will likely be a top 12 pick at his position in many rookie drafts. I’m willing to stretch my stance and consider him 
among the top 15 pass-catchers, but I’d be more comfortable taking him among the top 24. Draft capital and landing spot 
could persuade me to value him higher than the top 15.

Burks offers big plays from the slot and has potential to do work as a flanker in an NFL offense if he 
can refine his routes and shore up potentially problematic technical issues as a pass catcher. Burks’ skills as a runner are 
the strengths of his game. He should contribute early as a situational threat on offense and a return specialist in the kicking 
game. 
Burks has emerging skills as a receiver. He has a variety of release techniques to work past tight coverage at the line of 
scrimmage. Burks executes a pair of those techniques with the contrast between patience and suddenness required of good 
142
WR Deion Burks.pre_draft_advice:
The safe route is to value Burks as a big-play slot receiver who will earn 500-700 yards 
and 3-5 touchdowns as his usual production during his peak. That production bye-week fantasy value, at best and not a 
worthwhile investment. 
If the NFL team drafting Burks labels him as flanker or a flanker/slot, Burks could earn 700-900 yards and 4-6 touchdowns 
in this role. That’s WR3-WR4 material and draftable for dynasty formats with at least 35-men rosters. 
If Burks becomes the flanker, he could earn 900-1,100 yards and 5-7 scores and he’s in demand in any fantasy format with 
two receivers as starters. It makes Burks a safer post-draft commodity than pre-draft option.

WR Bryce Lance.elevator_pitch:
Trey Lance’s younger brother has the promise to make a name for himself to the degree that media 
will either lead with, “Bryce Young’s older brother,” in a few years when introducing Trey, or they’ll use “the Lance 
brothers,” if Trey gets a second chance to emerge. 
Bryce, like Trey, has the physical skills of a top-notch NFL starter. He’s big, fast, quick, and explosive, which gives him a 
high ceiling as an outside receiver. 
146
WR Bryce Lance.pre_draft_advice:
Lance is a must-draft option for leagues with at least 35 roster spots. For smaller rosters 
with practice squads, Lance could be available in the late half of the third or the fourth round and worth stashing for year. 
There is a chance a team falls in love with Lance’s ceiling and selects him on Day Two of the NFL Draft. If this happens, 
Lance’s draft capital will likely force him into the lineup early. There will be heightened danger for Lance to fail for the 
reasons described above and the draft capital will likely raise Lance’s fantasy ADP. There will be safer options at this point, 
but I’d still recommend him as a consideration if you have the luxury to take a chance on his ceiling.

WR Ted Hurst.elevator_pitch:
Hurst’s development could go in two distinct directions. If he emphasizes precision, refines his routes, 
and adds a little muscle to his frame, Hurst could become an aspiring A.J. Green – a big-play primary receiver who can 
defeat press-man coverage and run the full route tree. 
If Hurst bulks up further and his route tree is limited, he could become a big-play possession option like Tee Higgins who 
isn’t asked to run as full of a route tree and wins with his size at the boundary. 
151
WR Ted Hurst.pre_draft_advice:
Hurst is worth considering as a mid-to-late pick in rookie drafts. The NFL will draft him 
and likely before Round Five. Consider him a patience play who might deliver an early taste of what he could eventually 
become at various points during his rookie year.

WR Malik Benson.elevator_pitch:
Benson is a speedster capable of stretching a defense in the college game, but he’ll need to refine his 
release tools to bait NFL cornerbacks enough to win up the boundary like he does at Oregon. At this point, he has one 
reliable move that he performs with the refinement capable of doing the job against tight man coverage. 
Fortunately, there is more to Benson’s game than winning go routes and fades. He has some skill with setups midway 
through his stems to work free over the middle, but he must cut down on lapses that tip off his intentions to the safety 
playing over the top. 
155
WR Malik Benson.pre_draft_advice:
Benson is worth consideration as a late-round pick you intend on stashing for at least a 
year on a practice squad or at the end of a loaded roster where one spot taken with a seldom-used player is possible. 
Otherwise, monitor Benson from afar unless returns are part of your league’s scoring system and Benson is slated for this 
duty.

WR Junior Vandeross III.elevator_pitch:
Vandeross is a slot receiver with big-play potential who didn’t earn an NFL Combine invitation. This 
likely has to do with the combination of his height, weight, and program. If Wan’Dale Robinson went to Toledo instead of 
Kentucky, Robinson might not be earning a fat contract with the Titans right now. 
This has less to do with Robinson’s talent and worth ethic, but the opportunities draft capital provides players because the 
NFL isn’t as much of a meritocracy as it appears to fans. There are rarely open competitions with equal practice reps for 
late-round picks and UDFAs. 
160
WR Junior Vandeross III.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Vandeross from afar until you see his draft capital. If he earns draft capital inside 
the fifth round, make Vandeross a priority addition in PPR formats with at least 35 roster spots. If the draft capital is outside 
the fifth round, he could be worth stashing for larger rosters with at least 40 spots and a practice squad when the offseason 
rules allow for even more spots until the cut-down date.

WR Jordan Hudson.elevator_pitch:
Hudson is a possession receiver with enough speed to stretch the field against favorable coverage 
matchups. His release work against man coverage has enough craft for him to have success at the line of scrimmage and 
the speed is there to beat cornerbacks on intermediate and vertical routes. 
Hudson has more work ahead with route setups and breaks. The setups he uses are good, but he needs to develop more of 
them. Hudson’s breaks are better with shallow routes and less precise with some of his intermediate routes. Both vital 
phases of route running are heading in the right direction. 
164
WR Jordan Hudson.pre_draft_advice:
Hudson is a high-floor, medium-ceiling option with a better thanking than several players 
below him who have higher ceilings but slightly lower floors within the same tier. Monitor from afar unless Hudson lands 
enviable NFL Draft capital. If this happens, consider him for your expanded offseason roster.

WR Dane Key.elevator_pitch:
Key and Chase Roberts are similar prospects with the same Comparison Spectrum. The subtle 
difference may be that Key has more to offer in the middle of the field and as a runner after the catch. Key’s ceiling might 
also be close to the way Eric Decker played his game whereas Roberts might be close to Tim Patrick. 
Key sets up routes effectively heading into his breaks, but his breaks need more consistency and refinement. He can bend 
into weight drops and he has shown that he can execute flat speed turns, but he must do it at every range of the field and 
with precise execution. 
167
WR Dane Key.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Key from afar until you see if he acquires NFL Draft capital higher than the fifth 
round. If so, he could be worth stashing on expanded offseason rosters. 
Key is a high-floor, medium-ceiling option who may not have the upside of players ranked lower, but his skills make him a 
viable NFL option. That’s not fantasy-friendly, but it’s a truth about players with safe profiles versus volatile profiles that 
rankings without criteria may not show.

WR Eric Rivers.elevator_pitch:
Rivers is a slot receiver with some down-field ability, but the strength of his game is underneath 
routes. He delivers breaks with good snap, finds the open area, and generates yardage after the catch with good stop-start 
quickness and patient setups of blocks. Rivers extends well for the ball over the middle, and he withstands physical play. 
Pair his work on offense with his return skills and Rivers could deliver production in a role we’ve seen for Josh Downs and 
Jamison Crowder. That’s not exciting for fantasy football, but it’s necessary when offense use three and four receivers. 
171
WR Eric Rivers.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar. If your league includes scoring for special teams returns, monitor 
Rivers to see if he earns a role as a return specialist. Eventually, he might earn a dual role that makes him worthwhile.

WR Chase Roberts.elevator_pitch:
Roberts is a possession receiver with the size, strength, and build-up speed to win in the vertical game 
when working up the seam, breaking outside to the boundary from inside alignments, and with the help of double moves 
and play-action passes. Think Tim Patrick. If we want to be more ambitious with a player comparison and include an option 
who played more often outside and downfield, think Eric Decker. 
Roberts isn’t explosive, but he has some burgeoning craft with his releases from the line that could go a long way toward 
helping him become success with selected man-to-man opportunities in the vertical game. Getting early separation during a 
175
WR Chase Roberts.pre_draft_advice:
Think of Roberts as third for fourth receiver who may have peak years in the range of 
700-900 yards and 6-8 scores, but more often delivers occasional fantasy value as a match-up/bye-week option GMs can 
acquire as a short-term rental for their rosters. Monitor from afar.

Meke Brockington.elevator_pitch:
Brockington is a sure-handed, possession receiver with excellent strength for this size. He wins 
contested targets in tight quarters and can bully defensive backs and some linebackers in open space. 
Brockington’s game isn’t built on great speed but he’s quick enough to earn separation within the first 10 yards of a route 
and stack defenders, controlling the pace and path of the route. There’s good reason to think he’s faster than I’m 
characterizing, but there wasn’t enough proof to stand by it. 
180
Meke Brockington.pre_draft_advice:
Brockington is likely a high-floor, low-ceiling player to monitor from afar until he makes 
enough noise in training camp. If he does, consider Brockington as a free agent for your expanded offseason rosters. 
I like Brockington’s game, but there’s not enough to stand firm on the idea he will exceed projections as a situational 
contributor in the NFL.

WR Jalen Walthall.elevator_pitch:
Walthall has a bigger ceiling that it may appear because his stints at Hawaii and Incarnate Word 
effectively make him a small-school prospect who didn’t have access to the level of training that you’d likely see at the top 
Power Five programs. Walthall didn’t look like he was 191 pounds on film during his playing career, and it means there’s 
probably room for him to get quicker, faster, stronger, and bigger within the next 1-2 years in the NFL. 
The key phrase is “in the NFL,” and the fact that Walthall earned an invitation to the NFL Combine is a good sign that he 
could be drafted or taken as priority free agent with a decent contract. If this happens, Walthall will hopefully have the 
mindset to make the most of the two-year plan a team will have for him. 
184
WR Jalen Walthall.pre_draft_advice:
Walthall is a late-round pick for leagues with large rosters of at least 35-40 players and at 
least 1-2 practice squad spots. Otherwise, consider him for your expanded offseason roster as a summer stash who might 
hit early, and you’ve preempted the waiver wire. Low risk, decent reward potential.

WR Cyrus Allen.elevator_pitch:
Allen is a shifty slot receiver with big-play ability after the catch and in the vertical game against zone 
defenders. He’s at his best against underneath coverage where he can serve as an extension of the ground game or as a 
receiver who works across the middle into open space as he earns the ball in the intermediate game. 
Allen has potential to become a viable boundary receiver, but there are details to address with releases, stems, setups, 
breaks, and positioning against defenders before he attacks the ball. The likelihood for Allen to develop all these skills and 
overcome the label as a late-round or UDFA space player is small. 
188
WR Cyrus Allen.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar until we see if he earns NFL Draft capital. Regardless he’s wroth

WR Brenen Thompson.elevator_pitch:
Thompson has elite speed, good route breaks that should get better, and sound hands with only rare 
technical glitches. Wrap Thompson in the open field and he’s done, but that’s the trick that is difficult for defenders to figure

Monitor Thompson from afar in all but the largest leagues – 50-plus roster spots and

A former walk-on, I wanted to love Sobkowicz’s game, especially after seeing the highlight reel 
package in the link at the end of this report. He’s a skilled route-runner and pass-catcher who has impressive displays in his

potential for him to be a UDFA who doesn’t earn opportunities to compete in training camp. If an NFL team selects

WR Vinny Anthony II.elevator_pitch:
Anthony’s a quick receiver with good acceleration who has improved his game over the course of his 
college career. He has developed a decent range of tools to run successful routes at the college level. Now, it’s time for him

213
WR Vinny Anthony II.pre_draft_advice:
Anthony’s draft capital may warrant a late-round selection in large fantasy leagues, and it 
might pay off as a patient play. If I were going to bet on a patience play, I think CJ Daniels, Ja’Mori Maclin, and Camden

I’d monitor Anthony from afar. His physical profile makes him more commonplace in the NFL, which means he must excel at

WR Kendrick Law.elevator_pitch:
Law fits in the mold of flankers who also can work from the slot. He’s an explosive athlete who can be 
used around the line of scrimmage as an extension of the ground game. 
Law is also physical enough at the point of attack to become an asset as a blocker for the ground game. Although there are

WR Kendrick Law.pre_draft_advice:
Law is worth a late-round pick in leagues with at least 25 roster spots with 2-3 extra 
spots on the practice squad. If he has NFL success for fantasy GMs, he’ll likely be a patience play within 2-3 years, which

WR CJ Daniels.elevator_pitch:
Daniels is one of those players where it is hard not to like him when you watch his game on the field. 
He’s a physical ball-winner who displays acrobatic skill and terrific hand-eye coordination in tight coverage. Get Daniels 
within semi-realistic area of the football, and he’s better than most at making the difficult catch. 
Daniels also runs with physicality after the catch. He’s not explosive or sudden, but he’s powerful and determined. Get 
Daniels free in zone coverage or scheme routes that can work open with the help of misdirection, and Daniels can make big 
plays. 
221
WR CJ Daniels.pre_draft_advice:
Daniels is worth a late-round pick in leagues with large rosters with at least 45 spots, 
including a robust practice squad. Otherwise monitor Daniels from afar as a future desperation bye-week pick.

Zhaun Stribling.elevator_pitch:
Well-built with straight-line speed, Stribling has the hands and power to earn the ball in tight windows 
and deliver yardage after contact. His short-range routes are crisp enough for him to earn separation and generate yards 
after the catch when there’s a cushion. 
Stribling’s intermediate game lacks precision as a route runner. At this point, his game is about quick-hitting passes, 
straight-line routes, and vertical shots up the rails. This has a place in the NFL. It doesn’t always have a place in a starting 
lineup. 
225
Zhaun Stribling.pre_draft_advice:
Stribling will be a mid-to-late pick in fantasy drafts because there’s projected draft capital 
that Stribling has starter potential. He’s a bigger projection as a starter than characterized, even if there are some grains of 
truth about his game having that upside.

WR Aaron Anderson.elevator_pitch:
Anderson is a slot receiver with potential as a return specialist. He has speed to become a big-play slot 
option, but he hasn’t figured out how to ingrain it into his vertical routes so he’s maximizing separation. 
Anderson’s underneath routes are a different story. He uses stem work and setups to turn around opponents before he’s 
executing his break. 
229
WR Aaron Anderson.pre_draft_advice:
Anderson could be worth a late pick in leagues with rookie drafts with at least five rounds. 
Otherwise, monitor him as a free agent addition after your rookie draft to see what his NFL Draft capital will be.

Mori Maclin.elevator_pitch:
Related to former Eagles-Chiefs starter Jeremy Maclin, Ja’Mori is a quick slot-flanker hybrid who has 
cultivated some promising nuance to various parts of his game. Maclin has patience and suddenness with some of his 
releases, he has some nuanced setups at the top of his stems, and he has flat speed turns out of his three-step break. 
Maclin’s pass-catching is sound. He tracks the ball well. When there are lapses, he uses underhand attack in situations 
where overhand attack is the optimal choice, and it has led to him fighting the ball. 
233
Mori Maclin.pre_draft_advice:
Maclin is worth considering if you’re in a large league with 40-plus spots on a roster and 
the roster minimums are expanded after your rookie draft. If the NFL drafts Maclin, it probably happens after the fifth 
round.

WR Harrison Wallace III.elevator_pitch:
Wallace has the fundamentals to develop a well-rounded game as a flanker who can stretch the field 
when isolated to one side or in play-action scenarios. Like a lot of young receivers in this class, Wallace has acquired a lot of 
route tools and techniques, but he hasn’t mastered enough of them to be NFL-ready beyond a situational role. 
Wallace’s best work as a route runner is against zone coverage. He has a good feel for the triangle of defenders in the 
assigned area of his route and knows where to settle. Wallace also slides to open space quickly when his first break doesn’t 
earn a target. 
237
WR Harrison Wallace III.pre_draft_advice:
Consider Wallace at the end of drafts with large rosters of at least 40 spots and a practice 
squad. Otherwise, monitor the NFL Draft to determine if Wallace earns capital. If so, make him a free agent addition for 
expanded offseason rosters, and continue monitoring is yearly progress for the next 1-3 seasons if he earns any preseason 
love.

WR Camden Brown.elevator_pitch:
Brown has physicality at the catch point and after the catch. He’s an acrobatic ball-winner who 
withstands contact after earning good position on his opponent. He bounces off hits and makes defenders miss with the ball 
in his hand. 
From releases to setups, Brown has a lot of route tools at his disposal, but he’s master of none at this time. There’s a lot of 
suddenness to his game, but he must develop some patience to set up that quickness and bait defenders into mistakes. This 
will also allow Brown to further unlock his power at the most opportune moments during a release or at the top of a route. 
241
WR Camden Brown.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Brown in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Brown earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to 
monitor on your roster during camp. 
Among the players earning this type of advice from the RSP, Brown is one of note that GMs should be returning to the well 
to monitor or stash if he’s making incremental progress early in his career.

Tool around the Internet, and you’ll see YouTube videos touting Caldwell as the wide receiver who 
“dominated the NFL Combine.” At Cadwell’s size, a 4.31-second, 40-Yard Dash and a 42-inch vertical leap are impressive 
marks. 
These two marks won Caldwell additional media buzz, and possibly another look from teams. Yet, the RSP is old enough to 
remember Chris Conley. 
245
WR Jeff Caldwell.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Caldwell in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Caldwell earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent

WR Barion Brown.elevator_pitch:
Brown is an athletic flanker-slot receiver with good route setups but lackluster breaks. He has 
impressive catches on tape against tight coverage in the vertical game, but there are lapses with tracking the ball that 
create a bad chain of mishaps. When Brown leaves his feet unnecessarily because he doesn’t have a confident bead on the 
ball, he lapses into clap attacks or uses the suboptimal attack position for the trajectory of the target. 
Otherwise, Brown earns the ball against tight coverage and hard contact with a reliable frequency that one can envision an 
NFL team valuing him Brown as the third or fourth option in 11 personnel sets who can generate big plays up the seams 
against zone, manufactured plays outside against man, and from the backfield as a runner or screen receiver. 
249
WR Barion Brown.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Brown in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Brown earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to 
monitor on your roster during camp.

WR Octavian Smith.elevator_pitch:
Smith is a reliable pass catcher with good short-area quickness and acceleration to defeat man-to-
man coverage in the shallow and intermediate ranges of the field. He has promising displays of nuance at the line of 
scrimmage against tight coverage, and he knows how to set up defenders at the top of his stems. 
Smith is better with his breaks on shallow routes. His intermediate routes lack the precise break of a starter and this could 
hurt him early in his career because NFL defenders take much better angles out of breaks than college defenders. What was 
open at College Park will be shut at most pro stadiums. 
253
WR Octavian Smith.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Smith from afar. If Smith earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.

WR Reggie Virgil.elevator_pitch:
Virgil is possession-plus receiver who can win downfield thanks to his length, ability to earn position 
on defenders at the boundary and win the ball, and capable hands. Virgil is a limited route runner who must develop a 
better man-to-man and timing-route game to emerge as more than a situational player or reserve. 
Virgil’s routes are better in the shallow range of the field than the intermediate range. It’s this area of the field that is the 
difference between having every-down value and being limited to situational football. 
257
WR Reggie Virgil.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Virgil from afar. If Virgil earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.

WR Colbie Young.elevator_pitch:
Young is a well-built boundary receiver who moves with power. He’s a long-striding speedster with 
build-up speed. He wins contested targets and stretch the field with on fades, double moves, and play-action vertical 
targets. 
Suddenness is not his game, but he is learning how to set up breaks with techniques at the top of his stem that bait 
defenders out of position. 
261
WR Colbie Young.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Young from afar. If Young earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.

WR DT Sheffield.elevator_pitch:
Sheffield is an elusive big-play slot receiver who can also work at flanker. He’ll beat cornerbacks and 
safeties in the vertical game with his acceleration and speed. He could become even more valuable as a deep threat if he 
refines his releases so he’s baiting defenders with pacing and strid variations with the footwork. 
Focus drops, clap-attacks when extending wide of his frame, and drops when turning against his break path on catchable 
passes are all issues he must clean up. Sheffield makes some strong catches, but the consistency with the targets of 
underrated difficulty and unforced errors holds him back. 
265
WR DT Sheffield.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Sheffield from afar. If Sheffield earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be

WR Trebor Pena.elevator_pitch:
Pena is a possession slot receiver entering a league that is trending away from using this role in its 
base sets, Pena exhibits good pace change and other setups on short-range routes. He finds open zones well, but he’ll need 
to improve the sharpness of his breaks so he can maximize separation and potential yardage after the catch. 
Pena works through physical coverage to earn the ball and he has enough quickness and footwork to earn yardage after the 
catch. He’s not a big-play threat, but he can deliver competent possession work from the slot when called upon.

WR Trebor Pena.pre_draft_advice:

WR Jalil Farooq.elevator_pitch:
Farooq is a well-built, physical receiver with acrobatic skills and toughness at the catch point and with 
the ball in his hand, Farooq lacks compelling speed. If he wins against man coverage, he’s winning early. 
Farooq’s best work is in zone coverage where he finds space, works with his quarterback to create space, and he earns

Other than some bouts of clap-attacking with targets that force him to lay out for the ball on vertical routes, Farooq is a 
reliable third or fourth receiver for a team that wants a physical zone presence who can earn yardage after the catch.

WR Jalil Farooq.pre_draft_advice:

WR Max Tomczak.elevator_pitch:
A capable route runner with good hands, Tomczak is a short- and intermediate-range threat with a 
decisive and physical running style for his size. His routes have good setups for his breaks. If Tomczak can develop more 
refinement with his releases at the line of scrimmage and proves that his athletic enough to win against man-to-man

Releases: Tomczak has a staggered stance with 80/20 weight distribution favoring his front leg. His arms hang at either

WR Max Tomczak.pre_draft_advice:
