WR Will Pauling.elevator_pitch:
Pauling is a slot receiver with some shiftiness in the open field who can also win selected vertical shots 
from the inside-out against nickel corners. There are elements of good timing routes in his portfolio, but he hasn’t pieced it 
altogether for the intermediate ranges of the field. 
Pauling makes tough catches against contact and tight coverage. He also has too many targets that he drops against tight 
coverage and contact because of lackluster attack technique that appears to be an embedded part of his game. 
281
WR Will Pauling.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Pauling from afar. If Pauling earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.

Mega Blake.elevator_pitch:
Blake is a possession receiver with burgeoning craft as a route runner. He understands how to 
widen/narrow stems and pair them with setups that can manipulate zone and man defenders. 
Blake has proven reliable in the middle of the field against tight coverage and contact. He positions his frame well to the ball 
and to keep it away from the coverage. 
Blake’s hands are reliable and technically sound. There’s an occasional lapse with clap-attacking a target above his head, 
but this is a common issue for most receivers from time to time. 
284
Mega Blake.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Blake from afar. If Blake earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.

WR Romello Brinson.elevator_pitch:
Brinson earns a lot of targets as boundary threat on hitches, vertical routes, and passes that are an 
extension of the ground game. He has decent length in a thin frame, and the speed to separate one-on-one. 
Brinson has the athletic ability to develop dangerous releases, and he has array of them on display at SMU, but he must 
deliver them with pacing contrasts to bait defenders. The better he gets at this aspect of the game, the more dangerous his 
speed becomes. 
Brinson’s short game has decent breaks, but he must develop the same skills with intermediate timing routes. At this point, 
he’s one of the many prospects who have a short and deep game but not a complete game. 
If Brinson is going to stick to a roster with these limitations, he must stand out with his hands. There’s reason to believe he 
can do so. Brinson has excellent body control and some high-end tracking skills. 
Brinson must reduce attack issues at the catch point. His clap-attacking when extending his arms can turn a potentially 
impressive catch sour. 
After the catch, Brinson has sudden footwork and movement to create on his own. Expect Brinson to compete for a final 
roster spot or an opportunity on a practice squad if he’s drafted on Day Three or signed to a priority free-agent deal. 
Technical: Clap attacks. Most of his catches against Wake Forest. See if this is more widespread and you missed it with the 
other games. 
Releases: Brinson has a staggered stance with 80/20 weight distribution favoring his front foot. There are reps where his 
feet appear wider than might be optimal for him to work against tight coverage off the line. 
His arms hang at either side of his front knee. When Brinson begins his release, he sinks into his stance and rolls off his 
front foot without wasted motion. 
Brinson’s pads come over his knees, and he swings his arms with full motion to sell the vertical urgency of the route. 
Brinson uses a two-step break mid-stem, but there’s no nuance between patience and suddenness to set up the defender 
with the move. Brinson has a three-quick release to get inside. 
Brinson has a hard stick with range. He uses a double up to the outside to set up an inside release. Brinson has a read-step 
to set up an inside release. 
Brinson counters the defender’s hands with a wipe, shed, or shoulder reduction. Brinson also has a swat-swim counter. 
Brinson steals releases with a hesitation or stutter and a swat counter. He combines a stutter and stick to set up breaks 
inside. 
He combines a read step and two-quick to work outside-in and then downfield of the defender. Brinson also combines a 
feet-switch with a two-quick and counters with a wipe to get inside. 
The only releases where I’ve seen a combination of patient-and-sudden contrast is the double up and a hesitation-stick 
combo. Both happened for the first time six games into my sample of 2025 film. 
Separation: Brinson has the acceleration to earn separation on downfield routes in the intermediate, vertical, and deep 
ranges of the field. He stacks defenders when he earns 2-3 steps early in the stem. 
When running intermediate and deep stems, Brinson attacks them at full speed. When running shallow stems, his pacing 
can be lackluster and it has cost him completions because he couldn’t break back to the ball on time. 
Route Stems: Brinson narrows (dives) and widens stems to set up breaks. He also runs at the defender. 
Brinson has earned position inside by widening his stem and working into the feet of the defender before breaking inside on 
the post. 
289
WR Romello Brinson.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Brinson from afar in leagues with less than 40 roster spots and no practice 
squads. In those deep leagues, Brinson becomes a viable addition for expanded offseason roster spots via free agency if he 
earns NFL Draft capital.

WR Lewis Bond.elevator_pitch:
A productive possession-slot receiver, Bond has enough acceleration to win in the deeper zones. His 
route running is at its best in the underneath zones because he has crisp breaks in that range of the field and there’s less 
need for him to deliver advanced route setups against defenders. 
A productive pass-catcher at Boston College, Bond’s attack of the ball has enough flaws for concern that his hands won’t 
translate as well to the NFL. If Bond can prove his hands will translate as reliably as they did in school, Bond could earn a 
situational role as a slot receiver in a league might be veering away from that position as part of its base offensive scheme. 
292
WR Lewis Bond.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Bond from afar. Down the line, you may find Bond a free-agent consideration in a 
PPR format with large rosters (40-plus). This is if Bond earns a role similar to the players in his Comparison Spectrum at 
any point during the season or his career.

WR Anthony Smith.elevator_pitch:
Smith has good length and acceleration. He also displays some expertise with release footwork that 
gets him open downfield as a big-play weapon in a spread offense. 
While Smith’s acceleration is good, his quickness isn’t, and this can bog him down when working underneath coverage. He 
does his best work one-on-one at the boundaries with contested targets. 
Smith is a good zone route runner and uses his size to win man-to-man at the boundary. He must develop into a well-
rounded route runner to earn anything more than a reserve role or a spot on a practice squad. 
295

WR Hank Beatty.elevator_pitch:
Beatty is a big-play slot receiver with toughness at the catch point and enough burst and movement to 
generate big plays in open space. With the right matchups, he can earn big plays up the boundary as an outside option. 
Beatty’s route game is competent for zone coverage, but he must show – or develop – a man-to-man game. His release 
work lacks nuance that compels defenders out of position, and there isn’t enough evidence of Beatty using setups with his 
routes that is vital to route-running as a pro. 
299

WR Caleb Douglas.elevator_pitch:
A tall speedster with good length, Texas Tech featured Douglas in one-on-one scenarios at the 
boundary where he could use his dimensions and body control to earn big plays. The Red Raiders also manufactured 
opportunities for Douglas to work uncovered up the seams and flats of a zone stretched too thin. 
Douglas has the athletic ability to become a competent NFL big-play threat, but he must develop greater nuance with how 
he executes the abundance of footwork techniques he uses to release from the line of scrimmage. Douglas is better at 
delivering his route setups with greater nuance, but he still has refinement ahead there, too. 
302

WR Kobe Prentice.elevator_pitch:
A big-play slot receiver at Baylor, Prentice exploits the seams with his ball tracking, speed, and 
quickness. These athletic skills also make him an extension of the ground game in the flats. 
Prentice is an experienced zone receiver who understands how to get open against umbrella coverages in the college game. 
He’s reliable for quick-hitting passes and he’ll either make the first man miss or work through contact from second- and 
third-level defenders. 
Prentice has lapses with attack technique. He claps onto the ball, which either creates a violent recoil too difficult to corral, 
or the ball works through his hands because they are too wide as the targets passes through. 
306

WR Kaden Wetjen.elevator_pitch:
Iowa used Wetjen as a utility receiver from the wing, the backfield, split wide, and mostly from the 
slot. His best shot at NFL work will be in the return game. If Wetjen delivers there, he could ingratiate himself into a gadget 
role in an offense thanks to his skills as a ball carrier and competencies as a pass-catcher. He must show more as a route 
runner for an expanded role or become a perfect fit as a receiver out of the backfield. 
Releases: Wetjen has a staggered stance with 80/20 weight distribution favoring his front leg. His arms hang at either side 
of his front knee. 
311
WR Kaden Wetjen.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Wetjen from afar. If your league scores punt returns, Wetjen is a potential 
consideration.

TE Eli Stowers.elevator_pitch:
Formerly the No.12 high school quarterback in the country, Stowers had offers to play quarterback for UGA, LSU, 
Clemson, and Auburn. A knee injury during his last game as a high school junior began a series of events that changed his future. 
Stowers wound up at New Mexico State as their future quarterback. Instead, Stowers tore the labrum of his throwing arm in 2021. 
23
TE Eli Stowers.pre_draft_advice:
Stowers is technically an instant starter with his RSP grade, but he’s still on the cusp of a rotational 
talent. His landing spot will dictate if he’s an immediate impact player. He’s the safest move tight end on the board, but is 2026 the 
most dangerous year for the offensive trends to work away from the move tight end? Probably not, but 2-3 years from now, Stowers 
might find his production declining if these changes take root.

TE Michael Trigg.elevator_pitch:
Trigg might be the riskiest tight end in the draft. He has a flair for the spectacular, but difficulties with the 
mundane. He’s also limited as a blocker. 
While he has a great wingspan that could help him with specific blocks, Trigg’s physical dimensions are at their ceiling and he’s still 
undersized for work at the line of scrimmage. Trigg’s best work comes in open space as a lead blocker on perimeter runs. 
28
TE Michael Trigg.pre_draft_advice:
The safest path with Trigg is to wait to consider him after the NFL Draft shows us his landing spot. 
If you can land him in the third round of a rookie draft with 1.5 PPR scoring for the position, it’s worth the risk. 
Keep in mind that if Trigg or Kenyon Sadiq were wide receivers, would they be instant impact players? Brock Bowers would have 
been. I’m not as optimistic about Trigg and Sadiq. It means it’s probably safer watch from afar and let others take first- and or 
second-round shots on these guys.

TE Kenyon Sadiq.elevator_pitch:
Sadiq lit up the NFL Combine with speed, vertical leaping, and bench press performances. He’s an explosive athlete, 
which is what NFL teams seek as potential compensatory factors for a short and light move tight end. 
When you click on the highlights at the end of Sadiq’s scouting report you’ll see displays of speed and acrobatic catches. When Sadiq 
is playing to his potential, he can change the game with his big-play skills. He’s essentially a big vertical receiver who can draw 
mismatches from H-Back alignments. 
33
TE Kenyon Sadiq.pre_draft_advice:
Pre-draft, I’d take a chance on Sadiq if I can get him in the late half of the second round in a 1.5 
PPR format. Ideally, I’d prefer the third, but that may not prove realistic due to the buzz surrounding his athletic ability. It might be 
best to avoid most tight ends until we see their post-draft landing spots and focus on receivers and backs.

TE Justin Joly.elevator_pitch:
Joly is one of my favorites in this tight end class. He’s an elite pass catcher with a route game that can become 
starter-caliber with a year of work. 
The biggest concerns with Joly are his stature, and his lack of top-end speed. Both issues place caps on his potential despite having 
technical skills that would elevate Joly’s grade further if the physique and athletic ability were present. 
38
TE Justin Joly.pre_draft_advice:
Joly is worth a late-round pick in deep PPR formats and a mid-round pick after the third round in 
1.5 PPR systems.

TE Carsen Ryan.elevator_pitch:
Carsen Ryan didn’t receive an invitation to the NFL Combine and it’s the most puzzling development for me with this 
draft class. As I made my way through the tape of this class, I repeatedly found myself saying these things out loud for all but a 
handful of prospects: 
42
TE Carsen Ryan.pre_draft_advice:
Unless you’re in a league with RSP subscribers, Ryan should be available to you in the final rounds 
of rookie drafts before the NFL conducts its proceedings. He’ll be a priority summer stash for me on teams with practice squads and 
rosters at least 25-30 deep.

TE Sam Roush.elevator_pitch:
Roush runs effective routes, but it’s in a limited range of the field. It means he’s not a matchup threat in the 
receiving game. Roush will do his best work in zone and not as a primary option. 
Roush will be valued most for his blocking. When I arrived at this conclusion after watching Roush’s tape I also thought he’d be the 
best overall tight end in this class if the RSP’s Depth of Talent Score valued blocking on equal or greater terms as receiving skills. 
46
TE Sam Roush.pre_draft_advice:
If Roush joins a team where the scheme uses multiple tight ends at a time, increase his value 
accordingly. If Roush joins a team where the scheme is predominantly three receivers, he’s a mid-round value who will be a patience 
play for fantasy GMs.

TE Joshua Cuevas.elevator_pitch:
Cuevas has the makings of a good backup tight end who can contribute off the bench as the second or third option. 
If called upon to be the top tight end in the system’s passing game, Cuevas can deliver there, too. 
Cuevas is adequate-to-good at most roles required of his position, but not excellent at anything. What’s missing is an elevated skill 
that makes him a mismatch as a blocker, route runner, pass-catcher, or ball carrier. This also includes his size and athletic ability. 
50
TE Joshua Cuevas.pre_draft_advice:
Rankings are relative; Depth of Talent Scores are not. Cuevas can contribute and he might even 
earn extended playing time in an offense, but his best chance of fantasy success will come in an offense where he’s a high-volume 
option. 
52

TE Riley Nowakowski.elevator_pitch:
A walk-on at Wisconsin who redshirted the 2020 season. He began his career as a linebacker. He converted to tight 
end in 2023, and he became a key cog in the Hoosiers’ offense. 
Nowakowski has a low center of gravity and at the ceiling of his size position. He’s an H-Back in the mold of Chris Cooley, a former 
Washington stalwart who delivered five seasons with at least 730 yards – four of them in consecutive seasons. 
54
TE Riley Nowakowski.pre_draft_advice:
Nowakowski is likely the exceptional case of a prospect who offers a high level of situational value 
for an NFL team and a strong grade for his overall skill sets, but his ability to match up against opponents as a statistical force is 
unlikely. There’s a chance Nowakowski can become a productive fantasy option like Chris Cooley so his grade is warranted, but the 
likelihood of a team embracing Nowakowski as a statistically productive offensive contributor is small.

TE Max Klare.elevator_pitch:
A converted quarterback, there’s a lack of muscle memory with areas of Klare’s new position that should improve 
with experience, but it could limit his playing time early in his career. There are also questions about his size: Will he add muscle and 
will it make him more explosive or slow him down? 
Klare makes starter-worthy plays as a pass receiver, but he has lapses that could be due to lack of experience at the position. This 
could be a muscle memory problem that improves with practice reps and time. 
58
TE Max Klare.pre_draft_advice:
Klare has some boom-bust baked into his game because of his inconsistencies with his hands, 
limited size as a blocker, and how team fit could impact his opportunities. Is he his new team’s future TE1 or is he a talented TE2 
with limited volume in an offense that doesn’t prioritize him in the receiving game? 
60

TE Jack Endries.elevator_pitch:
Endries has a lot of Zach Ertz to his game. He works the middle and the flats with enough sophistication to develop 
into a reliable starter and check-down option who can also deliver big-play receptions off-play action. 
Endries knows how to sell routes with the techniques he has learned, and he’ll likely build on it as his career unfolds. He has good 
weight drop into breaks of routes that use return motion – think whip routes and certain variations of routes that work against zone 
defenders playing tight at the top of the stem. 
62
TE Jack Endries.pre_draft_advice:
Endries is a high-floor/low-ceiling receiver for fantasy because he’s fast enough to stretch the field, 
but not explosive enough to become a high-end statistical mismatch for opposing defenses. If the NFL’s offensive trends make a 
large shift to 12 personnel sets, Endries’ blocking might need 1-2 years of work before he can see the field as a third or fourth option 
in the passing game’s pecking order. 
Endries is another late-round consideration worth stashing in 1.5 PPR formats who might be earning too much buzz as a mid-round 
value. His future will likely be that of a competent receiver but one without compelling target volume.

TE Joe Royer.elevator_pitch:
Max Klare and Will Kacmarek were replacement options for Royer, who began his career at Ohio State but couldn’t 
stay healthy and Ohio State moved on from Royer as potential fixture in their offense. Royer was healthy enough at Cincinnati to 
display a lot of the skills Ohio State coveted. 
Royer has field-flipping speed in the play-action-aided vertical game we often expect from pro-caliber tight ends. Royer finishes plays 
in the middle of the field. He’s a rugged pass catcher who attacks the ball early and his lapses with clap-attacking are minor. 
66
TE Joe Royer.pre_draft_advice:
Royer will have pockets of fans who value him higher than his likely value. They’ll like his athletic 
ability on tape. Consider him late-round pick worth stashing for the upside of him becoming a low-end fantasy starter in 1-3 years. 
This class has a lot of tight ends with their grades tightly grouped. There’s not a ton of difference between what Royer offers and 
what Jack Endries, Max Klare, or Joshua Cuevas offer. With the right fit, Royer could emerge sooner in a role we’ve seen with Tyler 
Warren and Tucker Kraft. At worst, think 4-6 catches per game with 30-50 yards and red-zone work.

TE Oscar Delp.elevator_pitch:
There’s a lot to like about Oscar Delp’s game, but I was hoping I’d love it. The reason that love falls short has to do 
with his receiving performances against contact. 
Delp can earn the ball against tight coverage. He can take a wrap to his frame while catching a target. When a defender delivers a 
substantial hit, Delp has too much difficulty hanging onto the ball. 
70
TE Oscar Delp.pre_draft_advice:
Consider Delp a worthwhile mid-to-late stash in your drafts depending on the scoring system. 
Injuries, playing behind Brock Bowers, system changes, and instability at quarterback are potential reasons why Delp didn’t emerge 
as Georgia expected when they recruited him. 
There’s a lot more refinement to Delp’s game than his lack of buzz indicates. If he can’t add muscle, it might only lead to Delp 
having a long career as a skilled backup, but the price will probably be right to find out.

TE Will Kacmarek.elevator_pitch:
If you watched him at Ohio State, Kacmarek served as the big-bodied sidekick to Max Klare. Of course, if you 
watched Klare and Kacmarek together, you see just how underwhelming Ohio State’s system makes tight ends look in the receiving 
game. It wasn’t just Kacmarek’s fit in the Buckeyes’ system. 
Both Klare and Kacmarek looked more dynamic as receiving weapons at their former programs. This is especially true with 
Kacmarek. 
74
TE Will Kacmarek.pre_draft_advice:
Kacmarek is a high-floor, low-ceiling fantasy prospect at the position because he’ll likely be tied to 
the line of scrimmage and not utilized frequently enough in the intermediate passing game. 
If the NFL’s offensive trends continue to shift toward 12-personnel, Kacmarek will be a safer option than some of the move tight 
ends who can’t block and don’t offer more compelling receiving skills than their team’s WR3-WR4. Think of A.J. Barner in this 
respect. 
This translates to valuing Kacmarek as a late-round or offseason stash for dynasty teams with expanded rosters during the summer. 
There are players ranked below him with greater ceilings as a fantasy options, but lower floors due to their limited roles in the run 
game.

TE Lake McRee.elevator_pitch:
Route-running and receiving are the assets of McRee’s game. When it comes to running routes, McRee executes the 
details he knows with sophistication and suddenness when called for. 
McRee has strong body control to adjust to the ball near the boundary and when under duress. It wouldn’t shock me if McRee’s pass-
catching skills are closer to the elite tier than I graded, but the opportunities to earn that grade weren’t present. 
McRee’s on-field acceleration is notable, but he cannot sustain that rate of speed over longer distances. There’s a substantial 
difference with McRee’s pace between his first 10-15 yards and the ground he covers after. 
77
TE Lake McRee.pre_draft_advice:
McRee is worth consideration in 1.5 PPR scoring formats for tight ends in large-roster leagues with 
practice squad spots during the summer when there are expanded rosters. Otherwise monitor from afar for 2-3 years. McRee has the 
game of a player you add/drop as needed due to injury-related opportunities.

TE Nate Boerkircher.elevator_pitch:
A former walk-on at Nebraska, Boerkircher has the build, acceleration, and route-running potential to develop into a 
better pro than collegian. There’s enough ceiling to his game that he could emerge as a starter in 2-3 years. 
This is what an NFL team may believe if they take Boerkircher earlier than the sixth round. I’m not as optimistic, but concede it’s a 
possibility. 
Right now, Boerkircher could contribute as a receiver if called upon. He has minor details to address with his route game that could 
limit his effectiveness to the shallow zones and seams. 
81
TE Nate Boerkircher.pre_draft_advice:
Worth consideration in 1.5 PPR scoring formats for tight ends in large-roster leagues with practice 
squad spots during the summer when there are expanded rosters. Otherwise monitor from afar for 2-3 years.

TE Tanner Koziol.elevator_pitch:
Koziol is an excellent pass-catcher. He had the highest rate of difficult catches I tracked among the tight ends I 
studied this year. 
At the same time, Koziol’s route running doesn’t generate as much separation as he needs to avoid difficult contested targets. 
There’s no nuance with his releases and route setups heading into breaks. 
There’s a lack of suddenness as a mover. This includes his releases, his route breaks, and as a ball carrier. 
84
TE Tanner Koziol.pre_draft_advice:
Koziol is worth consideration in 1.5 PPR scoring formats for tight ends in large-roster leagues with 
practice squad spots during the summer when there are expanded rosters. Otherwise monitor from afar for 2-3 years.

TE Matthew Hibner.elevator_pitch:
If there’s an NFL team seeking a TE2 for 12 personnel sets who can stretch defenses when the offense runs 
misdirection and play-action, Hibner offers a combination of straight-line speed, ball-tracking, and blocking that will meet an 
offense’s needs. 
Hibner’s size is likely capped, and it means Hibner’s best work as a blocker is against second- and third-level defenders. Still, he can 
help against defensive ends and there are isolated assignments against linebackers where, at worst, he won’t fold immediately and 
at best, he could hold up. Hibner should develop into a good underneath receiver if he can develop weight drop into hard breaks and 
execute breaks further away from zone defenders. 
87
TE Matthew Hibner.pre_draft_advice:
Hibner is worth consideration in 1.5 PPR scoring formats for tight ends in large-roster leagues with 
practice squad spots during the summer when there are expanded rosters. Otherwise monitor from afar for 2-3 years.

TE Eli Raridon.elevator_pitch:
Raridon is what NFL scouts call a “projection pick.” NFL organizations often elevate a player’s grade ahead of his 
portfolio of film, because they like his athletic ability and his potential for additional physical development. Projection picks can work 
out, but sometimes the teams underestimate which skills are most difficult to develop. 
Raridon has the height of an every-down tight end and could have the frame to add the 15-20 pounds that he’ll need to handle that 
role. He’s fast enough to stretch a seam and beat corners outside due to his height and length advantage. 
90
TE Eli Raridon.pre_draft_advice:
Raridon may get that Notre Dame bump with his draft capital. If so, he could earn early 
opportunities based on that draft capital that aren’t commensurate with his current skill set. 
It means you might be overpaying for Raridon in dynasty formats. That said, if you see Raridon in the TE10-TE15 range in this class, 
there’s not a huge difference in grade among them. 
Monitor Raridon from afar. He might become a starter at some point, but not one you’ll have difficulty acquiring.

TE Jaren Kanak.elevator_pitch:
A former linebacker who transitioned to tight end in 2025, Kanak is undersized for a full-time starting tight end in 
this league unless he can leverage his speed with an elite receiving game. At this point, Kanak’s separation, routes, and pass 
catching are good enough to contribute. None of them are at a level that will help him compensate for his size and emerge as a 
quality starter. 
94

TE Dallen Bentley.elevator_pitch:
Bentley has good speed up the seam, he catches the ball well, and he’s a rugged blocker for his size who excels as 
a blocker from the wing, as a fullback, a blocker in detached alignments. He’s an H-Back. 
Let him loose against outside linebackers and safeties, and Bentley is an asset. Bump him inside, and it better be for a double team 
if that assignment is a lineman. 
At this stage, Bentley has some good route-running techniques against man and zone coverages, but he’s too deliberate with their 
execution. Even when he’s settling underneath zone defenders, he can take too long and drift toward the coverage. 
97
TE Dallen Bentley.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar unless in a deep league with 1.5 PPR scoring for TEs. If so, consider him for 
expanded summer rosters so you can stash him on your practice squad if he’s impresses early.

TE RJ Maryland.elevator_pitch:
Maryland is the best pass-catching tight end in this class. The rest of his game is the problem. The real question 
with Maryland might be whether an NFL team should draft him as a wide receiver. More on this at the end of this section. 
The son of former University of Miami and Cowboys star DT, Russell Maryland, after seeing the film you would be inclined to think 
Maryland’s dad was Michael Irvin. Maryland wins the ball with ease because he has strong hand-eye coordination and fluid 
positioning to the football against tight coverage. 
100
TE RJ Maryland.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Maryland from afar unless he earns the spring/summer Oronde Gadsden II buzz for a team 
that is inclined to use him as a fourth receiver more than a second tight end. The Chargers ultimately did this with Gadsden. 
Remember that injuries were a factor. 
Based on what I shared in the Elevator Pitch, Maryland’s score would be higher if the NFL considers him a receiver, but the collective 
scores of contributing receivers are also higher. It means the position won’t make a big difference for his outlook as much as the 
team fit.

TE Marlin Klein.elevator_pitch:
Klein should make a team as a third tight end because of his athletic ability and his baseline skills as a route runner 
and receiver. Blocking is the area where Klein has the most work to do. Fortunately, it’s the area where he should make the most 
gains as a native of Germany. 
104
TE Marlin Klein.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Klein from afar. If he earns good reviews during the spring or in early August, Klein would 
be worth adding to an expanded summer roster in 1.5 PPR formats as a potential stash for your practice squad. It’s more likely that 
you’ll cut Klein but keep him on your long list of players to consider for the first 2-3 seasons of their career if news warrants.

Quan Wright.elevator_pitch:
Wright has a large catch radius with targets arriving above his waist, big hands, and he tracks the ball like a starting 
NFL receiver. When the ball is in his hands, Wright is a compelling runner who can change direction with creativity, precision, and 
suddenness. 
107
Quan Wright.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Wright from far. If he shines in camp, consider for a summer-long addition to expanded 
rosters to monitor from your roster.

TE John Michael Gyllenborg.elevator_pitch:
Gyllenborg is a good pass-catcher with skills to work open against zone coverage. He has height to support more 
muscle on his frame. If Gyllenborg can maintain or build on his explosive athletic profile, he could emerge as an NFL contributor. 
It will take more than muscle. Gyllenborg must develop into a compelling route runner against savvy zone defenders and become an 
asset against man-to-man. At this point, Gyllenborg lacks nuance with even his existing route tools to reliably bait opposing 
defenders. 
111

TE Khalil Dinkins.elevator_pitch:
Dinkins is a good athlete for a move tight end, but his route game lacks refinement. Penn State’s offense didn’t 
feature Dinkins on routes where he had to deliver route nuances with stems and setups. Much of Dinkins’ work came against zone 
coverage, and it only required him to read the coverage, find the open space, and settle or tempo his breaks across the field while 
creating a friendly target. 
Dinkins is an adequate pass-catcher as an NFL reserve if he can deliver value on special teams. For Dinkins to become a reliable 
contributor in the passing game, he must limit his clap-attacks of targets so he exploits what will be limited opportunities early in his 
career. 
114
